name: Aqueduct at Vicus Augustanus
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 43 B3 unnamed aqueduct (Vicus Augustanus)

name: Aqueduct at Vorgium
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 7 C3 unnamed aqueduct (Vorgium)

name: Barbegal
description: At Barbegal in southern France are the remains of a Roman watermill complex.

name: El Habel tunnel (Saldae aqueduct)
description: An aqueduct tunnel, near Saldae, sometimes referred to as the Nonius Datus tunnel. It was a component of a Roman aqueduct that delivered water from the area of modern Toudja to Saldae (modern Bejaia in Algeria's Kabylia province). The tunnel's modern name derives from the nearby village of El Habel, which is located just north of Meccuc in the municipal district of Bejaia.

name: Jerwan Aqueduct
description: The Assyrian king Sennacherib had an aqueduct constructed between 703 and 690 B.C. near the ancient city of Girmua. The structure is part of the "Khinnis [Canal] System." The identification of the structure's builder is confirmed by means of several inscriptions.

name: Perrotte Aqueduct tunnel, aqueduct of Nimes
description: The aqueduct tunnel known as the Tunnel de la Perrotte stretches for more than 60 meters.

name: Segovia aqueduct
description: Supplied Segovia with water from mountains to the southeast

name: Unnamed aqueduct at Angitiae Lucus
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 44 D2 unnamed aqueduct (Angitiae Lucus)

name: Unnamed Aqueduct at Cillium
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 33 C2 unnamed aqueduct (at Cillium)

name: Unnamed aqueduct at Ephesus (Aqua Iulia?)
description: The longest and most recently-constructed Roman aqueduct in the province of Asia, measuring 36.5 km from its source in the hills south of Ephesus to the metropolis itself. The aqueduct was first commissioned by the emperor Hadrian in 122-126, and it portions of it remained in use into the medieval period. In modern times, it is known as the Değirmendere Aqueduct, after the nearby Kenchrios river (modern Değirmendere).

name: Unnamed aqueduct supplying Rusicade/Thapsus
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 31 F3 unnamed aqueduct (Rusicade and spring)

name: Aqueduct at Gambreion
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 56 E3 unnamed aqueduct group (Pergamum)

name: Aqueduct W of Diocletianopolis
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 22 B6 unnamed aqueduct group (Philippopolis)

name: Aqueduct at Tarracinae
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 44 D3 unnamed aqueduct (Tarracina(e))

name: Aqueduct near Šinuhtu
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 66 F1 unnamed aqueduct (Tyana)

name: Aqueduct at Sybaris
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 46 D2 unnamed aqueduct (Thurii/Copia)

name: Aqueduct W of Zliten
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 35 G2 unnamed aqueduct (Lepcis)

name: Aqueduct at Kepos
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 52 D2 unnamed aqueduct (“Aqueduct of Theodosius”, NNW Constantinopolis)

name: Aqueduct at Pineda de Mar
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 25 H4 unnamed aqueduct (Pineda de Mar, SW Blanda(e))

name: Ėl'khotovskiĭ nekropolʹ
description: Burial site in North Ossetia–Alania containing 58 burials associated with the Koban culture (8th-7th centuries BCE), as well as 26 medieval burials (14th century CE).

name: Tigris Tunnel Inscriptions
description: The so-called Tigris Tunnel is a cave ca. fifty miles north of Diyarbakır, Turkey and it forms a source of the Tigris River. In its vicinity there are several Assyrian monuments, including five inscriptions.

name: ‘Ammān al-Madanī civil airport
description: Bronze age building located within the civil airport of Amman.

name: ‘Nido’
description: ‘Nido’ or Nidum was the site of a Flavian Auxiliary fort and milestone; now Neath, Glamorgan, Wales.

name: ‘Praesidio’
description: Possibly originally named Praesidium, this small Roman fort located in modern Jordan is known today as Khirbet al-Khalde.

name: (H)Ebudes (islands)
description: An archipelago off the west coast of Scotland, whence the modern name Hebrides. Pliny numbers 30 islands among the Hebudes, whereas Ptolemy counts five among the Eboudai. Ptolemy's coordinates most closely match with the modern islands of Islay, Jura, Rathlin, Mull, and the Kintyre peninsula, but comprehensive and conclusive names and identifications in antiquity remain elusive and controversial.

name: (Nea) Paphos
description: The legendary birthplace of the goddess Aphrodite, (Nea) Paphos has been a center of activity since the Final Bronze Age when Mycenaeans honored the goddess there. The site was listed on the UNESCO World Heritage List in 1980.

name: [Bushahr]/Antiocheia in Persis
description: A city in Persis founded or re-founded by Antiochos I Soter, perhaps located at Bushahr, Rishahr, or Tawwaj.

name: [Dur-Katlimmu]/Magdalu
description: [Dur-Katlimmu]/Magdalu (modern Tell Schech Hamad in Syria) is a multi-period human settlement where occupation began in the Late Chalcolithic period and continued/reoccurred into the Roman period. The Assyrian city of Dur-Katlimmu (tenth to seventh centuries BCE) was perhaps founded by Shalmaneser I.

name: [Girsu]/Tello(h)
description: A Sumerian city-state.

name: [Nuzi]
description: Nuzi was an ancient city of Mesopotamia located southwest of Arrapha.

name: [Samarra]/‘Sumere’
description: Site of a major Islamic capital that controlled the provinces of the Abbasid Empire extending from Tunisia to Central Asia for a century. Inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage site in 2007.

name: *Azetium
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 45 F2 *Azetium

name: *Bocchori
description: A pre-Roman and Roman settlement on the island of Majorca. Its ruins are known as Pedret de Bóquer, which is located at the modern Port de Pollença. The original settlement dates ca. 1400 BC.

name: *Brigobannis
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 19 A2 *Brigobannis

name: *Letocetum
description: *Letocetum was an important military post near the junction of Watling Street and Icknield Street.

name: *Silina Inss.
description: *Silina Inss. (Isles of Scilly) form an archipelago off the southwestern tip of the Cornish peninsula of Great Britain.

name: Abai
description: Abai was an ancient town in Phocis, said to have been founded by the Argive Abas. It has been identified with a hilltop, fortified archaeological site known as Palaiokhori, near modern Exarchos. The site of an oracular sanctuary of Apollo and Artemis associated with Abai in ancient literature has been identified at nearby Kallipodi.

name: Abbatija tad-Dejr
description: A multi-period burial site located in the northwest of modern Ir-Rabat, Malta. Archaeological evidence points to first use of the site in the Punic and Roman periods. A quarry of possible Roman date was used in the construction of Byzantine-period hypogea. Further modifications were made in the late Medieval (post-Muslim) period, prior to abandonment of the site.

name: Abric Pizarro
description: Abric Pizarro is a prehistoric rock shelter located in the Spanish Pyrenees near the modern geopolitical border between Spain and France. Archaeological evidence shows that Neanderthals occupied the site for some 40,000 years, notably during the MIS 4 period, which lasted from around 71,000 to 57,000 years ago.

name: Abrittus
description: Abrittus was the site of a Roman fortress and settlement, located near modern Razgrad, Bulgaria.

name: Abrotonum/Sabratha
description: The westernmost of the "three cities" of Tripolis, Abrotonum/Sabratha was founded ca. 500 B.C. by the Phoenicians. The city was part of the Numidian Kingdom of Massinissa before becoming a Roman city.

name: Abū Dhuba‘
description: Minor site from which inscribed bricks have been found.

name: Abū Jāmūs
description: Ruin of unknown type where cuneiform tablets are said to have been discovered in modern Iraq.

name: Abū Ṭbīrah
description: Medium-sized Sumerian city close to modern Al-Nasiriyya.

name: Abydos
description: A city of Mysia located on the Hellespont.

name: Academy/Akademeia/Akademia
description: An archaeological site, location of Plato's philosophical school

name: Acemhöyük
description: The settlement mound of Acemhöyük is perhaps the location of the ancient Hittite center of Purushanda.

name: Achaion Limen
description: An ancient settlement in Aeolis, located between Myrina and Gryneion. According to Strabo, there was an altar of the Twelve Gods there.

name: Acharna
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 60 D2 Acharna

name: Achilleion
description: An ancient Greek city in the southwest of the Troas. A site that in antiquity was considered to be the tomb of Achilles is nearby at Beşiktepe.

name: Acholla
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 33 H2 Acholla

name: Acropolis of Athens
description: The acropolis of the ancient city of Athens in Greece.

name: Acropolis of Eretria
description: The Acropolis of Eretria became the site of the settlement during the Middle Helladic period.

name: Actium Victory Monument
description: A naval victory monument was built by Octavian at Nicopolis (Epirus) to celebrate the Battle of Actium.

name: Ad Flexum
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 44 E3 Ad Flexum

name: Ad Nonum
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 43 C2 Ad Nonum

name: Ad Silanum
description: An ancient road station labeled on the Peutinger Map and usually equated with the archaeological remains of a mansio at modern Puech-Crémat-Bas in France (BAtlas 14 I4 Ad Silanum)

name: Ada / Adalar
description: Ada / Adalar is a village in Southeast Anatolia. It is located a few kilometres southwest of Malazgirt in the Turkish province Muş. Nearby is a rock inscription of the Urartian King Minua (ca. 810-785/80 BCE), in which he reports the construction of a Canal named "Canal of Minua", which is still in use today. In Late Antiquity, the canal was attributed to the legendary Queen Semiramis by the Armenian historian Movses Khorenatsi (ca. 410–490s).

name: Adab
description: Bismaya, ancient Adab, is a major archaeological mound in southern Iraq. The site is located in the present-day Wāsiṭ Province about 65 kilometres south of al-Kūt. Excavations by E.J. Banks from 1903-05 uncovered extensive remains of the ancient city of Adab, predominantly dating to the 3rd and early 2nd millennium BCE. In its day, Adab was renowned, especially for its temple, the Emaẖ, dedicated to the goddess Ninẖursag.

name: Adamkayalar
description: A site of rock-cut relief sculpture, perhaps dating to the second century.

name: Adams 1069 (find site)
description: An archaeological site designated by Robert McC. Adams as 1069 that consisted of a settlement mound that was the find spot for a cuneiform inscription. The site’s ancient name is unknown.

name: Adilcevaz
description: Medieval castle of the town Adilcevaz on the shore of Lake Van where Urartian inscriptions have been found. Not to be confused with Kef Kalesi which is located further north and outside of Adilcevaz.

name: Adramyttium
description: An ancient city of Aeolis, located on the modern Karataş peninsula at Ören in Turkey's Balıkesir province.

name: Aegae
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 56 E4 Aegae

name: Aegates (islands)
description: The Egadi Islands, a small group of mountainous islands northwest of Sicily.

name: Aegimoeroe (islands)
description: An island group located off Cape Bon, Tunisia. Pliny the Elder knew of the "duae Aegimoeroe", presumably referring to Ile Zembra and Ile Zembretta.

name: Aeoliae (islands)
description: A volcanic archipelago in the Tyrrhenian Sea composed of eight islands.

name: Agdistis Sanctuary (Yazılıkaya)
description: A plateau around a monumental Phrygian rock‐cut altar at the extensive fortified settlement and sanctuary complex of Yazılıkaya in Turkey. Associated with Agdistis in the 20th century because of Roman epigraphic finds there, more recent excavations have demonstrated that the sanctuary was in use from the 8th–7th century BCE to the Roman Period (1st-2nd century CE). Continuity of ritual practice in front of the rock-cut altar is demonstrated by rock-cut bows, a ritual preparation area with hearths, a rock-cut statue identifiable as the goddess Matar, and the Roman-era votive inscriptions.

name: Ägelmoos
description: Ägelmoos is an archaeological site located in Beinwil am See in the Swiss canton of Aargau. It is a now-submerged site that was once a lakeside settlement and was occupied between ca. 4500 BCE and 850 BCE.

name: Agia Aikaterini square excavations
description: A multi-period archaeological site on Kastelli Hill, Khania, Crete, that has occupation stretching more than five millennia. Archaeological fieldwork was undertaken by the Greek–Swedish Excavations and the Danish Institute at Athens beginning in 2005.

name: Agios Georgios Pegeia
description: An ancient settlement at the church of Ag. Georgios on Cape Drepanon on the west coast of Cyprus.

name: Agora of Onchestos
description: Approximate center point of agora of Onchestos sanctuary visible in satellite imagery.

name: Agora of the Italians
description: A Hellenistic and Roman complex on the island of Delos, the so-called "Agora of the Italians" was likely built by Roman merchants. Its precise function is debated.

name: Aguntum
description: Aguntum was an ancient settlement of Noricum that received municipal status under Claudius.

name: Aianteion
description: Aianteion refers to a place on a promontory near İn Tepe where classical sources believed Telamonian Ajax was buried.

name: Aigaionos Monu.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 52 C4 Aigaionos Monu.

name: Aigiai
description: An ancient settlement in Lakonia, mentioned by Strabo and Pausanias. Since the early 20th century, scholars have connected it with Classical and Roman remains at a site consequently dubbed "Palaeochora" between modern Aigies/Aigiai (formerly Chania Koutoumous) and Limni.

name: Ain el-Sabil
description: Roman/late antique settlement, with visible remains of streets and mud-brick structures, including a church, two pigeon towers, and possibly residential units. Largely unexcavated.

name: Aïn Melliti
description: Aïn Melliti is the site of a Byzantine fort and church.

name: Ais Gorkis
description: Settlement in western Cyprus inhabited during the Neolithic period.

name: Akalissos
description: An ancient settlement of Lycia, located near the modern village of Karacaören in Turkey's Kumluca municipality (Antalya province).

name: Akanthos
description: An ancient settlement in the northeast of the Chalkidiki. The large site is immediately southeast of the modern town of Ierissos.

name: Akarçay Tepe Höyük
description: Akarçay Tepe Höyük, a settlement of the eighth and seventh millennia BCE located to the east of the Euphrates River in the immediate vicinity of the Syrian border near the Turkish village of Akarçay in the Birecik district of the province of Şanlıurfa. The site lies on the south bank of the Su Deresi stream, which flows west into the Euphrates River.

name: Akdamar Island
description: The second largest of the four islands in Lake Van, in eastern Turkey.

name: Akonesiai (islands)
description: An island group of the Cilician coast located between Pityoussa and Anemurion.

name: Akra
description: An ancient Greek city on the Kerch Strait whose remains are now largely submerged. The surveyed and excavated portions of the city, including fortification walls and other structures, lie on and offshore from an unnamed cape, south of the modern village of Naberezhnoye/Naberezhne, that separates the open waters of the strait from the Yanysh-Ghol Salt Lake.

name: Akra
description: A significant ancient settlement located in the modern Bannu district of Pakistan's Khyber Pakhtunkhwa province. The site, comprising several settlement mounds, is divided by a stream known as the Lohra nullah.

name: Al-Ghab
description: The al-Ghab (also el-Ghab) plain is located in northwest Syria, in the Al-Suqaylabiyah District.

name: al-Hajjar
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 95 C3 al-Hajjar

name: al-Jumayil
description: An archaeological site of Byzantine and possibly earlier date located at the modern town of al-Jumayil in Jordan. It may be identifiable as the biblical site of Beth Gamul.

name: Al-Khazneh
description: An elaborate, rock-cut monument at Petra built ca. 100 BC to AD 200 known as "Al-Khazneh" or "The Treasury". The Nabatean structure, originally used as a tomb, dates to the reign of Aretas IV Philopatris.

name: Al-Khiḍr
description: Dilmun Bronze age settlement

name: Al-Maqsha
description: Village in Bahrain, find place for cuneiform inscribed stele.

name: Al-Mukhaṭṭaṭ
description: Site with large mud brick platform, supposedly find place for Old Babylonian Dilbat cuneiform tablets.

name: Al-Rajībah
description: The findspot for a cuneiform inscription, possibly ancient KI.KAL.

name: Al-Rammāḥīyah
description: Find place for cuneiform brick inscription.

name: Al-Sabaa wa Arbain
description: Al-Sabaa wa Arbain (Saba‘ wa Arba’īn) is a small modern town in northeastern Syria (al-Hasakah Governorate). The settlement sits atop a small mound of ruins (ancient name not known).

name: Al-Sayāl
description: Find place for cuneiform inscription.

name: Al(al)komenai
description: At Al(al)komenai (near Palaiokastro, Ithaca, Ionian Islands) are the remains of a multi-period settlement, in use from at least the Archaic period to Roman times. There is also a tract of Cyclopean walling associated with the site.

name: Alaca Höyük
description: A Neolithic and Hittite settlement located to the northeast of Boğazkale.

name: Alalaḫ
description: A major Late Bronze Age city in the Amuq River Valley, inhabited during the Second Millennium BCE until about the 12th century BCE.

name: Alalaiou (islands)
description: Alalaiou Inss., the modern Dahlak Archipelago in the Red Sea, which today comprises 126 islands.

name: Alalie/Aleria
description: A city of Corsica with significant pre-historic, Greek, Etruscan, and Roman phases.

name: Alatzomouri Pefka
description: A large-scale Minoan dyemaking and dying establishment overlooking the modern Gulf of Mirabello at Pachia Ammos in Lasithi, Crete.

name: Alatzomouri Rock Shelter
description: Α small Μinoan rock shelter of uncertain function discovered alongside a highway on the modern Alatzomouri hill just west of Pacheia Ammos in Lisotho, east Crete. A significant assemblage of broken pottery and tools, all dating to EMIII, were found in the shelter.

name: Alb(i)um Intimilium/Albintimilium
description: Alb(i)um Intimilium/Albintimilium (modern Ventimiglia) was the main settlement of the Ligurian tribe of the Intemelii. In the 2nd century BC, it came under the control of the Roman Republic. Albintimilium became a municipium in 89 BC in the tribus Falerna. In the same period, the city walls were built, with several towers. The city flourished in the early Imperial period, with baths, an aqueduct, and a theater. In the 6th and early 7th century, it was a Byzantine kastron, coming under the control of the Longobards in 641. During the 7th and 8th centuries, the Roman city was slowly abandoned in favour of a smaller hilltop settlement focused around the Cathedral.

name: Alba Docilia
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 39 D5 Alba Docilia

name: Ale's Stones
description: A megalithic monument in Sweden in the form of a ship, likely dating from ca. AD 600, the end of the Nordic Iron Age.

name: Alexandria Ariorum/Artacavan/Harey
description: Alexandria Ariorum/Artacavan/Harey (modern Herat, Afghanistan).

name: Alexandria/Alexandropolis/Cufis
description: Now Kandahar in Afghanistan, Alexandria/Alexandropolis/Cufis is an ancient site founded by Alexander the Great ca. 330 BC. The indigenous people of the Hellenistic period were likely the Pakthas.

name: Alfar Romano de Cartuja
description: The Alfar Romano de Cartuja (Roman potters' workshop of Cartuja) was a pottery production center for southern Spain that was active from the 1st century till the later 2nd century CE.

name: Alhambra
description: Alhambra, Generalife, and Albayzín together form a UNESCO World Heritage Site that preserves elements of the medieval heritage of Granada, Spain. The Alhambra is a fortified palace complex that was begun by Muhammad I Ibn al-Ahmar, the first Nasrid emir and founder of the Emirate of Granada, in 1238.

name: Alignements du Manio
description: An alignment of menhirs that form a rectangular area.

name: Aliler Fortress
description: Aliler Castle is the modern Turkish name of an Urartian fortress situated near the village of Karaağaç, approx. 11 km southeast of Lake Van. Here, the remains of a column base with a fragmentary preserved Urartian inscription of the Urartian king Minua (9th / 8th century BCE) have been found.

name: Alişar Höyük
description: A settlement with occupation from the Chalcolithic period (fourth millennium B.C.) until the Phrygian period (first millennium B.C.).

name: Allianoi
description: An ancient spa, known in modern times as Paşa Ilıca in Turkey.

name: Almyras
description: A copper smelting site located near the modern town of Agia Varvara in Cyprus. The site contains remains dated to the Iron Age.

name: Altar of Androgeos
description: The Altar of Androgeos, son of Minos, at Phaleron is described by Pausanias.

name: Althiburos
description: Althiburos was a city of Africa Proconsularis that became a municipium under Hadrian and a colonia during the third century A.D.

name: Altıntepe
description: A Urartian settlement on a hill overlooking the Euphrates River

name: Aludiri
description: Urartian fortress of Aznavurtepe.

name: Alyke
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 51 D3 Alyke

name: Amantia
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 49 B3 Amantia

name: Amarna
description: A new capital for Egypt founded by Akhenaten during his reign as pharaoh during Egypt's Eighteenth Dynasty.

name: Amarynthos
description: An ancient coastal settlement on the island of Euboea, located at modern Palaiokhoria/Kato Vathia (just east of the modern Amarynthos).

name: Amberd
description: A 10th-century fortress located on Mount Aragats in modern Aragatsotn province, Armenia.

name: Ammaedara
description: An extensive ancient settlement and road station at modern Haidra in Tunisia with evidence for occupation from the Roman, Vandal, and Byzantine eras. The site seems to have been abandoned by the 10th century CE.

name: Ammaia
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 26 C2 Ammaia

name: Ammodios/Amouda
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 89 C3 Ammodios/Amouda

name: Amnya
description: The archaeological sites at Amnya, located in a bend of the homonymous river in Western Siberia, have been demonstrated to be perhaps among the earliest instances of fortified human settlement ca. 6000 BCE.

name: Amphitheater house (Mérida)
description: The so-called Amphitheater house at Mérida is a second-century CE funerary complex connected with one C. Iulius Sucesianus.

name: Amplero
description: The toponym Amplero refers to an archaeological area located to the northeast of Collelongo (L'Aquila) in Italy. The Institute of Archaeology at the University of Pisa conducted fieldwork at the site in 1969-1971.

name: Andelos
description: A Roman settlement in Spain

name: Andros (settlement)
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 60 A4 Andros

name: Anemurium
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 66 B4 Anemurium

name: Angeia
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 55 C2 Angeia

name: Anisa
description: Anisa was an ancient settlement of Cappadocia that was inhabited in Hellenistic, Roman, and Byzantine times.

name: Antandros
description: Antandros was a city on the coast of the Troad, near the head of the Gulf of Adramyttium.

name: Antiochia/Col. Caesarea
description: An ancient city in Pisidia.

name: Antiphrai/Leukaspis
description: The archaeological site of Marina el-Alamein.

name: Anubieion at Saqqarah
description: Late-period district of Saqqara, adjacent to the Teti Pyramid.

name: Apa Phoibammon (monastery)
description: The seventh-century monastery of St. Phoibammon was built on the upper terrace of the temple of Hatshepsut at Deir el-Bahri-

name: Apani
description: The site of a Middle Bronze Age village located on the Scogli di Apani archipelago near Brindisi, Italy.

name: Aperlae
description: Aperlae was a coastal settlement of Lycia. Its ancient economy is linked to the production of Tyrian purple.

name: Apidima Cave
description: Apidima Cave is located on the western shore of the Mani Peninsula in southern Greece. Archaeological excavation in the cave system has produced remains of both Neandertals and Homo sapiens dating to the Paleolithic. Cranial evidence from the cave suggests the presence of the earliest Homo sapiens known outside of Africa.

name: Apollonia
description: Ancient Greek trade colony which developed into an independent polis, and later a Roman city, in southern Illyria

name: Apollonopolis Magna
description: The city of Apollonopolis Magna was located on the west bank of the Nile, south of Thebes. As the seat of Horus, Greeks regarded it as a site connected with Apollo. It served as the capital of the second nome of Upper Egypt.

name: Apotheke
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 56 C3 Apotheke

name: Apsyrtides (islands)
description: The Apsyrtides Islands, a group of Liburnian islands located east of Istria.

name: Aq Chapar 1
description: At Aq Chapur 1 there is evidence of a monumental building surrounded by two fortified enclosures. At Aq Chapur 2 was a large monument similar to that of Aq Chapar 1. This site is surrounded by a defensive wall with reinforcing towers.

name: Aquae Flavianae
description: Aquae Flavianae, a place in Numidia (modern Fontaine-Chaude near Khenchela).

name: Aquae S(...)
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 20 F6 Aquae S(...)

name: Aquae Segetae
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 17 C2 Aquae Segetae

name: Aquae Sinuessanae
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 44 E3 Aquae Sinuessanae

name: Ar(ai)ai Inss.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 61 E3 Ar(ai)ai Inss.

name: Araba
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 82 D5 Araba

name: Arae incendii Neroniani
description: A series of altars commemorated the "Great Fire of 64" that occurred during the reign of the emperor Nero. These so-called "Arae incendii Neroniani" are assigned to the reign of Domitian. The original number of altars is unknown and debated. Epigraphy aids greatly in a reconstruction of this distributed monument, as does the in situ altar on the Quirinal Hill. The latter was originally situated opposite the temple of Quirinus and now lies beneath the Ministero della Casa Reale, near the modern church of S. Andrea.

name: Aras
description: Site for Urartian rock inscription (also known as "Delibaba inscription").

name: Arbeia
description: A Roman fort at present-day South Shields.

name: Arbor Low stone circle
description: An important stone circle of the East Midlands, dating to the Neolithic period.

name: Arch at F Street and 10th Street
description: Monumentalized passage arch marking the intersection of F Street and the south side of 10th Street in Dura-Europos.

name: Arch of Alexander Severus
description: The Arch of Alexander Severus at Thugga was built in 228.

name: Arch of Augustus (Fano)
description: A city gate in the form of a triumphal arch with three vaults. The gate marks the entry of the via Flaminia into the city.

name: Arch of Augustus (Pompeii)
description: The brick core of an arch monument stands immediately to the west of the Temple of Jupiter in the Forum of Pompeii. It has had various appellations including "Arch of Drusus", referring to Drusus Iulius Caesar the son of emperor Tiberius.

name: Arch of Augustus (Susa)
description: An arch dedicated to Augustus at Susa in the late first century BC that records the renewed alliance between the princeps and Marcus Julius Cottius.

name: Arch of Augustus at Aosta
description: The Arch of Augustus at Aosta is a commemorative arch dedicated to Augustus ca. 25 BC following victories over the Salassi.

name: Arch of Augustus at Ariminum
description: The Arch of Augustus at Ariminum was dedicated in 27 B.C. and marked the end of the Via Flaminia.

name: Arch of Caligula (Pompeii)
description: The so-called Arch of Caligula has been referred to by various names and is unlikely to be connected with the emperor Caius. It is located near the intersection of the Via di Mercurio with Via delle Terme and Via della Fortuna.

name: Arch of Caracalla
description: The Arch of Caracalla at Volubilis was constructed in 217 CE by the governor Marcus Aurelius Sebastenus in honor of the emperor Caracalla and his mother Domna Julia.

name: Arch of Caracalla
description: The so-called Arch of Caracalla at Theveste is a tetrapylon arch dating to the third century. Originally dedicated to the deified Septimius Severus with Julia Domna and Caracalla, the arch was repurposed and modified to function as a gate in the Byzantine city wall.

name: Arch of Caracalla, Djemila
description: A Roman triumphal arch monument dedicated to Caracalla, Julia Domna, and the deified Septimius Severus located at ancient Cuicul / Curculum (modern Djémila).

name: Arch of Constantine
description: The Arch of Constantine at Rome, a triumphal arch dedicated in A.D. 315.

name: Arch of Diocletian
description: The Arch of Diocletian dates to the late third century.

name: Arch of Galerius
description: The extant remains of a triumphal arch, constructed from 298 to 299 CE and dedicated in 303 CE under the tetrarch Galerius. It commemorates the capture of Ctesiphon from the Sassanids and forms part of a monumental entrance to the palace of Galerius.

name: Arch of Gallienus
description: The Arch of Gallienus marked the Porta Esquilina, a gate in the Servian Wall of Rome. The via Labicana and via Tiburtina issue from Rome at this point. The arch was erected in A.D. 262.

name: Arch of Germanicus
description: A triumphal arch built by C. Julius Rufus, and dedicated to Tiberius and his adoptive sons, Drusus Caesar and Germanicus.

name: Arch of Hadrian at Athens
description: The Arch of Hadrian is a monument in the form of a Roman triumphal arch, constructed by the citizens of Athens in AD 131 or 132 to honor the Roman emperor Hadrian.

name: Arch of Lucius Neratius Priscus
description: An arch dedicated to Lucius Neratius Priscus, suffect consul of A.D. 97 and governor of Germania Inferior.

name: Arch of Malborghetto
description: A fourth-century A.D. tetrapylon arch stands at the intersection of the Via Flaminia and a road linking the Via Tiberina and Veii.

name: Arch of Marcus Antonius
description: The so-called "Arch of Marcus Antonius" takes its name from a local tradition that the citizens of Aquinum built the monument in a single day to honor Antonius. In all likelihood, it is a monument connected either to a deductio of veterans sent to Aquinum in 41 BCE or to the work of Caius Calvisius Sabinus who reorganized the via Latina in 27 BCE.

name: Arch of Marcus Aurelius at Tripoli
description: A quadrifons arch at ancient Oea (modern Tripoli) that commemorates Roman victories over the Parthians in A.D. 161.

name: Arch of Septimius Severus
description: The Arch of Septimius Severus is situated at the northwest corner of the Forum Romanum. It was dedicated in A.D. 203 and serves to commemorate the Parthian victories of Emperor Septimius Severus and his two sons against the Parthians (194/195 and 197–199).

name: Arch of Septimius Severus
description: The Arch of Septimius Severus dates to 205.

name: Arch of Septimius Severus at Lepcis Magna
description: The Arch of Septimius Severus at Lepcis Magna dates ca. A.D. 203.

name: Arch of the Sergii
description: A Roman triumphal arch at Pula commemorating Lucius Sergius Lepidus and the family of the Sergii for service at the Battle of Actium in 31 BC.

name: Arch of Titus
description: A triumphal arch erected in summa Sacra Via and dedicated to the deified emperor Titus by his brother Domitian, ca. 81 CE.

name: Arch of Trajan
description: An arch dedicated to Trajan on the Via Flaminia at Carsulae, also known as the Arco di San Damiano.

name: Arch of Trajan
description: The Arch of Trajan at Maktar.

name: Arch of Trajan at Ancona
description: An arch erected to honor Trajan between A.D. 100 and 116.

name: Arch of Trajan at Benevento
description: A triumphal arch built in honor of Trajan at Beneventum; it marks the Trajanic extension of the Via Appia.

name: Arch of Trajan at Canosa
description: The Arch of Trajan at Canosa dates to the second century CE.

name: Archaeological Site at Plasi of Marathon
description: The excavations at Plasi, Marathon, have revealed evidence across a broad chronological spectrum, including evidence of an archaic sanctuary.

name: Archaeological Sites of Bat, Al-Khutm and Al-Ayn
description: Located in modern Oman, the protohistoric site of Bat and its neighbouring sites forms the most complete collection of settlements and necropoleis from the third millennium BC in the world.

name: Archaic compound (Gabii)
description: An archaic compound with evidence for ritual dining that has been identified by its excavators as a "regia".

name: Arco Augusteo di Siracusa
description: The remains of a Roman arch at Syracuse dated soon after the deduction of the Roman colony of 21 BCE.

name: Arco de los Blanco
description: A Roman gateway.

name: Arco dei Gavi
description: A Roman arch built by the gens Gavia.

name: Arco del Sacramento
description: A Roman arch of the second century A.D. that faced on to the forum of Beneventum.

name: Arco di San Lazzaro
description: The so-called Arch of Saint Lazarus is part of a ruined arcuated structure located in Rome along Via Marmorata. Since this is the general vicinity of the ancient Emporium, these remains may belong to an ancient horreum. Medieval folklore associated the building with Horatius Cocles.

name: Arco di Trevi
description: An opus quadratum monument with an arched gateway perhaps marking a boundary between the territory of the Equi and the Hernicans.

name: Arcus Argentariorum
description: A commemorative arch located in the Velabrum dedicated to the Severan emperors in AD 204 by the local association of money-changers (argentarii) and merchants (negotiantes). It was later incorporated into the church of S. Giorgio al Velabro.

name: Arcus Dolabellae et Silani
description: Situated on the Caelian Hill, the Arcus Dolabellae et Silani sits at the northern corner of the Castra Peregrina. The gate dates to A.D. 10 and is likely a rebuilding of an earlier gate that was part of the so-called Servian Wall, perhaps the Porta Querquetulana (or Querquetularia) or the Porta Caelimontana.

name: Arcus Novus
description: A now-lost arch on the ancient Via Lata. It was dedicated to Diocletian either in 293 CE (marking the occasion of his decennalia) or in 303-304 (joint triumph with Maximian). The present church of Santa Maria in Via Lata occupies the site of the arch.

name: Arcus Tiberii
description: A single-bay triumphal arch dedicated to Tiberius in 16 CE. This arch commemorated the recovery of Varus' standards lost in 9 CE. It stood on the Sacra Via at the northwest corner of the Basilica Iulia.

name: Area archeologica di Casarinaccio
description: During archaeological excavations carried out between 1926 and 1934 in Casarinaccio (Ardea), a monumental complex was identified. This consisted of a late republican basilica (first century BCE) and the remains of an archaic temple dedicated to Venus.

name: Area della Guastuglia
description: The archaeological site "Area della Guastuglia" at Gubbio is an architectural complex likely connected with civic religion during Gubbio's Roman phase.

name: Area di Monte Giulio e Capitaneria
description: The low hill known as Monte Giulio is anthropic, formed by the accumulation of sediment atop an ancient quay. Archaeological investigation beginning in 1968 revealed numerous architectural remains, including a cistern and a bath complex along the via Guidoni. The ruins of the so-called Capitaneria, or Harbor office, are located near the modern stele to the Fallen of Kindu. This complex seems to have been connected to the management of port traffic and was in use from the reign of Claudius until the fourth century.

name: Area Scheibler
description: An extramural, suburban area of Pisa that has been the focus of both rescue excavation and geophysical prospective. This fieldwork revealed a long arc of occupation activity stretching from the Iron Age to the Middle Ages.

name: Argiai (islands)
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 61 D3 Argiai Inss.

name: Arginous(s)ai Inss.
description: Arginous(s)ai Inss., a group of three islands close to the coast of Aeolis.

name: Argos
description: Argos was a city of ancient Greece that reached its cultural highpoint under Pheidon in the seventh century BC.

name: Argos Amphilochikon
description: An ancient settlement in northwest Greece, mentioned in Thucydides and other ancient sources. Most scholars have long identified it with archaeological remains that lie between the modern towns of Loutro-Krikellos and Ampelaki near the Gulf of Ambrakia (but see SAGT after which BAtlas).

name: Arinçkus
description: The findspot for an Urartian stele.

name: Armaouira
description: Armaouira (Urartian Argishtikhinili) was a fortified center of ancient Urartu. The site is located close to Armavir, Armenia.

name: Arminna East
description: Archaeological site on the Nile River.

name: Arpaia milestone
description: A Roman milestone of the Via Appia located in Arpaia, Italy.

name: Arpinum
description: An ancient center that originated as early as the seventh century B.C., which became a civitas sine suffragio in 305 B.C. Arpinum was the birthplace of Caius Marius and Marcus Tullius Cicero.

name: Arrapha/Karkha de Beth Seluk
description: The name of the ancient city of Arrapha is first attested in the time of the Neo-Sumerian empire. It later became part of the Akkadian empire. Its location is thought to be that of modern Kirkuk in northeastern Iraq.

name: Arras-sur-Rhône Roman Milestone
description: A Roman milestone located in the modern French commune of Arras-sur-Rhône, registered as a historical monument in 1926 when it was to be found in the cemetery of the church of Saint Clair.

name: Arslan Kaya monument
description: The rock-cut "Arslan Kaya monument" located just west of Lake Emre in Turkey is in the Phrygian Highlands. The monument carries an inscription in Old Phrygian to the goddess Materan.

name: Arslantepe (Melid)
description: Arslantepe became an important center in the Late Chalcolithic period. With the decline of the Hittite empire, the site became the center of an independent Luwian Neo-Hittite state (Kammanu). The state became a tributary of the Assyrian empire under Tiglath-Pileser I (1115-1077 BC) and it was sacked by Sargon II in 712 BC.

name: Artemis Thermia, T.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 56 C3 Artemis Thermia, T.

name: Aruch
description: Findspot for Urartian inscription.

name: Arumâ
description: A town mentioned in Neo-Assyrian sources that has been identified as modern Ḥirbe Ruma (Khirbet ar-Rumah) and Biblical Rumah.

name: Arx of Cosa
description: The citadel of the ancient city and location of its Capitolium.

name: Arzâ
description: A town mentioned in Neo-Assyrian sources; the modern site is T. Ǧāmā.

name: Aşağı Anzaf
description: A fortress located close to the Urartian capital Tushpa.

name: Aşağı Yarımca
description: Mound close to Harran where an inscribed stele has been found.

name: Asea
description: Asea was a town of Arcadia, located in the district Maenalia. Lying near the frontier of Laconia, Asea was to be found along the road from Megalopolis to Pallantium and Tegea. Asea participated in the foundation of Megalopolis and many of its inhabitants relocated there.

name: Ashdod/Azotos (Mesogeios)
description: An ancient settlement of the Philistines.

name: Asklepieion at Zea
description: A sanctuary of Asklepios above Zea harbour in Piraeus, founded in the late 5th century BC and active through the Imperial period. Sections of the sanctuary were explored archaeologically in the 1880s and 2000s.

name: Ašnakkum
description: Town in the Khabur River basin in Northern Syria, now known as Chagar Bazar. First settled in the Neolithic Period, the town appears to have been abandoned during the Early Bronze Age. It was resettled in the Early Dynastic period, becoming an administrative center of the Old Assyrian Empire.

name: Asomata
description: Burial site located southeast of Mount Vermion. The site was primarily used during the Archaic period (mid 6th-early 5th centuries BCE), with additional burials through the late Classical-early Hellenistic period and some additional signs of earlier human activity from the Middle Neolithic period through the Bronze Age. Rescue excavations were conducted on the site in 2000-2002 during the construction of the Egnatia Highway.

name: Aspendos/Primoupolis
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 65 F4 Aspendos/Primoupolis

name: Assos/Apollonia
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 56 C3 Assos/Apollonia

name: Assyrian rock relief at Yağmur
description: Inscribed relief, dated to the reign of Tiglath-Pileser I, on a large block near the fortified site of Yağmur in the Tur 'Abdin.

name: Atarneus
description: An ancient Greek city in Aeolis that lies on the mainland opposite the island of Lesbos, northeast of the town of Dikili in modern-day Turkey.

name: Ateste
description: Ateste was a city of northern Italy, located in the interior of Venetia.

name: Athenaeum of Hadrian (Rome)
description: The Athenaeum of Hadrian was located in regio VII near the Capitoline Hill in Rome. Founded by the Emperor Hadrian, it was designated for the promotion of literary and scientific studies and named after the city of Athens. Excavations connected to the Metro C line revealed a complex in the vicinity of the modern Piazza Venezia that has been identified as the site of the Athenaeum.

name: Athis/Neocaesarea
description: A first- through tenth-century CE settlement located on the right bank of the Euphrates River in Syria's Aleppo Governorate, the site of Dibsi Faraj was excavated in the 1970s ahead of the Tabqa Dam project which eventually inundated the area. The excavations were jointly organized by Dumbarton Oaks and the University of Michigan under Richard Harper, following initial work by the Syrian culture ministry. The site was shown to be a heavily fortified one with Roman and Byzantine phases. It came to be abandoned after ca. 859 CE.

name: Atrium Vestae
description: The Atrium Vestae or House of the Vestals refers to an architectural complex on the northwest slope of the Palatine Hill.

name: Auditorium Maecenatis
description: An early Imperial building on the Esquiline Hill in Rome, the Auditorium Maecenatis has been connected with Maecenas as it stands in the area of his gardens (horti). While its precise function is unknown, the building may have served as a conservatory.

name: Augusteum, Antioch in Pisidia
description: The Augusteum, or Sebasteion, at Antioch in Pisidia was a monumental architectural complex dedicated to the Roman Imperial cult. Its archaeological remains were first excavated in 1913. Approximately 60 fragments of the Res Gestae Divi Augusti were discovered at the site.

name: Augusteum, so-called Basilica at Herculaneum
description: The Augusteum, or so-called Basilica, at Herculaneum, was partially investigated in the eighteenth century. This large complex (68 by 40 meters) had colonnades on three of its sides and faced on to the Decumanus Maximus across from the Basilica Noniana and the College of the Augustales.

name: Augustobriga
description: Augustobriga was an ancient city located on the left bank of the river Tagus, between Emerita Augusta and Toletum.

name: Auld Bourtreebush
description: Auld Bourtreebush is a prehistoric stone circle in Aberdeenshire, Scotland.

name: Aurelium Cananefatium/Forum Hadriani
description: Aurelium Cananefatium/Forum Hadriani (modern Voorburg) was the northernmost Roman city on the European continent. It was destroyed ca. AD 270.

name: Avaris/Ramesses
description: Avaris, located in the Nile delta region of Egypt, served as the capital city of the Hyksos.

name: Avebury stone circle
description: Avebury is a Neolithic henge monument comprised of three stone circles dating to the third millennium BCE.

name: Ayanis
description: Ayanis (or Ayanıs, now Ağartı Kalesi) is an Urartean fortress and settlement located 35 km north of Van on a rocky outcrop 250 m above Lake Van (1867 m above sea level). It was founded in the 7th century BCE under Rusa, son of Argišti, and was named Rusahinili Eidurukai ("The City of Rusa in front of Mount Eiduru"). Here two inscriptions of Rusa, son of Argišti (7th century BCE), have been found which report that Rusa cultivated the barren land, built the fortress and the city as well as a “tower temple” (“susi temple”) and a “Gate for the God Haldi” (A 12-01 and A 12-09).

name: Ayaz-Kala
description: An archaeological site in Northern Uzbekistan, dating between the fourth century BCE and the seventh century CE.

name: Babylon
description: The ancient city of Babylon began as an Akkadian city-state founded by Amorites in 1894 B.C. The city was also the seat of the Neo-Babylonian Empire from 608 to 539 B.C.

name: Bagai
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 34 F2 Bagai

name: Bagistanus (mountain)
description: Mount Behistun is located in the Kermanshah Province of Iran. It is the site of the so-called Behistun Inscription, a multilingual inscription and rock relief established by Darius the Great (r. 522–486 B.C.).

name: Bahçecik
description: Urartian site containing a ruined building and some rock inscriptions.

name: Baiae
description: A Roman seaside community on the Bay of Naples that included thermal springs.

name: Bakchias
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 75 E1 Bakchias

name: Bakr Āwā
description: Large mound with citadel in the plain of Shahrizor, occupied from Early Bronze Age to modern time.

name: Balāwāt
description: Balāwāt is the site of the ancient Assyrian city of Imgur-Enlil. A place from the TAVO Index.

name: Baliares/Gymnesiae Inss. (Maior et Minor)
description: Baliares/Gymnesiae Inss. (Maior et Minor) (The Balearic Islands) is an island group of the western Mediterranean Sea, located to the east of the Iberian peninsula. In modern geographical terms, the four largest islands of the group are Majorca, Minorca, Ibiza, and Formentera, but the latter two are also part of the Pityussae island group of antiquity.

name: Balıklı
description: A Neolithic site that is a contemporary of Aşıklı Höyük and located 14km to its north-east. Occupied ca. 8300–7900 BCE, Balıklı provides evidence for early sedentism in Central Anatolia.

name: Ballatha
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 67 G3 Ballatha

name: Banasa
description: Colonia Iulia Valentia Banasa was one of the three colonies founded by emperor Augustus between 33 and 25 B.C. in Mauretania Tingitana.

name: Bantia
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 45 D3 Bantia

name: Baou-des-Noirs
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 16 D2 Baou-des-Noirs

name: Barake (islands)
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 6 B5 Barake Inss.

name: Barbalissus
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 68 F2 Barbalissus

name: Barç
description: A hilltop archaeological site in southeastern Albania, presently named after the adjacent modern village, that lies less than 2km northeast of modern Korça. Its precise nature is not yet known, but earlier suggestions that it was a burial mound have been refuted by the discovery of significant stone walls and small finds datable to the Iron Age and possibly the Hellenistic period.

name: Barm-i Dilak
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 94 C4 Barm-i Dilak

name: Barous(s)ai? (islands)
description: The Nicobar Islands

name: Barreira Megalithic Complex
description: A prehistoric megalithic complex in the modern Sintra municipality in Portugal's Lisbon district. It was formally identified in 1961 and designated as a cultural heritage site in 1993. The complex consists of at least 24 standing stones.

name: Başbulak
description: Find spot for a stone with Urartian inscription.

name: Basilica Argentaria
description: The toponym "Basilica Argentaria" is a descriptor that appears in fourth-century CE sources for a porticoed complex that dates to the reign of Trajan. This complex was built between the slope of the Capitoline Hill and the Forum Iulium during the terraforming of the Quirinal Hill that created space for the construction of the Forum and Markets of Trajan.

name: Basilica Noniana
description: An architectural complex at Herculaneum that was renovated following the earthquake of A.D. 62 thanks to the patronage of Marcus Nonius Balbus. The site is only partly excavated. It faced onto the Decumaus Maximus, and lies directly across Cardo III from the College of the Augustales.

name: Bassiana
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 21 B5 Bassiana

name: Bastam
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 89 G2 Bastam

name: Batas-Harir
description: A first-century CE rock-cut relief at Batas-Harir is likely associated with the Parthian culture and depicts a ruler, perhaps the king Abdissares or Izates II or III of Adiabene. The site is located in Iraqi Kurdistan in the Erbil Governorate.

name: Bathos
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 61 E4 Bathos

name: Baths of Faverges
description: The Roman Baths of Faverges date to the first century.

name: Bavian/Khinnis
description: Just a few kilometers upstream of the city, the 7th-century B.C. Assyrian king Sennacherib had an architectural complex built and several stele-like panels (with accompanying inscriptions) carved into a nearby cliff face. The site may have served as a summer residence for the Assyrian king and may have been used as a headquarters to supervise the construction of a vast network of canals and aqueducts that was created specifically for providing water to Nineveh and the Assyrian heartland. The "Khinnis Reliefs" were included on the 2014 World Monuments Watch to increase awareness of cultural heritage in the Khinnis/Bavian area.

name: Baysān
description: Find place for cuneiform inscription.

name: Beer Sheva/Berosaba
description: Tel Be'er Sheva is an archeological site in southern Israel that is believed to be the location of the remains of the biblical town of Beersheba.

name: Beidha
description: Beidha is a Neolithic archaeological site located just north of Petra near Siq al-Barid in Jordan. Diana Kirkbride (1957) and Brian Byrd excavated the site. Three phases of occupation were identified beginning in the eleventh millennium BCE.

name: Beit Shemesh
description: City 30 km west of Jerusalem inhabited from the Bronze Age until its destruction in 701 CE.

name: Bele(g)ia/Veleia
description: Bele(g)ia/Veleia (Iruña-Veleia) was a Roman town along the ab Asturica Burdigalam road.

name: Belginum
description: An ancient settlement (vicus) of the Treveri. Located near modern Wederath (Bernkastel-Wittlich district) in Germany.

name: Beltany
description: At Beltany in County Donegal, Ireland, is a Neolithic stone circle monument comprised of 64 menhirs. The monument dates ca. 1400-800 BC.

name: Berà
description: A Roman triumphal arch dedicated ca. 13 BC by Lucius Licinius Sura and perhaps dedicated to Augustus.

name: Berenice Panchrysos
description: Berenice Panchrysos was a settlement in the Eastern Desert of Egypt. It was located near Sabae in the Regio Troglodytica (modern Sudan). Its name ("all golden") derives from its proximity to the gold mines Jebel Allaqi (Jebel Ollaki) that were exploited in antiquity.

name: Berenice/Berenike
description: An ancient port city on the west coast of the Red Sea in modern Egypt, Berenice/Berenike was founded in 275 BCE by Ptolemy II Philadelphus, who named it after his mother, Berenice I of Egypt.

name: Bersiba
description: Bersiba was an ancient site in Syria, inhabited as early as the Neolithic period. To the Hittites the site was known as Masuwari.

name: Beta Samati
description: Archaeological investigation at the site of Beta Samati, located between Aksum and the Red Sea, has revealed remains of a significant Aksumite administrative center. The archaeological site is located in the vicinity of Yeha, Ethiopia.

name: Bethar
description: A largely overbuilt ancient village located in the West Bank. Archaeology indicates occupation as early as the 8th century BCE. Herodean fortifications, extended and occupied by Bar Kokhba's forces, were destroyed after a Roman siege in 135 CE.

name: Bethsaida
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 69 C4 Bethsaida?/Iulias?

name: Bhir Mound
description: An archaeological site located in Pakistan's Punjab province that includes some of the earliest remains of the city of Taxila, dating ca. 800–525 BCE.

name: Bhirrana
description: Bhirrana is an archaeological site located in the Fatehabad district of the north Indian state of Haryana. The site's initial occupation predates the so-called Indus Valley civilization with activity beginning in the 8th-7th millennium BCE.

name: Bigis/Bestia Desolata/Parabeste
description: An ancient settlement, located at modern Lashkargāh (formerly Bost/Boost) in Helmand province, Afghanistan.

name: Bir Abraq
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 81 E2 Bir Abraq

name: Bisutun/Bagistana/Vastan?/Baptana
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 92 C2 Bisutun/Bagistana/Vastan?/Baptana

name: Bīt-Adad-erība
description: A town mentioned in Neo-Assyrian sources that is identified with the modern site of Tell Baqqaq, a ruined settlement mound located ca. 10 km south of Gir-e pan that is now under water.

name: Bithias
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 67 G2 Bithias

name: Blanda
description: Blanda was an ancient city of Lucania, although Pliny the Elder and Ptolemy differ as to its placement; the former places it near the coast, the latter inland. Modern scholars are in agreement that Ptolemy was incorrect. It is to be identified with the significant archaeological site at Palecastro di Tortora in modern Italy.

name: Blatobulgium
description: A Roman fort at present-day Birrens, cited: BAtlas 9 D5 Blatobulgium

name: Bolnisi
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 88 C3 Bolnisi

name: Boncuklu Tarla
description: Boncuklu Tarla is an archaeological site located in the Mardin province of Turkey. The human settlement was occupied from the Late Epipalaeolithic to Pre-Pottery Neolithic B periods.

name: Borsippa/Burs
description: An ancient city of Sumer, southwest of Babylon, on the east bank of the Euphrates River. Modern Birs Nimrūd in Iraq.

name: Borysthenes/Olbia
description: Commonly referred to as "Olbia Pontica" to distinguish it from multiple other ancient sites with the same name, the remains of this multi-period settlement are located immediately south of the modern Ukrainian village of Parutyne on the west bank of the Dnieper–Bug Estuary.

name: Bostel
description: The archaeological site of Bostel is located in Castelletto, municipality of Rotzo, province of Vicenza. The site is located some 850 meters above sea level. This ancient alpine village was inhabited from the fifth to first centuries BCE.

name: Bouleuterion
description: A Bouleuterion complex at Nicopolis in Epirus.

name: Bouleuterion at Akragas/Agrigentum
description: A bouleuterion complex at Akragas (modern Agrigento in Sicily).

name: Bouleuterion at Aspendos
description: The Bouleuterion at Aspendos dates to the early second century.

name: Bouleuterion at Dodona
description: Constructed during the Hellenistic period, this meeting hall served the Epirote league. It was destroyed and rebuilt multiple times before going out of use for this purpose during the early Roman empire.

name: Bouleuterion at Ietas
description: The Bouleuterion at Ietas dates to the later second century B.C.

name: Bouleuterion at Patara/Arsinoe
description: The Bouleuterion at Patara/Arsinoe sits on the agora opposite the theater.

name: Bouleuterion of Onchestos
description: The Bouleuterion of Onchestos has been identified adjacent to the Temple of Poseidon.

name: Braq
description: Khirbet Braq is the modern name of a ruined Nabatean sanctuary at Ayn Braq in Jordan, a spring that, in antiquity, was channeled through an aqueduct to supply ancient Petra. The heavily looted site is located just south of modern Wadi Musa, on the west side of the modern road to Taybeh.

name: Bronze Athena/Athena Promachos
description: A colossal bronze statue of Athena on the Acropolis of Athens, built in the mid-5th century BC by Pheidias. The base was repaired in the time of Augustus. In Late Antiquity, the statue was transferred to Constantinople, where it was destroyed in 1203.

name: Brusselstown Ring
description: A large fortified settlement in modern County Wicklow, Ireland, with evidence of occupation in the Late Bronze and Early Iron Ages.

name: Brykous
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 60 E4 Brykous

name: Buddhas of Bamyan
description: Monumental statues of Gautam Buddha were carved into the cliff-face at Bamyan, located in the Bamyan valley in the Hazarajat region of central Afghanistan, The statues were, respectively, 35 and 53 m tall. They were intentionally destroyed by the Taliban in 2001.

name: Building with Three Exedrae
description: The Building with Three Exedrae at Hadrian's Villa, Tivoli, is an architectural complex with a squared central court onto which three exedra with fountains open.

name: Büklükale
description: Hittite city.

name: Bulls of Guisando
description: A series of Celtiberian sculptures located near El Tiemblo, Ávila, Spain. These statues were perhaps originally created during the second century BC.

name: Burmarina
description: Minor settlement in Upper Syrian Euphrates.

name: Burmarina Archive 1
description: The small town of Burmarina belonged to the area of the city Til-Barsip which was under Neo-Assyrian rule. An archive, consisting of about 130 clay tablets and fragments, including some bullae, was discovered in two rooms, presumably part of a private house, in Area F. Although most texts were written in Assyrian, some were in Aramaic, and some had short Aramaic notes written in ink. While most of the Assyrian texts were loan or purchase documents, the archive also includes short notes and a list. The Aramaic texts concern loans or are short notes written on bullae.

name: Busa delle Vette
description: A hut and some enclosures located in the Dolomites (Veneto, Italy), datable to between the 5th and 11th centuries CE.

name: Byblos
description: Occupied since the Neolithic period, historical Byblos was originally a major Punic center called Gebal.

name: Bylazora
description: Excavated since 2008, a settlement site near the town of Knezje in Macedonia's Sveti Nikole municipality, has been identified as ancient Bylazora.

name: Cabanes
description: The remains of a Roman arch consisting of two columns supported by a square base.

name: Cabeço do Vouga
description: The ancient settlement of Talabriga is probably to be associated with the Portuguese archaeological site known today as Cabeço do Vouga.

name: Caepionis Monu.
description: Caepionis Monumentum (Tower of Caepio) was a tower-cum-lighthouse built by the Roman proconsul Quintus Servilius Caepio in 140 BC. The aim was to make the Salmedina reef at the mouth of the river Baitis (Guadalquivir) navigable. The modern toponym is Chipiona.

name: Camp of Diocletian at Palmyra
description: Camp of Diocletian in the Ancient City of Palmyra. Coordinates determined by eye in Google Earth in November 2012, following description in UNESCO World Heritage List.

name: Canopus at Hadrian's Villa
description: The Canopus garden is set at the principal entrance to Hadrian's Villa. An imposing waterway occupies all its length.

name: Capalbiaccio Tricosto
description: The fortified site of Tricosto di Capalbiaccio is located in southern Tuscany, some 6 km inland from the ancient Latin colony of Cosa (Ansedonia). The archaeological site was initially identified by means of a field survey led by Stephen L. Dyson (1976-1980). Beginning in 2009 the site was again investigated. The site had a long occupational life, beginning as a minor Etruscan center (ca. seventh century BCE) and continuing in use through the Middle Ages (tenth century CE).

name: Capanna dell’Elceto
description: The so-called Capanna dell’Elceto was part of a fortified Protovillanovan settlement located near Monte Rovello, Italy.

name: Capena
description: An ancient urban center in Faliscan territory adjacent to Etruria, Capena was sacked by the Romans in 390 BC.

name: Capera
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 24 E4 Capera

name: Capo Graziano
description: The prehistoric village of Capo Graziano dates to the Middle Bronze Age and is located on the island of Filicudi. The site was excavated between 1952 and 1969. It is situated on a terrace some 100 m above sea level. At least 27 agglomerative huts were identified at the site.

name: Carchemish
description: An ancient city that was part of the Mitanni, Hittite, and Neo-Assyrian Empires (Kargamiš in Hittite; commonly Carchemish). The site was occupied as early as the Neolithic and Chalcolithic periods; evidence for Early Bronze Age cist tombs begins ca. 2400 BC. It was a key commercial center during the time of Ebla and Mari. The Mitanni controlled the site from the sixteenth to the fourteenth centuries and was conquered by Šuppiluliuma ca. 1350 B.C. A famous battle, attested in the Bible, was fought at the site in 605 BC between the Babylonian army under Nebuchadnezzar II and that of the Egyptian Pharaoh Necho II.

name: Careiae
description: An ancient town on the Via Clodia.

name: Carmarthen
description: A Roman fort in Wales occupied from about 75 CE to at least 100 CE.

name: Carnac megaliths
description: Carnac (Breton: Karnag) is a site in southern Brittany known for the so-called Carnac stones, a massive complex of Neolithic menhir stones. Inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 2025.

name: Casa dei Crescenzi
description: A structure in Rome built between 1040 and 1065 by one Niccolò, son of Crescenzio and Teodora, in order to control the Ponte Emilio, on whose transit the family charged a toll. The building incorporates ancient spolia.

name: Casa del Obispo
description: The archaeological site of Casa del Obispo has evidence for multiple urban phases, ranging from the eighth century B.C. to the eighteenth century.

name: Casae
description: Modern Henchir el Beguar/Begar, an archaeological site located in the Tunisian governorate of Kasserine. Inscriptions of the second century CE found here in the 19th century authorized the holding of regular markets at Casae and demonstrate that it was probably the principal settlement of the Saltus Beguensis, a Roman senatorial estate.

name: Casal Bernocchi-Malafede
description: An archaeological area along the Via Ostiensis with evidence of human activity stretching from the Archaic period to the time of the Roman Empire. SIgnificant archaeological features include an extensive necropolis and a Roman bathing facility.

name: Casas del Turuñuelo
description: An archaeological site located in Spain's Extremadura region, Casas del Turuñuelo is associated with the Tartessian culture and dates from the fifth century BCE.

name: Caseggiato di Bacco e Arianna
description: The House of Bacchus and Ariadne at Ostia dates to the Hadrianic period and adjoined the Serapeum.

name: Caselle in Pittari
description: An ancient Lucanian settlement located within the territory of the comune of Caselle in Pittari has been explored via archaeological excavation since 2014. This site occupies parts of a large plateau. Evidence for an axial road running north-south and several large structures has been documented.

name: Casteddu di Ceccia
description: A stone-built monument of the Torrean civilization located south of Porto-Vecchio, Corsica.

name: Casteddu di Tappa
description: Casteddu di Tappa is a Bronze Age archaeological site in Corsica connected with the Torréen culture.

name: Castel Fiorentino
description: The castle was the site of the Holy Emperor Frederick II's death in 1250.

name: Castell Bryn Gwyn
description: Castell Bryn Gwyn is a circular Neolithic enclosure some 54 m in diameter that shows signs of re-use during the Iron Age and in the Roman period. The site is located on the Isle of Anglesey.

name: Castellazzo di Taneto
description: A Medieval fort dating from the mid-ninth to the eleventh century CE.

name: Castelliere di Monte Acuto
description: A multi-period site on Monte Acuto with evidence for habitation (Late Bronze Age) and cult activities (mid-first millennium BCE).

name: Castelvecchio di San Gimignano
description: The site of Castelvecchio di San Gimignano likely had sacred significance in the first millennium BCE. The area was occupied in the late sixth to early seventh centuries CE as it provided a location to control movement between southern Tuscany and Piedmont. The site was fortified in the twelfth century.

name: Castrum Invi
description: An Italic site located at the mouth of the Incastro river and in the territory of Ardea.

name: Castrum Minervae/Athenaion/Limen Aphrodites
description: An ancient settlement of the Sallentini in Apulia. Legendary history held that this was the spot where Aeneas first landed in Italy, thus the assignation 'Limen Aphrodites' or 'Portus Veneris'.

name: Castrum Novum
description: Castrum Novum was a colonia maritima established on the coast of Etruria.

name: Castrum Perticae
description: A fortified site of the later Byzantine period located at Finale Ligure.

name: Castulo
description: An Ibero-Roman settlement of Baetica that was inhabited beginning in the Neolithic period. It is noted for the silver mines of the Sierra Morena.

name: Cat Stane
description: An irregular standing stone bearing a fragmentary Latin inscription, the Cat Stane is likely a Neolithic monument re-used for a burial of the fifth or sixth century AD.

name: Çatıören
description: Çatıören is the modern name for ruins of the Hellenistic through Byzantine period in eastern Rough Cilicia

name: Caunus
description: An ancient city of Caria in southwest Asia Minor.

name: Caveirac milestone
description: A Roman milestone - (CIL 17-02, no. 256).

name: Celadussae (islands)
description: Celadussae Inss., a Dalmatian archipelago in the Adriatic Sea.

name: Cellarulo
description: An area in the Contrada Cellarulo of Benevento that is designated as an archaeological area with evidence for the later phases of ancient Benevento's history.

name: Cento Camerelle (Bacoli)
description: The so-called Cento Camerelle at Bacoli is an archaeological site that dates initially to the late republican period. A multi-story structure with provisions for a water system and cisterns, the building is linked initially to the Roman consul Quintus Hortensius Hortalus and later to Antonia Minor, the mother of the emperor Claudius.

name: Cercadilla
description: A Late Roman palace complex connected with the emperor Maximian.

name: Cercina (island)
description: A group of islands lying off the east coast of Tunisia in the Gulf of Gabès, the islands were a place of exile in Roman antiquity.

name: Chalasmenos
description: Chalasmenos is an archaeological site that preserves remains of a Late Minoan (LM) IIIC settlement. This site is located on a low hill (240 m asl) immediately to the south of the Cha Gorge near Monastiraki in eastern Crete.

name: Chalcidicum
description: Augustus uses the generic term Chalcidicum to describe a porch he built as an approach to the Curia Iulia.

name: Chanār-i Pāyn
description: Bronze Age site, low mound in Lorestan where cuneiform brick inscription has been found.

name: Chanctonbury Ring
description: Chanctonbury Ring is a prehistoric hill fort located on Chanctonbury Hill in West Sussex, England. It is part of the present-day South Downs Way.

name: Chani Kokkonas
description: Chani Kokkonas is an archaeological site in Tempe valley, Thessaly, Greece. The excavations revealed remains of a Hellenistic complex with both ritual and workshop areas, as well as a Byzantine church and guesthouse (khan).

name: Chapel of Wadjmose
description: Chapel dedicated to the prince Wadjmose, son of Pharaoh Thutmose I.

name: Charcha/‘Arcaiapis’/Karcharoman
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 89 C3 Charcha/‘Arcaiapis’/Karcharoman

name: Charterhouse Warren Farm Shaft
description: The Charterhouse Warren Farm Shaft is a vertical shaft (approximately 20 meters deep) in the Carboniferous limestone plateau of the Mendip Hills, Somerset, southwest England. Originally identified as a swallet, this cave feature was the site of archaeological excavations in 1972-6 and again in 1983-6. The site has produced evidence of several stratigraphic horizons and, notably, a number of disarticulated human and faunal remains. The human remains come from at least 37 Bronze Age individuals and their demise seems to have been the result of deliberate violence.

name: Chatal Höyük (Amuq)
description: Mound site in the Amuq Plain inhabited from the late 4th millennium BCE-mid-1st millennium BCE. In the Late Bronze Age Chatal Höyük was the site of a sizeable town with connections to the Hittite Empire. In the Iron Age the town was within the sphere of the Syro-Hittite state of Unqi, based at Tell Tayinat/Kinaliya. The town gradually declined, but remained inhabited without interruption until at least the fifth century BCE.

name: Chega Sofla
description: An archaeological site located in the Zeydun plain south of the provincial city of Behbahan, Iran.

name: Chelidoniae (islands)
description: A group of islands located off Cape Gelidonya on the south coast of Anatolia, modern Beşadalar (Five Islands), Turkey. Ancient commentators vary on the number of islands in the group, some counting only the largest two.

name: Chersonesos
description: Chersonesos was an ancient Greek colony founded in the southwestern part of the Crimean Peninsula, ancient Taurica. Settlers from Heraclea Pontica established the site in the sixth century B.C.

name: Chil Zena
description: A mountainous outcropping located to the west of Kandahar, Afghanistan. A flight of forty stone steps leads to the top of the outcropping. The bilingual inscription of the third century BCE known as the Kandahar Bilingual Rock Inscription is found here.

name: Chiyāpuykah
description: Bronze Age site, low mound in Lorestan where cuneiform brick inscription has been found.

name: Choburak-I
description: Cemetery site in the Altai Mountains containing burials associated with the Bulan-Koba culture (4th century CE).

name: Chogha Bonut
description: Chogha Bonut is an archaeological site located in the Khuzistan Province of Iran. It lies approximately 5km to the west of Chogha Mish. The tell was the site of a settlement of the Elamite civilization and was settled by ca. 7200 BCE.

name: Chogha Mish
description: The Chalcolithic settlement site of Chogha Mish, also known as Tappeh-ye Choghā Mīsh, dates originally to the early seventh millennium BCE and is located on the Susiana Plain of western Iran. The site was occupied continuously from the Neolithic period to the Proto-Literate period. It was eventually eclipsed by nearby Susa, but BAtlDir maintains its occupation through at least the end of the early Roman imperial period, and possibly later.

name: Choirades Nesoi
description: A small archipelago composed of two islands - San Pietro and San Paolo - in the Gulf of Taranto.

name: Choragic Monument of Lysicrates
description: The Choragic Monument of Lysicrates is a round structure erected in 335/4 BC in Athens to honor the victory of a choregos in a dramatic competition.

name: Chorazin
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 69 C4 Chorazin

name: Chrysopolitissa complex
description: The Chrysopolitissa complex in Paphos is an archaeological area that includes various ruins. Among these are those of an early Christian basilica (fourth to seventh centuries), a medieval Franciscan church (thirteenth century), and the so-called Pillar of St. Paul.

name: Chryssi-Gaidouronisi
description: The archaeological remains of a small Minoan settlement of approximately 12-20 houses on the island of Chryssi. Evidence for murex processing was discovered during excavations conducted in 2008.

name: Chughā Gāwānah
description: Large mound in the middle of Shahabad-e Gharb (Islamabad) in Western Iran, possibly ancient Palum.

name: Chughā Pahn East
description: A mound where a cuneiform inscription has been found.

name: Chughā Pahn West
description: A mound where a cuneiform inscription has been found.

name: Chughā Sabz
description: A mound where a cuneiform inscription has been found.

name: Cillium
description: Cillium (modern Kasserine, Tunisia) occupies a strategic location in the gap between the Semmama and Chambi mountains, along the road from Gafsa.

name: Çine Tepecik Höyük
description: Human occupation at Çine-Tepecik begins in the Chalcolithic period. The site is located in a fertile alluvial plain near the Meander River in western Anatolia. In the Late Bronze Age, this place was located in the Arzawa lands.

name: Cividade de Bagunte
description: The Cividade de Bagunte is a fortified Iron Age site that became Romanized. It is located in the municipality of Vila do Conde in the Porto district of Portugal.

name: Civita d'Arlena
description: Civita d'Arlena (also known as Civita del Fosso di Arlena) is an archaeological site that occupies a hilltop near Lake Bolsena. It was the site of Etruscan occupation until the third century BCE. Excavations carried out by the École française de Rome (1952 to 1958) revealed parts of a fortification wall and a sacred building, along with evidence for a city plan.

name: Claterna
description: Claterna was a Roman a town of Gallia Cispadana, located along the Via Aemilia between Bononia and Forum Cornelii.

name: Clickimin broch
description: A Broch tower with other associated Iron Age structures.

name: Co(n)silinum/Cosilianum
description: Co(n)silinum/Cosilianum was a statio on the Via Popilia (Annia) in ancient Calabria.

name: Col di Marzo, Perugia
description: Survey and excavation at Montelabate overlooking the upper Tiber valley in the territory of Perugia have revealed evidence for an Etruscan settlement on the hill of Col di Marzo (Perugia). This place is located some 10 km from Arna and rises to 645 meters above sea level.

name: Col. Clunia (Sulpicia)
description: Col. Clunia (Sulpicia) was a Roman city of Hispania Tarraconensis. The site was besieged by Cn. Pompeius Magnus during the war against Sertorius.

name: Col. Ulpia Traiana/Tricensimae
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 11 G1 Col. Ulpia Traiana/Tricensimae

name: Colle delle Fate
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 42 F4 Colle delle Fate

name: Colle Madore
description: Colle Madore is an archaeological site connected with the Sicanians.

name: College of the Augustales
description: The so-called College of the Augustales at Herculaneum was built at the end of the first century BC. The brothers A. Lucius Proculus and A. Lucius Iulianus served as patrons of the project.

name: Collegium at Italica
description: A building that has been identified as the seat of a Collegium because of its internal structure and size, was previously known as the"House of the Exedra".

name: Colonnacce
description: Le Colonnacce refers to preserved elements of the lateral, north-south Corinthian-order colonnades that flanked the Forum Transitorium.

name: Colossus Neronis
description: The "Colossus Neronis" or "Colossal Statue of Nero" was a monumental bronze statue of the first-century CE emperor reportedly created by Zenodorus. The statue was originally an element of the sprawling Domus Aurea of Nero but the monument endured long after Nero's reign. The statue's original position likely marked the summit of the Velian Hill. The Flavians refashioned the statue to resemble Sol and Hadrian relocated the statue nearer the Flavian amphitheater as part of the work to create the Temple of Venus and Roma.

name: Column of Julian
description: The Column of Julianus, ca. 362 CE, honors the military exploits of the Roman Emperor Julian.

name: Column of Marcus Aurelius
description: The Column of Marcus Aurelius is a monumental victory column erected along the Via Lata between 176 and 193 CE. The monument celebrated victories realized against the Marcomanni and the Sarmatians (172-175 CE).

name: Column of Trajan
description: The Column of Trajan (or Trajan's Column) is a victory column that was erected in the Forum of Trajan at Rome ca. 113 in order to commemorate Trajan's victories in the Dacian Wars. The column is particularly noted for its helical historiated bas relief frieze decoration.

name: Comana Pontica/Hierocaesarea
description: An ancient city of Pontus.

name: Comitium of Pompeii
description: The complex referred to as the Comitium of Pompeii is located in the southeast corner of the square of the Forum and was built during the second century BC.

name: Comitium of Poseidonia/Paestum
description: The comitium is a component of a monumental forum complex constructed at Paestum after the city became a Latin colony in 273 B.C.

name: Complesso dei Riti Magici
description: The so-called "complex of the magic rites" is a building located in Regio II of the archaeological site of Pompeii. Excavations were conducted in 1954, 1957, 1963, and 1985. The modern toponym is derived from various ritual items discovered in the complex, which may have been dedicated to the cult of Sabazios.

name: Complesso episcopale
description: The Late Antique to early Medieval "Episcopal" complex of the Santissima Trinità located at Venosa.

name: Complutum
description: Complutum (modern Alcalá de Henares in Spain) was an ancient oppidum founded in the first century CE and largely re-fashioned in the third century.

name: Conimbriga
description: Conimbriga was a Celtic and Roman settlement in Portugal that was inhabited between the ninth century BC and the seventh century AD.

name: Coptos
description: An ancient settlement on the Nile river (modern Quft/Qift in Egypt) that flourished from pre-dynastic times through late antiquity.

name: Corent
description: Corent was the site of a Celtic and Gallo-Roman oppidum.

name: Corfinium
description: Corfinium was the principal city of the Paeligni in the valley of the Aternus river. The site becomes the capital of the breakaway Italic groups that oppose Rome during the Social War.

name: Corinthus/Korinthos
description: The ancient Greek and Roman city of Corinth, located in the Peloponnese, Greece. Also known today as Archaia Korinthos and not to be confused with the nearby modern town.

name: Cornus
description: Cornus was an ancient settlement on the west coast of Sardinia. The site is located in the modern municipal territory of S. Caterina di Pittinuri.

name: Cortaillod-Est
description: Settlement on the northern shore of Lake Neuchâtel in Switzerland containing the inundated remains of a Bronze Age village, inhabited ca. 1010-955 BC.
