description: The site of Podere Cannici has been investigated by means of both non-invasive geophysics and by excavation. Data reveal multiple phases of activity and occupation.

name: Podere Marzuolo
description: Podere Marzuolo was the site of Roman pottery production from the first century B.C. until the early fourth century A.D. The site has yielded evidence for the production of terra sigillata pottery as well as evidence of blacksmithing.

name: Podouke/Arikamedu
description: Podouke/Arikamedu is an archaeological site located in Kakkayanthope, Ariyankuppam Commune, Puducherry, India.

name: Poggio Civitate (Murlo)
description: An Etruscan elite complex of the seventh and sixth centuries B.C. located at Poggio Civitate, a hill adjacent to the medieval town of Murlo and about 25 km south of Siena.

name: Poggio Murella
description: A large, rectangular cistern (Castellum aquarum) located near Poggio Murella was built in opus reticulatum.

name: Poiessa
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 58 G2 Poiessa

name: Polonnaruwa
description: An ancient city of the Kingdom of Polonnaruwa dating ca. AD 1070. Inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1982.

name: Polyrrenia
description: Polyrrenia was an important ancient city of western Crete.

name: Pomarico Vecchio
description: A fortified center of Lucania founded in the fifth century B.C. or earlier, Pomarico Vecchio was influenced by its close contacts with the Greek cities of Magna Graecia.

name: Pompei Tropaea
description: In 71 BC as Pompey returned to Italy from Iberia he set up a monument in the Pyrenees known as the Pompei Tropaea. This monument commemorated the 876 cities Pompey had conquered. Pompey's monument was spoliated in late antiquity and a medieval church and later priory were built on the site. Spoliated in turn, what is left of all these structures now comprises the Sitio arqueológico de Panissars on the French/Spanish border.

name: Pompeii II.3.7 garden entrance
description: Pompeii II, 3, 7 is a garden entrance linked to II, 3, 8 and II, 3,9. II, 3, 8 is identified as a workshop, either the Officina vasaria or caupona of Nicanor. This complex was positioned across the street from the Palaestra.

name: Pompeiopolis/Sebaste
description: Pompeiopolis/Sebaste, in Paphlagonia, was founded under Pompeius in 64 B.C. in the valley of the Amnias river. The ancient site, known today as Zımbıllı Tepesi Höyüğü, is located across the river (modern Gökırmak) from modern Taşköprü in Turkey.

name: Pompey's pillar
description: A Roman triumphal column in Alexandria, Egypt.

name: Pontia (island)
description: An island placed by Ptolemy off the coast of the Greater Syrtis, along with Maia and Misynos. The name Pontia is used in the plural by Ps. Skylax to identify all three islands as a group. These islands are identified with modern scholars as prominent parts of the modern Legarah reef (Tre Scogli) off the Libyan coast.

name: Pontiae (islands)
description: An archipelago in the Tyrrhenian Sea off the west coast of Italy.

name: Pontikokastro
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 61 F4 Pontikokastro

name: Porta Borsari
description: A first century A.D. gate at Verona, the point at which the Via Postumia entered the city.

name: Portara on Naxos
description: The Portara is the 26-foot-tall doorway of an unfinished Ionic temple of the Late Archaic Period on the island of Naxos.

name: Porte de Mars
description: The Porte de Mars is a Roman triumphal arch in Reims, France, that dates from the third century A.D.

name: Porte Noire (Besançon)
description: A Gallo-Roman triumphal arch dedicated to Marcus Aurelius.

name: Porticus Absidata
description: The Porticus Absidata was an exedra porticata that served as the northern apsidal entryway to the Forum of Nerva, also known as the Forum Transitorium.

name: Porticus Aemilia
description: A utilitarian porticus and storage building located outside the Porta Trigemina, the Porticus Aemilia was erected in 193 BCE by Marcus Aemilius Lepidus and and Lucius Aemilius Paullus.

name: Porticus Minucia
description: The Porticus Minucia was a portico that stood in the Campus Martius in Rome. It was initially built by M. Minucius Rufus, consul in 110 B.C.

name: Porticus Octaviae
description: A porticus in the area of the Circus Flaminius, the Porticus Octaviae was dedicated by Augustus sometime after 27 B.C. in honor of his sister, Octavia Minor. The porticus replaced the Porticus Metelli. It was restored twice after major urban fires in A.D. 80 and 203.

name: Porticus Philippi
description: A portico that surrounded the Temple of Hercules Musarum in the Campus Martius of ancient Rome. The construction of the portico was the result of the patronage of L. Marcius Philippus, the stepfather of Augustus.

name: Porticus Pompei
description: A porticus built in 55 B.C. in connection with the Theater of Pompey. The Curia Pompei, site of Iulius Caesar's assassination in 44 B.C., was likely located within the portico.

name: Portus
description: The great ancient maritime port of Rome, located on the right-hand bank of the Tiber River.

name: Portus Iulius
description: The so-called "Portus Iulius" is an ancient Roman port complex on the Bay of Naples. Its remains are now submerged due to the bradyseism of the Phlegraean Fields. The archaeological remains were first rediscovered in 1956.

name: Possible heroön (Salamis)
description: A seven-room building was brought to light by underwater archaeological research in Salamis. The architectural remains may be those of a hero shrine (heroön) connected with the Greek hero Ajax.

name: Potentia
description: A city settled in an area subjugated by Rome during the fourth century B.C. The local populace rebelled against Rome after the defeat at Cannae (216 B.C.), however the defeat of the Carthaginians eventually led to Potentia's reincorporation into the Roman sphere.

name: Pottenbrunn burial ground
description: Burial site near modern Pottenbrunn, Austria, containing 43 burials dated to the Roman period.

name: Pozzo Pantaleo
description: The 'Pozzo Pantaleo' is a Roman mausoleum that made use of a cistern of the early Imperial period. In a third phase the site was repurposed as a shrine dedicated to the Christian Saint Pantaleon.

name: Praetorian Guard Barracks at Amba Aradam Metro Station
description: Excavations for Rome's Metro C public transit line at the Amba Aradam Station revealed the remains of a second century A.D. barracks connected with the Praetorian Guard, along with a domus identified as that of a praetorian commandant. Although ruinous, the architectural complex demonstrates good preservation of some features, notably floor mosaics and wall paintings. The second century A.D. stratigraphic horizon was reached at a depth of approximately 12 meters.

name: Praetorium at Gortyna
description: The Praetorium at Gortyna dates to the first century A.D.

name: Pre-Mithraeum Building(s)
description: Fragmentary remains of three phases of pre-Roman period buildings under the foundations of the Mithraeum at Dura-Europos.

name: Priddy Circles
description: Priddy Circles describes a linear arrangement of circular earthwork enclosures near the village of Priddy, Mendip Hills, Somerset, England.

name: Priene/‘Lince’?
description: Priene/‘Lince’? was an ancient Greek city of Ionia on an escarpment of Mycale Mons.

name: Propylaea at Athens
description: A monumental gateway at the western end of the Athenian Acropolis. As an element of the Periklean remaking of the Athenian Acropolis, work on the Propylaea commenced in 437 BCE and was abandoned, unfinished, in 432 BCE. The architect Mnesikles was responsible for the Propylaea.

name: Proserpina
description: A Roman-era reservoir and dam northwest of modern Mérida in Spain, which was tapped to supply Emerita Augusta (modern Mérida) via aqueduct.

name: Prummu?/Primii?
description: The one-time capital of the ancient Kerma culture in Nubia.

name: Prytanikon at Athens
description: The Prytanikon was the seat of the Prytaneis and was located on the west side of the Agora in proximity to the Old Bouleuterion, with which it formed an architectural unit. This structure was supplanted by the later Tholos and its remains are referred to as "Building F". It dates to the later period of the Peisistratids, ca. 550-525 B.C.

name: Psophis
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 58 B2 Psophis

name: Public administration buildings
description: The administration buildings on Pompeii's forum were built before 80 B.C. and renovated following the earthquake of A.D. 62. The central building is generally regarded as the tabularium.

name: Public building (?) area at Halos
description: According to Reinders, the slope above the lower town of Halos was probably used for public buildings.

name: Puente Tablas Iberian Oppidum
description: An Iberian settlement at the modern town of Puente Tablas (Jaén, Andalucia), with initial settlement in the late Bronze Age that continued until the 4th century BCE. The site was reoccupied during the Islamic period.

name: Puig Castellar (Biosca)
description: A hilltop fortified site southwest of modern Biosca in Catalonia, Spain. The site exhibits architecture and pottery indicating its function as a Roman military castellum during the second century BCE.

name: Pullariae (islands)
description: Pullariae Inss. (the modern the Brijuni or the Brionian Islands).

name: Punta Secca
description: An ancient settlement at Punta Secca on Sicily's southern coast. Many scholars have long identified it as a "Kaukana" mentioned by Ptolemy and Procopius, and this identification has been reflected in modern toponymy and in the scholarly literature. It has been rejected -- because there is no natural harbor at Punta Secca -- by R.J.A. Wilson in favor of an as-yet-unlocated position on the coast somewhere east of Capo Scalambri.

name: Purpurariae (islands)
description: An island group off the western coast of Morocco.

name: Puy d'Issolud
description: A Gallic site in the French commune of Vayrac, near the village of Saint-Denis-lès-Martel, often considered to be identifiable with the ancient cite of Uxellodunum, mentioned by Caesar.

name: Puzriš-Dagan
description: An ancient city of Lower Mesopotamia, Puzriš-Dagan was founded in the twenty-first century B.C. by King Shulgi of Ur.

name: Pydna
description: An ancient settlement and seaport in Macedonia, located south of modern Makrigialos. According to Apollodorus, the population was relocated to a new, inland site by Archelaos (see Pydna/Kitron), but it is clear that this original, coastal site was reoccupied.

name: Pyraloi (islands)
description: The Lamu Archipelago is located in the Indian Ocean near the northern coast of Kenya.

name: Pyrrichos
description: An ancient city of the Peloponnesian Mani, located near the modern village of Kavalos/Pyrrhikhos.

name: Qa`la Bost
description: Qa`la Bost is an ancient fortress within the ancient city of Bust, located on the Helmand River in southern Afghanistan and overseeing a major crossroads linking Kandahar and India to eastern Iran. Standing remains at the site predominantly date to the Islamic Middle Ages, but archaeological finds suggest an occupational history dating back to at least the first millennium BCE.

name: Qā`lah Arbīl
description: Citadel mound of modern Erbil, find place for cuneiform inscription.

name: Qal‘at ‘Awaynah
description: Stronghold with traces of Assyrian buildings where inscribed blocks have been found. Possibly ancient Alše.

name: Qal’eh-i Imam
description: Qal’eh-i Imam (“Imam Fortress”), also call Qal’eh-i Marivan (“Marivan Fortress”), is one of the most important castles and fortresses in the Kurdistan Province, Marivan District, Iran. This now-ruined military fortification played a key role during the Safavid Period (1502–1737) and was an administrative venter of Ardalanians and Babanians rulers in Kurdistan. The discovery of a stele fragment of the Assyrian king Sargon II (r. 721–705 BC) in the drystone wall enclosing one of the buildings of the village Barqaleh might attest to a settlement there in the eighth century BC.

name: Qalat al-Bahrain
description: An ancient settlement with evidence of occupation stretching from 2300 BC to the sixteenth century AD, Qal'at al-Bahrain was once the capital of the Dilmun civilization. In 2005, it was listed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site.

name: Qalat-i Dinka
description: Archaeological site in the Peshdar Plain with remains dated to the Neo-Assyrian period. The mound is part of a larger frontier settlement known as the Dinka Settlement Complex.

name: Qalhat
description: An ancient city in Oman that was a key location for Indian Ocean trade networks.

name: Qaqun
description: Settlement mound from Assyrian times, with medieval ruins.

name: Qaryat ad Dawā’irah, Libya
description: A possible fortified farm site.

name: Qasr al-Mshatta
description: An Umayyad palace in Jordan, the construction of Qasr al-Mshatta is thought to have been started under Caliph Walid II around A.D. 743-44, just before the Abbassid Revolution, and it was never completed.

name: Qasr al-Sumayra
description: A late Roman fort in Egypt's Kharga Oasis.

name: Qasr el-'Al
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 71 B3 Qasr el-'Al

name: Qasr eth-Thuraiya
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 71 B3 Qasr eth-Thuraiya

name: Qasr Nessima
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 79 D3 Qasr Nessima

name: Qasr Shemamok
description: A tell located some 30 kilometers south of Erbil in Iraq's Kurdistan Region. Occupation at the site began in the second millennium BCE and continued through Parthian and Sassanian phases.

name: Qaṣr-i Abūnaṣr
description: Small mound with Achaemenian remains.

name: Qatna
description: A city in western Syria that was first settled in the Early Bronze Age III (ca. 2700-2600 BCE). In the Middle Bronze Age (2000-1600 BCE), it became a major trading center and the capital of a regional kingdom. In the Late Bronze Age, the city of Qatna became a vassal of first the Mitanni Kingdom, then the Neo-Hittite Empire, and Egypt. The city's acropolis was abandoned in the Late Bronze Age I, though the surrounding area remained inhabited. It was resettled in the 10th century BCE as part of the Aramaean kingdom of Hamath, but was sacked by the army of Sargon II in 720 and ultimately abandoned in the 6th century BCE.

name: Qattara?/Karana?
description: Tell al-Rimah, is the site of a settlement inhabited from the third to first millennium BCE that has been identified as the site of either Qattara or Karana.

name: Qermez Dere
description: A tell located in Tal Afar in Nineveh Governorate, Iraq, that was the site of a Neolithic settlement established ca. 8500-7900 BCE. Neolithic roundhouses were documented at the site.

name: Quadriporticus of the theatre
description: A four-sided portico standing behind the large theater of Pompeii (VIII, 7, 16-17). The complex was constructed ca. 80 BC.

name: Quartier du Stade at Delos
description: Neighborhood surrounding the stadium in the settlement of Delos, constructed during the Hellenistic period.

name: Qurghan tepe
description: Identified by W. Ball as "Qurghan tepe, Imām Ṣāhib" and distinct from other similar toponyms.

name: Quseir Amra
description: A desert castle built in the early eighth century CE by Walid Ibn Yazid, who would become the future Umayyad caliph Walid II. Inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1985.

name: Qutb Minar
description: The second tallest minar (73 m) in India after Minar-E-Fateh was built by Qutb-ud-din Aibak beginning in AD 1192. The monument is listed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site.

name: Rabat Tapah
description: FInd place for cuneiform inscription.

name: Rās al-Maṭbakh
description: Find place for cuneiform inscription.

name: Ras Ibn Hani
description: Archaeological evidence on this cape on the northwest coast of the modern country of Syria demonstrates continuous human occupation from the Bronze Age through Byzantine times, down to the modern day.

name: Razliq
description: Razliq is a village situated ca. 13 km north of the city Sarāb in Eastern Azerbaijan (Iran). Here a rock inscription of the Urartian king Argišti II, son of Rusa (end of 8th / first half of 7th century BCE), has been found which reports military campaigns of Argišti II to this region. As well as the rock inscription of Nashteban (CTU A 11-5) it is situated on the southern slope of the Sabalān Mountains at an altitude of 4810 m. Although the inscription reports the building of a fortress named ‘stronghold of Argišti’ no such archaeological remains have been discovered so far. Together with the rock inscriptions found in Nashteban (CTU A 11-5) and Shisheh (CTU A 11-6) the rock inscription found near Razliq is one of the Urartian inscriptions found furthest to the east.

name: Regia
description: The Regia is an ancient complex along the Sacra Via in Rome at the margin of the Forum Romanum, adjacent to the Temple of Vesta. It is understood as a sort of ritual headquarters of Rome's early kings and, later, as a structure connected with the office of the pontifex maximus.

name: Regio V, Pompeii
description: The so-called Regio V, a largely unexcavated archaeological subdivision located in the northern section of Pompeii, contains a mixture of commercial and domestic structures, including a gladiators' barracks.

name: Residential Palace ("P")
description: Residential Palace ("P") is often regarded as the residence of Cyrus the Great at Pasargadae.

name: Review Palace
description: An Assyrian armory was built to the south of the citadel at Nineveh. The ruins of the 7th-century B.C. architectural complex are directly below the modern settlement built on top of Nebi Yunus, the site's smaller mound.

name: Rhaithou
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 76 D5 Rhaithou

name: Rhamnous
description: Rhamnous was an ancient settlement of Attica adjacent to the Euboean Strait and a coastal deme of the tribe Aiantis.

name: Rhapta
description: A city and trading port on the east coast of Africa, mentioned in the Periplus of the Erythraean Sea and other sources. Probably to be located in the area south of modern Dar es-Salaam, in or around the Rufiji Delta and Mafia archipelago (Tanzania). Rhapta's location has not been archaeologically verified, but monumental architecture identified in 2016 off the north coast of Mafia island is a strong candidate.

name: Rhodussae (islands)
description: A group of small islands in the Propontis.

name: Rhypes
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 58 C1 Rhypes

name: Ring of Brodgar
description: The Ring of Brodgar (also known as Brogar, or the Ring o' Brodgar) is a Neolithic henge and stone circle monument in Orkney, Scotland.

name: Rione Terra
description: The original urban nucleus of Puteoli, dating from the second century B.C. onwards.

name: Ripa Puteolana
description: A coastal area of Pozzuoli (ancient Puteoli) with numerous ancient remains is now submerged due to the bradyseism of the Phlegraean Fields. The ruins of a Nabataean shrine were documented here in 2024.

name: Rochemaure milestone
description: A Roman milestone of the second century A.D.

name: Rock inscription near ‘Ayn-i Rūm
description: Site for Urartian rock inscription.

name: Rofalco
description: A fortified Etruscan site located in the territory of Vulci.

name: Rognes
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 15 E2 Rognes

name: Rollright Stones
description: A complex of three Neolithic and Bronze Age megalithic monuments dating from the fourth to second millennia B.C. located on the borders of Oxfordshire and Warwickshire in the English Midlands.

name: Roman Agora of Athens
description: The Roman agora at Athens is located to the north of the Acropolis and to the east of the ancient Agora.

name: Roman arch
description: A triumphal arch located in the civic forum.

name: Roman arch
description: The Roman arch that stands at Musti (Tunisia).

name: Roman arch (Medinaceli)
description: A triple-bay Roman Imperial arch near Medinaceli, Spain, that was originally dedicated to the emperor Domitian and later re-dedicated to Trajan.

name: Roman arch at Capera
description: A quadrifons arch at Capera.

name: Roman arch at Spoletium
description: The so-called "Arch of Drusus and Germanicus" at Spoleto.

name: Roman baths at Is Bangius
description: Terme Romane di Is Bangius

name: Roman forum at Cupra Maritima
description: The Roman forum at Cupra Maritima.

name: Roman milestone at Locmeltro
description: A Roman milestone.

name: Roman Military Palace
description: Palace building located at the northeast within the military sector at Dura-Europos.

name: Roman necropolis at al-Bass
description: The Tyre necropolis, part of the UNESCO World Heritage site of Tyre, contains a triumphal arch and hundreds of stone sarcophagi dated to the Roman and Byzantine periods. It is located adjacent to an older Phoenician burial site.

name: Roman temple at Ain Amur
description: The archaeological site of a Roman temple at Ain Amur.

name: Roman villa of Torre di Pordenone
description: A Roman villa and bath complex located in the valley of the Noncello River. The villa complex, whose original construction dates to the first century BCE, was likely rebuilt during the second century CE.

name: Roman Wall and Albarrana Islamic Tower
description: The Roman Wall and Albarrana Islamic Tower are included in the UNESCO World Heritage Site "Archaeological Ensemble of Mérida".

name: Roussolakkos
description: A Minoan site in eastern Crete.

name: Royal Palace of Qatna
description: A large palace constructed in about 1600 BCE in Qatna, an important Bronze Age city in Syria.

name: Rudiae
description: An ancient Messapic town, Rudiae was the birthplace of the poet Ennius.

name: Ruínas romanas das Carvalheiras
description: An archaeological site at Braga located close to the forum of the ancient city that preserves remains of domestic architecture dating from the first century BCE until the fifth century CE.

name: Rujm el-Farideyyeh
description: A Roman military site in Jordan, it was bulldozed out of existence between 2013 and 2017.

name: Rujm el-Hiri
description: An ancient megalithic structure located in the Israeli-occupied portion of the Golan Heights about 16 km (9.9 miles) east of the coast of the Sea of Galilee. Rujm el-Hiri consists of concentric stone circles with a tumulus at its center. The structure dates to the Early Bronze Age II period (ca. 3000–2700 BCE).

name: Rukhaegarh
description: Settlement site near Nalanda, Bihar District, India, containing remains dated from the Northern Black Polished Ware period through the early Medieval period.

name: Rusubbicari Matidiae
description: This ancient settlement, mentioned in several Roman-era itineraries and sources, has been identified with the ruin/archaeological site known in modern times as Marsa al Dajaj, which is located adjacent to the modern Algerian coastal village of Zemmouri El Bahri.

name: Rutschi Tig
description: The findspot for a cuneiform inscription.

name: S. Chuís
description: A Pre-Roman hillfort in the Asturias region, near the northern coast of the Iberian peninsula. The fort was constructed around 1000 BCE and remained in use throughout the Iron Age and shows signs of occupation during the Roman period through the third century CE.

name: S. Erasmo di Cesi
description: An ancient settlement, architectural complex featuring polygonal masonry, and associated burials at modern S. Erasmo di Cesi dates to the archaic period and has evidence for activity through the third century BCE.

name: Sa Torreta
description: The Talayotic settlement of Sa Torreta is located at Es Grau, in the north-eastern part of Menorca.

name: Saarbrücken
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 11 H3 Saarbrücken

name: Sabu-Jaddi
description: Sabu-Jaddi is the site of multi-period Nubian rock art and is located along the Nile River downstream of the third cataract region on the east bank of the Nile. These rock drawings are found on sandstone cliffs between the Sudanese villages of Sabu and Jaddi.

name: Saepta Iulia
description: An enclosure in the Campus Martius surrounded by porticoes built by Marcus Agrippa before ca. 26 BCE.

name: Saint-Aunès milestone
description: A Roman milestone of the Via Aurelia.

name: Saint-Martin-le-Bas, Gruissan
description: An archaeological site covering some 3 ha in the modern French commune of Gruissan (Aude department), with occupation beginning in the first century BCE and continuing to Late Antiquty.

name: Salem/Salumias
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 69 C5 Salem/Salumias

name: Salmanağa
description: Find place for cuneiform inscription.

name: Sam'al
description: A Hittite and Aramaean city located at Zincirli Höyük in the Anti-Taurus mountains. The site appears in Neo-Assyrian sources as Samʾalla and Aramaic texts as šmʿl (also yʾdy). It is identified with the modern site of Zincirli.

name: Samaria/Sebaste
description: Samaria/Sebaste was an ancient city of Iudaea. In 30 B.C. it passed under the control of Herod the Great and was renamed Sebaste in honor of the emperor Augustus.

name: Samothrace (settlement)
description: The homonymous settlement on the Aegean island, located on its north shore (modern Paleopolis).

name: Šamuḫa
description: Hittite settlement.

name: San Esteban Ancient Settlement
description: An ancient settlement that flourished during the Roman Republic and Early Empire. It is located on the modern Cerro San Esteban at El Poyo del Cid in the Calamocha municipality, Teruel province, Aragon, Spain. Its ancient name is not known.

name: San Giovenale
description: The archaeological remains of an Etruscan settlement were discovered at San Giovenale, close to Blera, Italy, by the Swedish Institute at Rome in the 1950s and 1960s. The ancient name of the site is unknown.

name: San Giuliano
description: An Etruscan settlement evidenced by its extensive, surviving necropolis, which now lies within the Parco Naturale Regionale Marturanum in the administrative territory of Barbarano Romano, Italy. Its modern name is transferred from a nearby medieval church.

name: San Rocco Military Complex (Trieste)
description: A Roman military fortification at modern Trieste, dating from the third to the first centuries BCE.

name: Sanctuary at San Casciano dei Bagni
description: An ancient sanctuary located near the village of San Casciano dei Bagni, located south of Siena, Italy, has produced, following archaeological excavation, a large cache of metallic votive offerings - coins, figurines, statues, anatomical votives - dating to the late republican and early imperial periods. These votives were recovered from waterlogged mud deposits associated with the thermal springs at the site that have been frequented since at least the first millennium BCE.

name: Sanctuary of Artemis at Avlonas
description: A sanctuary possibly sacred to Artemis on the island of Lemnos.

name: Sanctuary of Bendis
description: Sanctuary of Bendis located on the Acropolis of Salamis

name: Sanctuary of Isis and Magna Mater
description: The Sanctuary of Isis and Magna Mater at Roman Mogontiacum (modern Mainz, Germany) was active between the first century and the third century A.D.

name: Sanctuary of Poseidon (Onchestos)
description: Pausanias describes a Sanctuary of Poseidon located at Onchestos that preserved a grove sacred to the god and that Homer had once praised.

name: Sant Agustí Vell
description: Sant Agustí Vell (also known as Poblat talaiòtic de Sant Agustí or "Talayotic village of Sant Agustí") is an archaeological site attributed to the Talayotic culture located in the municipality of Es Migjorn Gran in the south of the Balearic island of Menorca. It flourished between 850 BCE and the Roman invasion in 123 BCE.

name: Santa Cristina in Caio a Buonconvento (Siena)
description: An ancient vicus with a thermal complex (first century BCE to fourth century CE) with phases of post-ancient re-use. The site is located in the modern Italian municipal territory of Buonconvento, Tuscany.

name: Santi Quaranta
description: The remains of a long, early Imperial-period Roman cryptoporticus at Beneventum that was re-used in the Middle Ages. It is situated between the modern San Lorenzo district and the rural area of Cellarulo, near the Basilica of the Madonna delle Grazie in Benevento, Italy.

name: Santorini Archipelago
description: The islands of the Santorini archipelago, inhabited until the Late Bronze Age, were formed by modern and historic volcanism.

name: Sapinuwa
description: Bronze Age Hittite city at the location of modern Ortaköy, Turkey

name: Sar Mashhad
description: Sar Mashhad, located in the Dadin Rural District, Jereh and Baladeh District, Kazerun County, Fars Province, Iran, is the site of a Sasanian rock relief of a lion hunt, along with accompanying inscriptions. The monument dates to the reign of the king Bahram II (r. 274–293 CE).

name: Sar-i Pol-i Zohāb
description: The findspot for a cuneiform inscription.

name: Sarantaporo ancient settlement
description: Located just east of modern Sarantaporo in the Elassona municipality (Larisa, Greece), this ancient settlement was excavated in the late 20th century. Its excavators proposed an identification with ancient Doliche, which previously had been only provisionally located. It may represent the original seat of Doliche. The late-antique fortified site at Kastri Livadiou (q.v.) seems to have made use of spoliated material including an inscription that names Doliche as a polis.

name: Sarazm
description: An ancient, proto-urban site located in north-western Tajikistan.

name: Sardarapat
description: Find place for cuneiform inscription.

name: Sardurihinili
description: An Urartian fortress constructed in the eighth century BCE and destroyed in the seventh century BCE.

name: Sarepta/Makra Kome
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 69 B3 Sarepta/Makra Kome

name: Sarissa
description: Sarissa was a Hittite site of the second millennium BC. Its remains are located at Kuşaklı, Turkey. Excavations have recovered a substantial archive of Hittite texts.

name: Sarıca
description: The find spot for a cuneiform inscription.

name: Sartaba/Alexandreion
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 70 G1 Sartaba/Alexandreion

name: Sarvestan Palace
description: The Sassanid Palace at Sarvestan is an elite building of the fifth century A.D. It is located approximately 90 km southeast of Shiraz.

name: Satyrion/Saturum
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 45 F4 Satyrion/Saturum

name: Sauria?
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 54 D4 Sauria?

name: Say(e)?
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 81 A5 Say(e)?

name: Sboryanovo Historical and Archaeological Reserve
description: Protected area located between the villages of Sveshtari and Malak Porovets in northeastern Bulgaria containing over 140 identified archaeological sites from different historical periods. Among the sites in the landscape is the Thracian Tomb of Sveshtari, a Unesco World Heritage site.

name: Schola Praeconum
description: The so-called Schola Praeconum is located at the foot of the Palatine Hill near the church of S. Anastasia. A black-and-white floor mosaic discovered during excavations depicts public heralds known as praecones, thus the name of the site.

name: School of Homer Ag. Athanasios excavations (Ithaca)
description: A site on Ithaca that local tradition links to both Homer and Odysseus. Excavations in 1998 near the Church of St. Athanasius documented a series of walls including both Bronze Age and Iron Age phases, as well as a Hellenistic building constructed using orthostates, and a circular structure with a diameter of more than 7 meters.

name: Scornavacche
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 47 F4 Scornavacche

name: Scythopolis/Nysa
description: A settlement located at the juncture of the Jordan and Jezreel valleys, Scythopolis/Nysa had important Egyptian through Roman phases.

name: Sebasteion at Aphrodisias
description: The Sebasteion at Aphrodisias was a temple complex dedicated to Aphrodite and the Iulio-Claudian emperors that local patrons financed. It was built between ca. 20 and 60 CE. The complex was excavated between 1979 and 1981.

name: Sebastopolis/Herakleopolis/Karana?
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 64 C1 Sebastopolis/Herakleopolis/Karana?

name: Segesta/Egesta
description: A center of the Elymian people in northwestern Sicily.

name: Sela
description: Sela (also as-Silaʿ and es-Selaʿ) was the the capital of the land Edom. The city is equated with the biblical Joktheel/Jokteel/Jectehel.

name: Seleucia/Claudioseleucia/Seleucia Sidera
description: Seleucia Sidera was an ancient city in the northern part of Pisidia, Anatolia, founded by Seleucus I Nicator or Antiochus I Soter in order to protect the military road across northern Pisidia.

name: Selinus
description: A Greek colonial city located on the southern coast of Sicily, Selinus was said to have been founded as a colony of the city of Megara Hyblaia.

name: Sembel
description: The Pre-Aksumite settlement of Sembel, located near Asmara in Ethiopia, dates ca. 800 BCE.

name: Sennabris/Ginnabris
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 69 C4 Sennabris/Ginnabris

name: Septempeda
description: Septempeda was an ancient settlement of Picenum. Its Roman phase began as a conciliabulum in the third century B.C.

name: Septizonium
description: The Septizonium (or Septizodium) is a monument located at the extreme southeastern corner of the Palatine Hill, built by the Severan emperors. It is attested to textually in the Historia Augusta, its footprint is visible on the Severan marble plan (Forma Urbis Romae), and its foundations have been explored through excavation.

name: Serapeum of Alexandria
description: The Serapeum in Alexandria was a temple to Serapis erected by Ptolemy III in the third century B.C. It was destroyed in A.D. 391.

name: Serapieion
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 74 G4 Serapieion

name: Serpent Column
description: An ancient bronze column that was displayed in the Hippodrome of Constantinople, the Serpent Column was part of a monument originally installed at Delphi and relocated to Constantinople by Constantine I in A.D. 324. The monument was originally built to celebrate the Greek defeat of the Persian Empire at the Battle of Plataea in 479 B.C.

name: Serra Lustrante
description: Writing in PECS, D. Adamesteanu notes that Serra Lustrante was found to be the site of a Hellenistic sanctuary.

name: Serra Maiori
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 46 D1 Serra Maiori

name: Serra Orrios
description: An important nuragic complex that includes remains of a megalithic megaron-type structure.

name: Serrone di Bove
description: The small hill that is known as "Il Poggio di Serrone di Bove" is located in the hinterland of ancient Norba and was the site of ancient settlement activity. Architectural remains on the site include those of a polygonal masonry sub-struction or platform as well as an ancient roadway that may be part of the road tract from Norba to Cora.

name: Sesekreienai? Inss.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 5 C3 Sesekreienai? Inss.

name: Settecamini
description: An archaeological site located at the midpoint of the Via Tiburtina linking Rome and Tibur.

name: Shadikanni/Ar(a)ban/(H)Oraba?/*Castellum Arabum
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 89 C4 Shadikanni/Ar(a)ban/(H)Oraba?/*Castellum Arabum

name: Shaduppum
description: Shaduppum was a small but heavily fortified city dating from the middle of the third millennium to the second millennium B.C.

name: Shah Tepe (Afghanistan)
description: Shah Tepe (Afghanistan) or Nayibabad is located in Samanghan Province northwest of Tashkurghan. This is an area of tepes that range in date from the Epi-paleolithic (seventh millennium B.C.) to the sixteenth century A.D.

name: Shahdād
description: Find place for cuneiform inscription.

name: Shahr-e Sukhteh
description: A Bronze Age urban settlement, associated with the Jiroft culture, located in Sistan and Baluchistan provinces in the southeastern part of Iran. The site was added to the UNESCO World Heritage List in 2014.

name: Shamīt
description: Find place for cuneiform inscription.

name: Shams el-Din
description: The ancient settlement of Shams el-Din in Egypt's Kharga Oasis, which contains a necropolis and 4th century AD church.

name: Shanidar Cave
description: Shanidar Cave is a cave located in the Zagros Mountains, within the Erbil Governorate of the Kurdistan Region in northern Iraq. The cave was the site of hominin activity beginning some 65,000 years ago. Following the first archaeological investigation of the cave in 1951, fieldwork has continued and, among other finds, has documented the remains of a dozen Neanderthals. There was proto-Neolithic activity in the cave, dated ca. 10,500 BCE and related to the Natufian culture. Some 35 individuals of this phase were buried in the cave.

name: Shaytanskoe Ozero II
description: Site on the western bank of Shaytanskoe Lake, Sverdlovsk Oblast, containing remains dated to the early Bronze Age.

name: Shephela
description: Find place for cuneiform inscription.

name: Shiqāft-i Gulgul
description: Assyrian rock cut relief.

name: Shir as-Sanam Inscription
description: A Neo-Babylonian rock relief of Nebuchadnezzar II was found in the area of the modern city Qubbayat, close to the Wadi Oustane and the Jebel Akroum. The monument at Shir as-Sanam is about 16 Km from Qubbayat, on the road to Chambouq and Hermel. It is about located ca. 120 meters from the road, on the western side, 15 m. above the ground.

name: Si(b)de
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 61 F3 Si(b)de

name: Šibaniba
description: A settlement mentioned in Neo-Assyrian sources, Šibaniba is identified with the modern site of Tell Billa in Iraq. Although excavated in the early 20th century, very little was published about the archaeology of the site (but see now Creamer 2021).

name: Siccae (islands)
description: Siccae Inss. (the Cíes Islands) an archipelago off the coast of Pontevedra, Spain. The archipelago includes three islands, Monteagudo ("Sharp Mount" or North Island), do Faro ("Lighthouse Island", or Illa do Medio, "Middle Island"), and San Martiño ("Saint Martin" or South Island).

name: Sidon/Col. Aurelia Pia
description: Sidon/Col. Aurelia Pia is an ancient maritime city and Phoenician metropolis

name: Siedlung Forschner
description: A Neolithic archaeological site located in Bad Buchau, Germany, and included in the UNESCO World Heritage Site "Prehistoric Pile Dwellings around the Alps".

name: Sigiriya
description: An archaeological site currently interpreted as the capital city of the parricidal King Kassapa I (A.D. 477–95). The site was listed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1982.

name: Sikait
description: Ancient Senskis, a large settlement in Egypt's Eastern desert on a branch tributary of Wadi Gemal (Wadi Sikait) with archaeological remains dating between the first and sixth centuries CE. The settlement was associated with the mining of emerald (i.e., green beryl) and is one of several in the region.

name: Sikani
description: A town mentioned in Neo-Assyrian sources that is generally identified with Tell Fekheriye (Tell Faḫārīya).

name: Silbury Hill
description: Silbury Hill is a prehistoric artificial chalk mound near Avebury and is part of the Stonehenge, Avebury and Associated Sites UNESCO World Heritage Site. The mound stands to 40 meters (131 ft) in height.

name: Sillian milestone
description: An uninscribed Roman milestone discovered in 1970 at Sillian, Tirol, Austria. The milestone corresponds to the 24th mile from Aguntum.

name: Sillyon
description: Sillyon was an ancient Graeco-Pamphylian fortress and city near Attaleia in Pamphylia, on the southern coast Asia Minor.

name: Silvium
description: Silvium (modern Gravina in Puglia) was founded as a Greek colony. It was a town on the frontier of the Peucetii.

name: Simiou?
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 80 D1 Simiou?

name: Simyra?
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 68 B4 Simyra?

name: Sindos
description: A tell settlement, located 20km west of Thessaloniki.

name: Singos
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 51 B4 Singos

name: Sippar
description: Sippar (or Zimbir) was an ancient city located on the east bank of the Euphrates river at the site of modern Tell Abu Habbah. In order to avoid confusing this site with its sister city of Sippar-Amnanum (Tell ed-Der), it is sometimes labeled as Sippar-Yahrurum.

name: Sippar-Amnanum
description: Sippar-Amnanum (modern Tell ed-Der; also referred to as Sippar-Annunītu) was a small city located 6 km northeast of Sippar and 63 km north of Babylon. The goddess Annunītu was the patron deity of this sister city of Sippar, and Eulmaš was its principal temple. An archive of ca. 2,000 cuneiform tablets dating the the reigns of several kings of the First Dynasty of Babylon was excavated in the house of Ur-Utu, the chief lamentation-priest of the goddess Annunītu. From several inscriptions of Nabonidus (r. 555–539 BC), Babylon’s last native king, certainly, Sippar-Amnanum (which he refers to as Sippar-Annunītu) was still occupied and its cult was still in use in the sixth century BC, although it had been (partially) destroyed by the seventh-century-BC Assyrian king Sennacherib (r. 704–681 BC).

name: Sirenes/Seirenoussai (islands)
description: Sirenes/Seirenoussai Inss. (modern Le Sirenuse or Li Galli) is an archipelago off the Amalfi Coast of Italy. In Greek mythology, the islands were home to the Sirens who tempted sailors, including Odysseus.

name: Sirmium
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 21 B5 Sirmium

name: Sirsukh
description: Sirsukh is a ruined urban center that forms part of the archaeological landscape at ancient Taxila (Punjab, Pakistan). This urban center may have been founded during the Kushan era at the end of the first century CE and represents the last of the ancient cities of Taxila.

name: Sisi, Kephali tou Agiou Antoniou
description: Kephali tou Agiou Antoniou is an archaeological site in Agios Nikolaos, located near Sisi on the north coast of Crete. Remains of monumental Minoan architecture have been documented there.

name: Sitifis
description: An ancient site at modern Sétif in Algeria, originally founded as a colonial settlement for Roman veterans during the reign of the emperor Nerva.

name: Skalia
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 61 D3 Skalia

name: Sklavokampos
description: A Late Minoan structure that first excavated in the 1930s. The Sklavokampos complex contained at least 17 rooms and yielded a range of evidence, including at least 39 sealings.

name: Slonta
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 38 C1 Slonta

name: so-called 'Midas Monument'
description: A rock-cut Phrygian monument dating to the seventh century BCE that carries an inscribed dedication in Old Phrygian by Ates, son of Arkias, to Midas.

name: So-called "Buildings A and B"
description: Site of masonry buildings in Aquae Sulis, modern-day Bath.

name: So-called "No.4 Abbeygate Street"
description: Remains of large building found in the settlement of Aquae Sulis, modern Bath.

name: So-called Arch of Hadrian (Capua)
description: The remains of a triple arch in opus latericium marking the pomerium of Capua. It is perhaps originally constructed under the Flavians.

name: So-called Arco di Druso
description: The popular toponym associated with this monument - Arco di Druso or Arch of Drusus - is a misnomer and is misleading. This is problematic given other textual, epigraphic, and archaeological attestations that mention honorific monuments dedicated to Nero Claudius Drusus. This place is the site of the remains of a triple-bay arch that is now reduced to the central bay. The monument sits astride the Via Appia just inside the Porta S. Sebastiano and has been referred to as "Arco di Druso" since the sixteenth century. The monument is built of travertine and faced with marble. This arch, in fact, supported the Aqua Antoniniana, a branch of the Aqua Marcia. The connection with Drusus is erroneous.

name: So-called Area L in Berenike
description: The ruins of an ancient building with a large peristyle, probably built around the second century AD, in the southwest area of the archaeological site of Sidi Khiribech, in Berenike (modern Banghazi).

name: So-called Palace of the Dux
description: A large building complex located on the western hill of Apollonia, probably built between the fifth and sixth centuries A.D. Its position and size, the presence of a chapel with a reliquary, and some public rooms led Goodchild to identify it as the residence of the Dux.

name: So-called Residential Insula in Apollonia
description: An architectural complex located on the West side of the East Church in Apollonia (Cyrenaica). Excavated in 1954-55, it was first thought to be a temple of Isis (Stucchi 1975). According to Kenrick (2013), it is more likely to have been a small production centre, maybe for the fermentation of wine.

name: So-called Roman Gymnasium at Syracuse
description: A monumental complex in the ancient city of Syracuse, probably built around the first century AD. It was initially identified as a gymnasium or a thermal bath; it includes the ruins of a theatre, a quadriporticus, and a temple.

name: So-called Tempio di Venere e Cupido
description: The so-called Temple of Venus and Cupid at Rome is part of the complex of buildings associated with the Sessorian palace. The structure's modern assignation is owed to the discovery of a fragmentary statue of Sallustia Orbiana in the guise of Venus Felix. Sallustia Orbiana was the wife of Alexander Severus. The statue is now held in the Museo Pio Clementino in the Vatican Museums.

name: So-called Temple of Hercules
description: A site on the Via Appia that is likely commercial in nature but that has long been associated with a shrine mentioned by Martial.

name: So-called West Gate of Apollonia
description: An unusual structure close to the water edge, on the western side of Apollonia, refurbished in Byzantine times. The presence of Maltese crosses led to its interpretation as a martyrion, but an administrative role has also been suggested.

name: Soknopaiou Nesos
description: An ancient settlement in the Egyptian Fayum, known today as Dime.

name: Soloi/Pompeiopolis
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 66 F3 Soloi/Pompeiopolis

name: Son Espases Roman Camp
description: This Roman camp, discovered in northern Palma, Mallorca, during construction of the Hospital de Son Espases, dates to Metellus' conquest of the island ca. 123-121 BCE. The visible remains on the north side of the hospital were consolidated and moved from the larger site, which is no longer visible.

name: Son Fornés
description: A prehistoric, Talaiotic settlement dating from the tenth to mid-seventh centuries B.C. on the island of Majorca.

name: Son Mercer de Baix
description: Son Mercer de Baix is a naviform settlement on the island of Menorca. These "boat-shaped" stone settlements belong to the prehistoric cultures of the Balearic Islands.

name: Sororium Tigillum
description: Sororium Tigillum describes a wooden beam that stretched across a branch of the Sacra Via that climbed the Oppian Hill near the Compitum Acilii. The monument was flanked by altars of Iuno Sororia and Iuno Curiatius. It is an ancient monument about which there are many stories, the primary one being a link to the Horatii and Curiatii and Publius Horatius' slaying of his own sister.

name: Spina
description: An Etruscan port city at the mouth of the Po River on the Adriatic Sea, most likely founded at the end of the sixth century B.C.

name: Sporades (archipelago)
description: An archipelago off the east coast of Greece in the Aegean Sea consisting of 24 islands.

name: Square Castan at Besançon
description: A square containing architectural remains of the Roman city.

name: Square Peristyle
description: The Square Peristyle is the modern name for a large square peristyle in the northeast corner of the Agora of Athens, begun around 300 BC, but never completed. It may have functioned as a lawcourt in the early Hellenistic period. It was demolished and used to build South Stoa II in the early 2nd century BC. The site was subsequently used for the Stoa of Attalos

name: Stabiae
description: An Oscan and Roman settlement located on the bay of Naples.

name: Stageira
description: An ancient city on the east coast of the Chalcidian peninsula. Birthplace of Aristotle.

name: Standing Stones of Stenness
description: The Standing Stones of Stenness is a Neolithic monument on the mainland of Orkney, Scotland, generally dated to the third millennium BC.

name: Starotitarovskaya 4
description: An archaeological site at Starotitarovskaya 4, Russia, is included as numbered feature 165 on the Barrington Atlas map 87 M2.

name: Statues of Harmodios and Aristogeiton
description: Statues of the tyrannicides Harmodios and Aristogeiton, the assassins of the tyrant Hipparchos and symbols of the Athenian democracy. An initial pair, made by Antenor were erected in the Athenian Agora ca. 510 BC, taken away to Susa by the Persians in 480 BC and returned by Alexander or one of his successors in the late fourth century BC. A replacement pair, made by Kritios and Nesiotes were erected in the Athenian Agora in 477 BC. With few exceptions it was forbidden to erect other statues in the vicinity. Part of the base of the second pair has been found, but not in situ.

name: Stele of Caius Vibius Quartus
description: The stele of a Roman military officer dating to the first century A.D. located at modern Dikilí Tas (Ντικιλί Τας), an archaeological site with evidence for both prehistoric and Byzantine occupation. The stele stands just to the east of modern Krenides, only 2km from the center of the ancient city of Philippi, which lies on the west side of the modern town.

name: Stele of Horemheb at Karnak
description: Horemheb was the last pharaoh of the 18th Dynasty of Egypt. The deeds of his career were recorded on a stele that was mountaed at the foot of the Tenth Pylon of the Karnak temple.

name: Stele Ponte di Apollo
description: A stele carrying a Latin inscription that dates to the Severan period (ca. 206 CE) recording the restoration of the bridge that had been destroyed by the "fury of the sea".

name: Stelida
description: Promontory South-West of the modern settlement of Naxos, where important remains of obsidian production were found (Paleolithic to LBA)

name: Stoechades (islands)
description: A group of four islands off Hyères, France, in the Mediterranean Sea.

name: Stone of the Pregnant Woman
description: The Stone of the Pregnant Woman in Baalbek, Lebanon is one of the largest monoliths ever quarried.

name: Stonehenge
description: An ancient formation of stones set in a field near Salisbury (Wiltshire County, England).

name: Strassen Roman Milestone
description: A Roman milestone for the twentieth mile from Aguntum was found at modern Strassen in Austria.

name: Strategeion
description: Palace located in block C9 at Dura-Europos.

name: Strophades/Plotai (island group)
description: The modern Strophades/Strofades islands (Stamfani and Arpi): an Ionian island group mentioned in Vergil and other ancient sources.

name: Strophilas
description: Ancient site on the promontory of Strophilas, Andros. Archaeological evidence of a town from Late Neolithic to Early Cycladic (4500-3300 BC).

name: Stroviki Helladic Settlement
description: A settlement mound immediately south of modern Stroviki (municipality of Orchomenos, Thessaly and Central Greece) has yielded archaeological evidence for activity from the Neolithic to the Classical period, with a significant floruit during the Helladic period when the site bordered the northern edge of Lake Copais. Evidence of Mycenean-era hydraulic works involved in the partial draining and control of the lake have been identified in the vicinity.

name: Stryme
description: Remains of a 7th-century walled settlement on the Molyvoti peninsula inhabited by Thasian colonists.

name: Su Nuraxi di Barumini
description: A nuragic site in Barumini, Sardinia that was named as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1997.

name: Sulaiymah
description: Large mound by the Diyala River, find place for a large number of cylinder seals.

name: Surkh Kotal
description: Surkh Kotal is an archaeological site of southern Bactria and one of the most significant sites connected with the Kushan civilization.

name: Surtiĭn dėnzh
description: Ritual complex in central Mongolia consisting of eight deer stone monuments surrounded by nine stone rings and several cairns.

name: Šuruppak
description: An ancient Sumerian city located about 35 miles south of Nippur on the banks of the Euphrates river. A place from the TAVO index.

name: Susa/Seleucia ad Eulaeum
description: An ancient city of the Elamite, Persian, and Parthian empires of Iran. Located at modern Shush, Susa was inscribed on the UNESCO World Heritage List in 2015.

name: Sybaris/Thurii/Copia
description: Sybaris was an ancient city of Magna Graecia founded as an Achaean colony as early as 720 BCE on a plain that was drained by both the Crati and Sybaris rivers. Greeks from Kroton destroyed Sybaris ca. 510 BCE. Following this destruction, two successive cities, Thurii and Copia, respectively, were established on the same site. This topic is of particular interest to ancient authors, at least 70 of whom mention it.

name: Sybota (islands)
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 54 B3 Sybota Inss.

name: Sybrita
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 60 C2 Sybrita

name: Sychem
description: An ancient settlement in the Levant, attested Biblically and in ancient Egyptian documents and the Amarna letters. Scholars associate this settlement with the archaeological site of Tel Balata, which is located in the modern West Bank and has seen attention from archaeologists since the early 20th century.

name: Syedra
description: A site in Cilicia Aspera near Demirtaş.

name: Sykeon Roman bath (Thessaly)
description: The remains of a Roman bath complex that included mosaic pavements and marble statuary were documented during rescue excavations in 2004 to the west of Sykeon in Thessaly.

name: Symplegades/Kyaneai (islands)
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 53 B2 Symplegades/Kyaneai Inss.

name: Tabernae Veteres
description: The so-called Tabernae Veteres were shops built in the valley of the Forum Romanum. The textual tradition assigns responsibility for this project to Lucius Tarquinius Priscus. The state owned the shops and leased them to tenants.

name: Tabriz: Iron Age Cemetery
description: In 1997, during work to restore and consolidate the "Blue Mosque" in modern Tabriz, Iran, an extensive Iron Age cemetery was discovered and excavated. Since 2007, the site has been partly open to the public as an open-air museum.

name: Tabula Traiana
description: The Tabula Traiana is a monumental inscription in what is now Serbia commemorating the completion of a Trajanic road along the Danube. The inscription was moved from its original spot to its present location in 1972.

name: Tadrart Acacus
description: The Acacus Mountains are known for rock-art dating from 12,000 BC to 100 AD and were inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1985 because of the importance of these paintings and carvings.

name: Takht-i Sangin
description: Takht-i Sangin is an ancient settlement located near the confluence of the Vakhsh and Panj rivers. It may be the source of the so-called "Oxus Treasure".

name: Tal-i Malyan/[Anzan]
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 94 C3 Tal-i Malyan/[Anzan]

name: Talati de Dalt
description: Talatí de Dalt is a Talaiotic site located on Menorca.

name: Talianki
description: Talianki or Talianky is an archaeological site of the Cucuteni-Trypillian culture located near the village of the same name in Cherkasy Oblast, Ukraine. The large settlement dates ca. 3850–3700 BCE.

name: Tall ‘Arbīd
description: Mound with remains from third millennium BCE.

name: Tall ‘Atij
description: Small settlement on the Khabur river inhabited during the Early Bronze Age (ca. 2900-2600 BCE). The site may have been located on an island during the period of its habitation.

name: Tall Abū al-Dhahab
description: Archaeological site in the marches of Dhi Qar where Iraqi excavations have revealed secular and religious buildings belonging to the First Sealand Dynasty 1740–1374 BCE including a number of objects bearing cuneiform inscriptions.

name: Tall Abū Ṣālabīkh
description: The find spot for a cuneiform inscription.

name: Tall Afghanī
description: Find place for cuneiform inscription.

name: Tall al-A‘shārnah
description: Find place for cuneiform inscription.

name: Tall al-Disim
description: Mound where cuneiform inscription has been found.

name: Tall al-Gharrān
description: Find place for cuneiform inscription: high mound with eroded brick walls, north of Babylon.

name: Tall al-Ḥadīdī
description: Bronze age site, possibly ancient Azu.

name: Tall al-Majātil
description: Find place for cuneiform inscription.

name: Tall al-Qiṭar
description: Late Bronze Age fortress-settlement, possibly ancient Til-Abni, find place for cuneiform inscription.

name: Tall al-Raqa‘i
description: Find place for cuneiform inscription.

name: Tall al-Sifr
description: Find place for cuneiform inscription.

name: Tall al-Umayri
description: An archaeological site of western Jordan that dates from the Early Bronze Age (ca. 3200–2100 BC) to the Hellenistic period (323–30 BC).

name: Tall Bint al-Amīr
description: Babylonian site where find of cuneiform inscription has been made.

name: Tall Dāmiyā
description: Iron Age site in the Central Jordan Valley, sometimes identified with the historical place Adama.

name: Tall Fisnah
description: Mound of a small rural settlement at the confluence of the river Robal with Tigris in modern Iraq. The site is now submerged by the reservoir created by the Mosul dam.

name: Tall Fray
description: A Bronze Age settlement mound, submerged since 1974 in Lake Assad.

name: Tall Gasir
description: Group of mounds in the Ram Hormuz Valley.

name: Tall Ghaḍaiyrīfah
description: Find spot (large mound) for an inscribed door socket.

name: Tall Ḥalāwah
description: Find place for cuneiform tablets.

name: Tall Hanas
description: Middle Assyrian Settlement.

name: Tall Ḥuwaysh
description: Find place for cuneiform inscription.

name: Tall Imlīḥiyah
description: A settlement mound now submerged in the Hamrin basin; a findspot for cuneiform inscriptions.

name: Tall Jūkha
description: Early dynastic settlement.

name: Tall Karay Qāsim
description: Small mound, periodically submerged in water, where cuneiform inscription has been found.

name: Tall Kozkhān
description: A settlement mound at which cuneiform brick inscriptions have been found.

name: Tall Marwāny
description: Mound in the Lower Middle Euphrates valley, find place for cuneiform inscription.

name: Tall Mīthlay
description: Minor mound at which a cuneiform tablet was found in a grave.

name: Tall Nūyaja
description: Mound and find place for cuneiform inscription.

name: Tall Qabr Abu al-’Atīq
description: Mound on the left bank of the Euphrates with Bronze Age settlement.

name: Tall S‘aīd
description: Find place for cuneiform inscription.

name: Tall Saka
description: Find place for cuneiform inscription.

name: Tall Shimshārah
description: Find place for cuneiform inscription.

name: Tall Siānū
description: Find place for cuneiform inscription.

name: Tall Sītak
description: Find place for cuneiform inscription.

name: Tall Umm al-ʻAjarib
description: Find place for cuneiform inscription.

name: Tall Yalkhī
description: Mound and find place for cuneiform inscription, partly submerged.

name: Tall Yārah
description: Mound and find place for cuneiform inscription.

name: Tall Zubīdī
description: Find place for cuneiform inscription.

name: Tamna
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 4 C2 Tamna

name: Tanagra
description: Tanagra was an ancient settlement of Boeotia.

name: Tang-i Var Rock Relief
description: The Tang-i Var rock relief of the eighth-century-BC Assyrian king Sargon II (r. 721–705 BC) is carved on a rock face of the Kūh-e Zīnāneh in the Tang-i Var pass in Iranian Kurdistan, about 50 km southwest of Sanandaj and 85 km northwest of Kermanshah. This difficult-to-reach relief was carved in a niche cut into the cliff face about 40 m above ground level. The Akkadian inscription, now badly weathered, records several of Sargon II’s campaigns, especially the military expeditions against the land Karalla, against which the Assyrian army campaigned in 716, 713, and 706 BC.

name: Tanis
description: An important settlement in the eastern Nile delta (modern San el-Hagar), Tanis boasted a massive temple precinct and was the burial site for Egyptian kings of the 21st and 22nd dynasties.

name: Tantalus Monu.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 56 F4 Tantalus Monu.

name: Tapah Barmī
description: Mound and find place for cuneiform inscription.

name: Tapah Daylam
description: Place where cuneiform inscription has been found.

name: Tapah Farukhābād
description: Small town mound in Deh Luran Plain.

name: Tapah Gārān
description: Tapah Gārān is an archaeological site located near Tappeh Qor’ān (Islamic Republic of Iran).

name: Tapah Gutwand
description: The findspot of an ancient brick with an Elamite inscription.

name: Tapah Khībr
description: Large mound and site for Iron Age fortress. Possibly ancient Ḫarḫar.

name: Tapah Mūsiyān
description: Find site for cuneiform inscription.

name: Taphiae? (islands)
description: A group of small islands in the Ionian Sea located between Leucas and the Acarnanian coast. The main island of this group can probably be identified with the modern island of Meganisi, which in the ancient sources is called Táphos.

name: Tapureli
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 66 D3 Tapureli

name: Taq-i Bustan
description: Site in western Iran with a series of Sassanid rock reliefs carved in the fourth century A.D.

name: Taq-i Girra
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 92 B2 Taq-i Girra

name: Tarasov Mound Site
description: Area with 17 Early Iron Age mounds located near the modern village Tarasovo and the Kama river in Russia's Udmurtia state. All mounds had settlements except Tarasov I, which was the location of a religious sanctuary and cemetery. The site has been partially destroyed by oil digging in the area.

name: Tarbiṣu
description: Tarbiṣu, now modern Sherif Khan, was an Assyrian city located about 5 kilometers north of Nineveh. It was the principal cult center of the god Nergal in Assyria. Starting in the 8th and 7th centuries B.C., Tarbiṣu was a royal city and one of the primary residences of the Assyrian crown prince, who was trained in the art of kingship in the palace built there.

name: Taricheiai Nesoi
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 33 H1 Taricheiai Nesoi

name: Tarsus/Antiochia ad Cydnum
description: An important city and river port on the Cydnus river located in Cilicia Pedias.

name: Tas-Silġ
description: A hilltop on Malta that is the locus of multi-period settlement and religious activity from the Neolithic period to late antiquity.

name: Tasciaca?
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 14 G1 Tasciaca?

name: Tauria (island)
description: The modern Habibas Islands, off the Algerian coast, are probably to be identified with the ancient Tauria island.

name: Tchogha Zanbil
description: An ancient Elamite complex founded ca. 1250 BC by the king Untash-Napirisha. Tchogha Zanbil was added to the UNESCO World Heritage List in 1979.

name: Teatro Marittimo
description: The so-called "Maritime Theater" at the Villa of Hadrian in Tivoli is sometimes referred to as the 'island villa'. It served as a kind of inner sanctum within the larger complex of the Villa Hadriana.

name: Tel Batash (Timnah)
description: Tel Batash, an archaeological site located in the Sorek Valley of modern Israel, lies northwest of Beit Shemesh. This site has been identified as the ancient Timnath or Timnah, a Philistine city in Canaan.

name: Tel Hazor
description: Tel Hazor (Hebrew: תל חצור‎‎) is an archaeological site located in the southern Hula Valley overlooking Lake Merom. From the Middle Bronze Age to the Israelite period in the ninth century B.C., Hazor was the largest fortified city in the region. It served as a commercial center with links to Babylon and Syria. In the Book of Joshua, Hazor is described as “the head of all those kingdoms” (Josh. 11:10).

name: Tel Kisson
description: Tel Kisson, also known as Tell Keisan, is an archaeological site located 8 km from the Mediterranean shoreline in Israel's Galilee region. Human occupation at the site of the tel begins in the Chalcolithic period. By the middle Bronze Age, the site was prosperous and fortified. It experiences possible destruction in the twelfth century BCE and recovers, though at a reduced scale. From the later eleventh century BCE, the site is occupied as perhaps an economic enclave of Phoenician Tyre.

name: Tell 'Ali
description: A small site on the left bank of the Lower Zab where an archive of ca. twenty-five Middle Assyrian texts was discovered. The town was situated more or less where travellers from Aššur to the cities Nuzi and Arrapha would have crossed the Lower Zab. Tell 'Ali is identified with ancient Atmannu.

name: Tell Abraq
description: Tell Abraq was an ancient Near Eastern city.

name: Tell Abu Marya/[Apqu]
description: Tell Abu Marya/[Apqu] was a settlement of western Assyria, the modern Tell Abu Marya.

name: Tell Abu Qubur
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 91 F4 Tell Abu Qubur

name: Tell Abu Salabikh
description: Tell Abu Salabikh was the site of a small Sumerian city of the third millennium BC.

name: Tell Abu Wajnam
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 89 E4 Tell Abu Wajnam

name: Tell Afis
description: Iron Age settlement in Syria. The site has been tentatively identified as Hadarikka/Hazrek, the capital of the region of Luhuti/Luash, which became part of the kingdom of Hamath around 796 BCE.

name: Tell al-Fakhar
description: Tell al-Fakhar is located in northeastern Iraq. Excavations between 1967 and 1969 two occupation phases dating to the Mitanni/Kassite and Neo-Assyrian periods.

name: Tell al-Hawa
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 89 E4 Tell al-Hawa

name: Tell al-Lahm
description: Tell al-Lahm is probably to be identified with Dur-Yakin, Duru(m), and/or Kissik, which are mentioned in Assyrian and Neo-Babylonian sources, and also in some first-millennium BCE texts together with Ur, Uruk, Larsa, Eridu, and Kullab (Uruk sanctuary).

name: Tell al-Mashhad
description: Archaeological site in Jordan, located approximately 10 km northwest of Madaba. The site shows signs of primary occupation during the Iron Age II (900-600 BCE).

name: Tell al-Ubaid
description: Tell al-'Ubaid is a small tell located west of Ur.

name: Tell Amarna
description: An ancient settlement, located just to the north of the modern village of ‘Amārinah (Jarabulus District, Aleppo Govenorate, Syria). Archaeological evidence at the site dates from the 6th millennium BCE to the early Byzantine period.

name: Tell Arpachiyah
description: A Neolithic site occupied in the Halaf and Ubaid periods.

name: Tell Baqrta
description: Tell Baqrta, an archaeological site located 28 km south of Erbil, may be the Neo-Assyrian town known as Baqarru.

name: Tell Baradan
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 91 G3 Tell Baradan

name: Tell Barri/[Kahat]
description: Tell Barri/[Kahat] is an archaeological site located along the Wadi Jaghjagh, a tributary of the Khabur River, in north-eastern Syria's Al-Hasakah Governorate. The ancient name of the site was Kahat. The site is occupied from the later Halaf period (late seventh to early sixth millennium BCE) to the Iron Age (first millennium BCE). The tell is substantial - at least 32 meters in height, covering an area of 37 hectares - and was accompanied by a lower town of some 7 hectares.

name: Tell Beydar
description: Tell Beydar (ancient Nabada) is an archaeological site in NE Syria that was first settled ca. 2600 BC.

name: Tell Brak
description: Tell Brak, located in the Upper Khabur of northeastern Syria, was occupied between the sixth and second millennia BCE. The tell stands ca. 40 m tall and covers ca. 130 hectares.

name: Tell Chuēra/Harbe
description: Tell Chuēra (also known as Tall Dahr ash Shuwayrah), a settlement in northern Syria inhabited from the fourth to third millennia BCE. A small settlement, called Harbe, was built on the site during the Middle Assyrian period.

name: Tell Djassa
description: Tell Djassa is an archaeological site in the upper Khabur River basin identified by salvage excavations in 1988-1990. Four main phases of human occupation were identified, all belonging to the third millennium BCE. The site was re-settled in the later first millennium BCE as demonstrated by Hellenistic pottery.

name: Tell el-Hesi
description: Archaeological site located in southern Palestine with signs of habitation (with gaps) from the late Neolithic through the Hellenistic period, as well as burials from Late Antiquity.

name: Tell el-Moqdam
description: An ancient settlement in Egypt, known as Leontopolis (one of two in the area) in Greco-Roman sources.

name: Tell el-Retaba
description: Tell el-Retaba is a large, multi-phase settlement site located in the central part of Wadi Tumilat. Its human activity ranges from the period of Hyksos settlers to the Third Intermediate Period to the 19th century.

name: Tell el-Yahudiya
description: An ancient settlement in Egypt, known as Leontopolis (one of two in the area) in Greco-Roman sources.

name: Tell en-Nasbeh
description: Tell en-Naṣbeh is an archaeological site ca. 12 km north of the Old City of Jerusalem. The site is often identified with the biblical Mizpah in Benjamin.

name: Tell er-Rameh
description: Tell er-Rameh is an ancient settlement mound located in the plain of the River Jordan, some 12 miles from Jericho.

name: Tell es-Samarat
description: A multi-period settlement site located in the Jericho Governorate, West Bank.

name: Tell es-Sawwan
description: A tell located along the Tigris River where human occupation began in the fifth millennium BCE. The site's material remains are chiefly associated with Ubaid, Hassuna, and Samarra cultures.

name: Tell es-Sweyhat
description: Tell es-Sweyhat or Sweyhat refers to an ancient settlement mound or tell located along the Euphrates river in what is now the Raqqa Governorate of northern Syria. This human settlement was occupied from the beginning of the third millennium BCE until its collapse in the early second millennium BCE. The site remained occupied during the Early Bronze-Middle Bronze age transition. There is later reoccupation of the site during the Hellenistic and late Roman periods.

name: Tell Faq'ous
description: Citadel on the modern banks of Lake Assad in Syria located near the Emar site and containing remains dated to the late Bronze Age.

name: Tell Fidain/Appadana?/Neapolis?
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 91 B2 Tell Fidain/Appadana?/Neapolis?

name: Tell Habuba Kabira
description: The northernmost of two associated settlements located on the western bank of the Euphrates, showing signs of habitation from the Late Uruk period through the Middle Bronze Age. Rescue excavations were conducted at the site before it was inundated by the Tabqa Dam Reservoir in the early 1970s.

name: Tell Hamidi/[Ta'idu]?
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 89 D4 Tell Hamidi/[Ta'idu]?

name: Tell Hammam
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 93 B2 Tell Hammam

name: Tell Hammam al-Turkmen
description: Tell Hammam al-Turkmen is an ancient settlement mound or tell located on the left bank of the Balikh river, approximately 80 km north of Raqqa, Syria. The tell has a diameter of 500 meters and stands 45 meters tall.

name: Tell Jidr/[Dabrum]?/Karkar?
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 93 B2 Tell Jidr/[Dabrum]?/Karkar?

name: Tell Khaiber
description: A tell in southern Mesopotamia (modern-day Iraq located 13 km kilometers west of Nasiriyah and 25 km south of the ancient city of Larsa.

name: Tell Khoshi
description: A tell located in Iraq's Sinjar region that was first noted by Seton Lloyd in the mid-1930s. Lloyd conducted limited excavations. Additional fieldwork, including both survey and excavation, took place in the late twentieth and early twenty-first centuries. The site's life began in the middle of the third millennium BCE and continued into the second.

name: Tell Leilan
description: Tell Leilan is situated near the Wadi Jarrah, in the Khabur River basin in Al-Hasakah Governorate, northeastern Syria. The ancient names of the site are Shekhna and Shubat-Enlil.

name: Tell Mohammad Diyab
description: An archaeological site in northeastern Syria inhabited in the Early Dynastic through post-Akkadian period (ca. 2750-1800 BCE).

name: Tell Mohammed Arab
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 89 E4 Tell Mohammed Arab

name: Tell Qaramel
description: A settlement mound with evidence of human activity stretching from the Pre-Pottery Neolithic A period to the Hellenistic period.

name: Tell Sabi Abyad
description: Tell Sabi Abyad is located in the Balih River Valley, in northern Syria. The ancient name of the site is not yet known.

name: Tell Sukas
description: The ancient Shuksi or Suksi, the archaeological site of Tell Sukas is a Late Bronze Age tell located south of Jableh, Syria. The site was situated at the center of the fertile plain of Jableh and had access to two natural harbors.

name: Tell Taban/[Tabite]/Thebet(h)a?
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 89 C4 Tell Taban/[Tabite]/Thebet(h)a?

name: Tell Tweini
description: Tell Tweini is an archaeological site located near Jableh, Syria. It was inhabited from the third millennium BC to the Persian period.

name: Tell Umm Amer
description: The archaeological site of Tell Umm Amer in the Gaza Strip is included on the Madaba Map under the name "Tabatha". The site was the birthplace in 291 CE of Saint Hilarion who founded an eponymous monastery there. The monastery was destroyed in 614.

name: Tell Uqair
description: The archaeological site of Tell Uqair, ancient name unknown, is noted for its Uruk period architecture. This includes a small tripartite temple that stands on an irregular platform.

name: Tell Wilaya
description: Tell Wilaya is a small site that was occupied from the Jemdet Nasr Period to the Old Babylonian Period. Its ancient name is not known, but it may have been Keš.

name: Tell Yelkhi
description: Tell Yelkhi is an archaeological site located in Iraq's Diyala Governorate. The site was inundated by the Hamrin Dam project, although salvage excavations were carried out. The site was first settled in the early third millennium BCE and continued through the Kassite period in the late second millennium BCE.

name: Temenos of Athena
description: The area on the northeast side of the Piana di Himera is delimited by walls and contains four religious buildings (temples A, B, C, and D), probably dedicated to Athena. The foundations are still visible in the archaeological site of Himera.

name: Tempio di Venere, Baiae
description: The so-called "Tempio di Venere" is likely part of a bathing complex dating to the reign of Hadrian. The surviving structure has an octagonal plan.

name: Temple of Minerva Medica
description: The so-called Temple of Minerva Medica is, in fact, a nymphaeum located along the line of the Anio Vetus aqueduct; it most likely dates to the fourth century AD.

name: Temple of Olympian Zeus, Athens
description: The Temple of Olympian Zeus is also known as the Olympieion. A colossal temple dedicated to Zeus, it was built in three stages, beginning in 515 BCE and ending in 131 CE.

name: Temple of Tellus (Thugga)
description: The Temple of Tellus (Thugga) dates to the reign of Gallienus ca. 261 CE.

name: Temple of the Fabri Navales
description: The Temple of the Ship Carpenters at Ostia dates to the later second century CE.

name: Templum Pacis
description: The Temple of Peace in Rome was vowed by Vespasian following the Jewish War.

name: Teniky
description: An archaeological site in the modern Parc National de l'Isalo in southern Madagascar. The site comprises rock-cut terraces, niches, walls, and other features in a style unique in the region. Radiocarbon dating of charcoal and stylistic dating of imported ceramics place activity at the site to a period spanning the 10th - 12th centuries CE. The excavators tentatively interpret the site as part of a former necropolis made by settlers with Zoroastrian origins.

name: Tenuta della Lucchina
description: An archaeological area in the suburbium of ancient Rome, now in the quartiere Ottavia, with multi-phase evidence for tombs of the Etruscan culture, a segment of a Roman road, and evidence for Roman villas.

name: Tepe Gawra
description: An ancient Mesopotamian settlement located in the Mosul region of northwest Iraq.

name: Tepe Ghabristan
description: Settlement in the Qazvin Plain intermittently inhabited from the 5th-1st millennia BCE; the westernmost of the three Bronze Age tells comprising the Sagzabad Cluster.

name: Tepe Hissar
description: Tepe Hissar, the site of an ancient settlement near Damghan in north-eastern Iran.

name: Tepe Horreeye
description: Place with archaeological remains where Elamite inscription has been found.

name: Tepe Sagzabad
description: Tell site in the Qazvin Plain inhabited from the Neolithic through the Achaemenid periods; the central tell comprising the Sagzabad Cluster.

name: Tepe Yahya
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 3 G4 Tepe Yahya

name: Tepe Zagheh
description: Chalcolithic settlement in the Qazvin Plain, Iran, occupied in the 6th-5th millennia BCE; the easternmost mound comprising the Sagzabad Cluster.

name: Tepeköy Höyük
description: Settlement in eastern Turkey containing signs of inhabitation dated to the Early Bronze Age. Rescue excavations were conducted at the site prior to its inundation by the Alpaslan-2 Reservoir in 2021.

name: Termes
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 25 B4 Termes

name: Termessos
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 65 D4 Termessos

name: Terpni/Paliokastro
description: A hilltop archaeological site (Paliokastro) located approximately 3km west southwest of the modern Greek town of Terpni (Serres regional unit). Its ancient name is uncertain; some scholars have suggested it be identified with "Graero," which is named on the Peutinger map. Excavations have produced evidence for habitation as early as the 6th century BCE, remains of a Roman-era spa and basilica, and continued (re)use into the 6th century CE.

name: Terravecchia di Grammichele
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 47 F4 Terravecchia di Grammichele

name: Tetrapylon at Aphrodisias
description: The monumental columnar entrance to the sanctuary of Aphrodite

name: Tha'anach
description: Tell site at the northern edge of the Nablus Range inhabited from the Iron Age to the present.

name: Thaenae
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 33 G3 Thaenae

name: Thaima
description: Thaima or Tayma is an oasis with a long history of human settlement.

name: Thamna
