description: An Early Christian Basilica located near the grove of Kraneion that dates to the sixth century CE.

name: Kursi paleochristian basilica
description: An early Christian basilica was excavated at Kursi, near the Sea of Galilee in the Golan Heights.

name: La Temple de la Madeleine, Geneva
description: The oldest Christian church in the city of Geneva, the Temple de la Madeleine site has been in continuous use as a church since the 5th century. The current structure on the site dates to the 15th century.

name: Labraunda
description: Labraunda is an archaeological site located in Caria, just west of Ortaköy, Turkey. The site was sacred to both Carians and Mysians.

name: Lateran Basilica, Palace, and Castra Nova equitum singularium
description: Site of a basilica and palace from early Roman imperial times, as well as an military installation built by Septimius Severus for the Roman imperial cavalry bodyguard. Now the site of the Archbasilica of St. John Lateran (rebuilt in the 16th century): Complesso dei San Giovanni in Laterano (Basilica, Palazzo Apostolico Lateranense, edifici annessi) Holy See; listed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1990.

name: Latomos Monastery
description: A late fifth century AD church, later associated with a small monastery in Thessaloniki, Greece.

name: Laubendorf
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 19 F3 Laubendorf

name: Lehun
description: An ancient site located on the northern plateau of the Wadi Mujib in modern Jordan. Under excavation since the 1970s, the site has so far provided evidence for human activity from the prehistoric period through the Islamic.

name: Limani Ozia
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 54 B3 Limani Ozia

name: Little Hagia Sophia
description: former Greek Eastern Orthodox church in Istanbul converted to a mosque

name: Little Metropolis church (Athens)
description: The Little Metropolis church or Panagia Gorgoepikoos is a Frankish, Late Byzantine, or Ottoman-period domed cross-in-square church in central Athens. It is decorated with spolia from earlier structures.

name: Locoteiacus
description: A Benedictine abbey that is located 7 km south of Poitiers and was founded by Martin from 361 to 371.

name: London Roman basilica
description: A civic basilica in ancient Londonium, the remains of which the Museum of London archaeology service (MOLA) has documented at a site located at 85 Gracechurch Street, London. The site is dated ca. 70 CE.

name: Loutra Hypatis
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 55 C3 Loutra Hypatis

name: Lower Church at Banganarti
description: Church dating to the 7th century CE in Nubia that was succeeded by St. Raphael Church II. The site is located in the modern Sudanese village of Banganarti.

name: Luxeuil Abbey
description: Located in France's Burgundy region, the Luxeuil Abbey was founded ca. 585–590 by the Irish missionary Saint Columbanus.

name: Lysi
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 72 D2 Lysi

name: Lysimacheia
description: An ancient city in Aetolia, located 3km south of the modern city that now bears its name.

name: Madonna del Parto
description: Madonna del Parto is a Christian church at Sutri, dating to the thirteenth and fourteenth centuries. The rock-cut sanctuary repurposes a Mithraeum dating to the first and second centuries.

name: Madonnna del Canneto
description: The sanctuary site of Madonnna del Canneto is located at Settefrati within the confines of the Abruzzo national park in Italy. It has a long history as a sacred site, beginning with sacred activities dedicated to Mefitis in the later first millennium BCE.

name: Magnetum?
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 24 C3 Magnetum?

name: Makaravank'
description: Medieval Armenian church and monastic complex dated to the 9th-13th centuries.

name: Mănăstirea Halmyris
description: An Early Christian basilica and martyrium at Halmyris.

name: Mar Abay
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 89 C3 Mar Abay

name: Mar Abrohom
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 89 D3 Mar Abrohom

name: Mar Awgin
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 89 D3 Mar Awgin

name: Mar Matta
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 89 F4 Mar Matta

name: Mar Melki
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 89 D3 Mar Melki

name: Mar Yaqub
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 67 H2 Mar Yaqub

name: Mar Yohannon
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 89 D3 Mar Yohannon

name: Marcianum
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 44 F4 Marcianum

name: Marcianus Fundus
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 44 G4 Marcianus Fundus

name: Marialba Paleochristian Basilica
description: Marialba Paleochristian Basilica dates to the fourth and fifth centuries.

name: Marimbault Roman Villa
description: The 12th-century Eglise Saint-Vincent de Marimbault was built atop a probable Gallo-Roman villa.

name: Martyrion at Aphrodisias
description: The Roman Tetrakionion at Aphrodisias has been identified as a Martyrion. Its date of construction is uncertain, ranging from as early as the late second century CE to as late as the 5th-6th century CE.

name: Martyrios
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 70 G2 Martyrios

name: Mausoleum of Diocletian
description: A mausoleum built to entomb Diocletian at his retirement palace that was constructed around 295 CE and is located in modern day Split, Croatia.

name: Mausoleum of Galla Placidia
description: An ancient funerary chapel, part of the Santa Croce complex in Ravenna, Italy, possibly built as an imperial mausoleum (ca. AD 417 - 425). The empress Galla Placidia (d. AD 450 ) is the presumed, likely patron of the building, though she is very unlikely to have been entombed within one of three sarcophagi found inside.

name: Mausoleum of Theodoric
description: A monument built in Ravenna, Italy, ca. AD 520 by Theodoric the Great as his future tomb. After Belisarius reconquered Ravenna in 540, the tomb was converted into the church of Sta. Maria della Rotonda.

name: Megiste Laura Saba
description: An Eastern Orthodox Christian monastery overlooking the Kidron Valley located between the Old City of Jerusalem and the Dead Sea.

name: Meroth
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 69 C3 Meroth

name: Messon
description: An extensive ancient sanctuary complex on the island of Lesbos, thought to have been dedicated to Zeus, Hera and Dionysos and dating back to at least the 7th century BCE. A Christian basilica was built on the site in the late fourth century CE.

name: Metanoia Mon.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 53 B2 Metanoia Mon.

name: Metapa
description: According to Polybius, this ancient settlement of Aetolia was razed by Philip of Macedon. Stephanus of Byzantium places Metapa in Acarnania. Pritchett (SAGT) meticulously describes the vicinity and identifies the site of Metapa based on ancient stone blocks in conditions of reuse and abandonment, as well as plentiful ceramic sherds in the vicinity of an older and modern church of the Analipsis on the lake shore just east of the modern village of Daphnias.

name: Molzbichl
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 19 F3 Molzbichl

name: Monastery of Saint John the Theologian
description: The Monastery of Saint John the Theologian (also known as the Monastery of Saint John the Divine) is a Greek Orthodox monastery dating to AD 1088 on the island of Patmos.

name: Monastery of Saint Simeon Stylites
description: The monastery of Saint Simeon Stylites, located ca. 30 km northwest of Aleppo, Syria, is one of the oldest surviving Byzantine churches and dates to the fifth century. The church is built on the site of the pillar of St. Simeon Stylites.

name: Monte Monaco di Gioia
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 44 F3 Monte Monaco di Gioia

name: Montebuono
description: The remains of a villa were excavated near the church of San Pietro ad centum muros in the nineteenth century. Based on epigraphic evidence, the site is connected with M. Vipsanius Agrippa.

name: Monteverde
description: The eleventh century monastery of Santa Maria di Monteverde sits atop and reuses elements of Roman and pre-Roman settlements on the hilltop.

name: Morulls
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 25 F4 Morulls

name: Musaifna
description: Excavations at Tell Museifneh (Eski Mosul) in the 1980s revealed remains of a well-built stone building that was identified as a Christian basilica dating to the seventh century A.D. The apisdal basilica measured ca. 50 x 50 meters.

name: Mushabbak Basilica
description: The remains of a well-preserved Byzantine church (ca. 470) located five km west of the Monastery of St Simeon. The ruins lie near the town of Daret A'zzeh.

name: Mygdaleza basilica
description: The remains of an Early Christian basilica dating to the fifth and sixth centuries.

name: Narbek
description: A rock-cut church located about 5km west of the modern town of Omar Mukhtar in Libya.

name: Nea Moni
description: An eleventh-century monastery on Chios built by Constantine IX Monomachos. The site was listed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1990.

name: Nebo Mons (church)
description: Site of a modern church and monastery atop Mount Nebo in modern Jordan on a site commemorated by earlier churches built on a site traditionally held to be the burial location of the prophet Moses. The first ancient church here is mentioned earliest in the itinerary of Egeria.

name: Neklone
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 75 D2 Neklone

name: Nemea Early Christian basilica
description: An Early Christian basilica located southeast of ancient Nemea.

name: Nessana
description: Nessana is an ancient site located in the Negev Desert. Established during the Hellenistic period, Nessana has numerous occupational phases including as the site of a Roman military garrison in the fourth century CE. The site flourishes in connection with the Christian pilgrimage route linking the Holy Land to Mount Sinai (sixth and seventh centuries CE).

name: New Basilica of Saint Apollinaris
description: A basilica in Ravenna, Italy, erected by Theodoric the Great in the first quarter of the sixth century AD.

name: Nike (Monument at Marathon)
description: A monument to the Battle of Marathon. Eugene Vanderpool describes the location of the monument as lying in the northeastern part of the plain of Marathon. A Christian chapel known as the Panagia Mesosporitissa marks the vicinity of this monument. In the area of the chapel are a well that reuses ancient marble and the ruins of a medieval tower that was built from re-used ancient blocks, including a massive Ionic column capital and two large column drums.

name: Northern Cluster
description: The Northern Cluster of rock-cut churches at Lalibela.

name: Nostra Segnora de Mesumundu
description: Nostra Segnora de Mesumundu ("Our Lady of Mesumundu") is located in the territory of Siligo, Sardinia, Italy. This archaeological site preserves remains of multiple architectural phases. A mid-Imperial (likely second-century CE) Roman complex, which included baths, was reused, in part, for the construction of a Christian church in the sixth century.

name: Oberlienz
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 19 E3 Oberlienz

name: Onia
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 14 G1 Onia

name: Orbaniacum
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 14 G1 Orbaniacum

name: Örendibi
description: Modern name for ruins of a Byzantine church and surrounding rural settlement in eastern Rough Cilicia

name: Ostia Synagogue
description: The ancient synagogue located in Ostia Antica is one of the oldest known synagogues in the world. The structure's earliest phase dates to the reign of Claudius and it continued in use until the fifth century.

name: Pagai
description: A fortified port located on the Gulf of Corinth near Alepokhori.

name: Palaiomonastero
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 61 D2 Palaiomonastero

name: Paleochristian basilica
description: A Paleochristian basilica was discovered at the eighth mile of the Via Tiburtina in 2003. The subterranean basilica reused an earlier quarry and may be related to San Sinforosa. The site dates from the late fourth to the middle of the fifth century CE.

name: Paleochristian Basilica
description: The Paleochristian Basilica in the Villa Gordiani Park is located adjacent to the mausoleum and immediately post-dates the tomb.

name: Paleochristian basilica at Vravrona
description: An early Christian basilica located west of ancient Brauron in Attica.

name: paleochristian basilica of Lugdunum Convenarum
description: The paleochristian basilica of Saint-Bertrand-de-Comminges.

name: Paleochristian basilica, Illa del Rei
description: The Paleochristian basilica located on Illa del Rei dates to the sixth century.

name: Paleochristian Church, Lin
description: A paleochristian church located in Lin, Korçë County, Albania, with fourth- and fifth-century mosaics.

name: Pampligua
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 24 H2 Pampligua

name: Panagia Angeloktistos
description: The church of Panagia tis Angeloktistis or Panagia Angeloktisti is a Byzantine church located in the village of Kiti, Cyprus. Its origins lies in the fifth and sixth centuries. It was listed as a tentative UNESCO World Heritage site in 2015.

name: Panagia Ekatontapiliani
description: Panagia Ekatontapiliani or Panagia Katapoliani is an important Early Christian church complex in Parikia town, on the island of Paros, Greece, that was founded in 326 CE.

name: Panagia tis Kyras
description: A twelfth-century CE Greek Orthodox church located north of modern Livadia on Cyprus' Karpasia peninsula. Its apse had been adapted from part of an older building and contained a 7th-century mosaic of the Theotokos (i.e., Mary, mother of Jesus).

name: Pantheon
description: A temple in the Campus Martius of Rome that was first built by Marcus Agrippa (ca. 27-25 B.C.) and then re-built by the emperor Hadrian (after A.D. 126). In A.D. 609 the structure was consecrated as a Christian church, S. Maria ad Martyres.

name: Paphor, Mon.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 77 E2 Paphor, Mon.

name: Parco delle Tombe di via Latina
description: A cluster of Roman Imperial tombs along the Via Latina excavated in the nineteenth century.

name: Patriarchal Basilica of Santa Maria Assunta of Aquileia
description: The Basilica di Santa Maria Assunta, Aquileia dates originally to the fourth century.

name: Patriasdorf
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 19 E3 Patriasdorf

name: Peroushtitsa
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 22 B6 Peroushtitsa

name: Petrera
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 72 D2 Petrera

name: Petrera
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 72 C3 Petrera

name: Pfaffenhofen
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 19 D2 Pfaffenhofen

name: Philosophiana
description: An ancient settlement not far from Piazza Armerina, in the municipality of Mazzarino. Archaeological finds suggest it was inhabited from the early Roman Imperial period to the third century A.D., when it was abandoned. The site was then re-occupied during the Middle Ages.

name: Phlabianai
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 64 B3 Phlabianai

name: Pianabella
description: A large Christian basilica discovered in 1976 several hundred meters southeast of the Porta Laurentina of Ostia. The original phase dates to the end of the fourth century A.D.

name: Pieve di San Giovanni Battista a Sillano
description: A ruined church that was once part of the diocese of Volterra, San Giovanni Battista a Sillano is unique in Tuscany for having a Romanesque-Norman façade. The ruined edifice likely dates to the twelfth and thirteenth centuries although the parish exists as early as the mid-tenth century.

name: Pieve di San Pietro ad Mensulas
description: A Romanesque church located in the Italian comune of Sinalunga (Siena). Saint Donatus of Arezzo may have founded the church in the fourth century.

name: Pieve Socana
description: Pieve di Sant'Antonino a Socana, a Medieval parish church, is built atop the remains of an Etruscan sanctuary.

name: Pirdop
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 22 B6 Pirdop

name: Pompeiacum/Vernemetis
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 14 F4 Pompeiacum/Vernemetis

name: Porta Maggiore Basilica
description: The so-called underground basilica near Rome's Porta Maggiore is situated 12 meters below ground level. This vaulted space with a triple nave design is decorated with an elaborate stucco work program. The "basilica" was discovered accidentally in 1917. The site has been dated to the first century BCE and is likely linked to the activities of the neo-Pythagoreans.

name: Prophetes Elias Troullakiou
description: An ancient settlement on the island of Siphnos. The current church sits on Byzantine foundations and is surrounded by remains of older fortifications and other structures.

name: Ptghnavank
description: Church building in the modern village of Ptghni, Kotayk province, Armenia, dated to the 6th-7th centuries CE.

name: Ptoou Nkalamon
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 75 D3 Ptoou Nkalamon

name: Qal’at Sem’an/Church of Saint Simeon Stylites
description: An ancient church, located in modern Syria. Inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 2011 as part of the "Ancient Villages of Northern Syria."

name: Qalat el-Baben
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 80 B4 Qalat el-Baben

name: Qalb Lozé
description: An ancient place, located in modern Syria, with remains of an ancient basilica. Inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 2011 as part of the "Ancient Villages of Northern Syria."

name: Qalbloze
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 67 D4 Qalbloze

name: Qasr Libya, West Church
description: At Qasr Libya, the ancient Theodorias, two churhes have been identified. The Byzantine settlement was refounded under Justinian in 539 CE.

name: Qasr Serij
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 89 E4 Qasr Serij

name: Qenneshre
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 67 G3 Qenneshre

name: Ras Abu el Daraq
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 76 B3 Ras Abu el Daraq

name: Reccopolis
description: Reccopolis is an urban, Visigothic, ex-novo foundation made under king Liuvigild in A.D. 578. The archaeological remains at the site include a palace complex, public area, basilica, walls, and aqueduct. The site is known today by the Spanish version of its Latin name (Recópolis); it is located near the modern village of Zorita de los Canes in the Spanish province of Guadelajara.

name: Red Basilica
description: A Roman temple built above the Selinus river was originally constructed to worship Egyptian gods, most likely Isis and/or Serapis. In the Byzantine era, it was converted into a church of St. John. It was subsequently ruined, although the rotunda on the north side is still an active mosque.

name: Rephidim
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 76 D4 Rephidim

name: Riva S. Vitale
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 39 D2 Riva S. Vitale

name: Roman forum of Iulium Carnicum
description: The Roman forum and civic basilica at Iulium Carnicum (modern Zuglio)

name: S. Agapito
description: S. Agapito, the Palestrina Cathedral also known as the Cattedrale di Sant'Agapito martire, was built atop the ruins of Praeneste's civic forum and its temple. The relics of Saint Agapitus were transferred to this location in 898. The present basilica was consecrated on 16 December 1117 by Pope Pasquale I.

name: S. Agnese
description: A titular church on the Via Nomentana, S. Agnese houses the remains of the eponymous saint. The present church was built by Pope Honorius I in the seventh century AD, on the site of a fourth century basilica built by Constantine I.

name: S. Alessandro
description: The basilica and catacombs of S. Alessandro are located at the seventh milestone of the Via Nomentana.

name: S. Autonomos, Mon.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 52 F3 S. Autonomos, Mon.

name: S. Benedictus
description: A monastery originally established by St. Benedict of Nursia in A.D. 529/530.

name: S. Clemens
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 43 E2 S. Clemens

name: S. Cornelia, Mon.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 44 B1 S. Cornelia, Mon.

name: S. Cugat
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 25 H4 S. Cugat

name: S. Desideratus, Mon.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 18 B3 S. Desideratus, Mon.

name: S. Giovanni Lippioni
description: S. Giovanni Lippioni is the site of a pre-Roman sanctuary that was later repurposed as a Christian church.

name: S. Laurentius
description: The Papal Basilica of Saint Lawrence outside the Walls (Basilica Papale di San Lorenzo fuori le Mura) is one of the Seven Pilgrim Churches of Rome. The basilica serves as the shrine and tomb dedicated to Saint Lawrence and its earliest phases likely belong to the fourth century AD.

name: S. Maria d’Artès
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 25 G4 S. Maria d’Artès

name: S. Maria di Galeria
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 43 B1 S. Maria di Galeria

name: S. Maria di Minerbe
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 39 H2 S. Maria di Minerbe

name: S. Martinus
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 44 E3 S. Martinus

name: S. Pantaleón
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 25 B3 S. Pantaleón

name: S. Passera
description: S. Passera is a Christian church on the Tiber river in Rome, opposite the Basilica of Saint Paul Outside the Walls. The current church dates to the ninth century and incorporated the remains of a Roman tomb. The church is traditionally connected to a community of local miners.

name: S. Paulus
description: One of Rome's four major papal basilicae, S. Paulus was founded by Constantine I in the early fourth century A.D. and expanded by Valentinian I in the 370s. The basilica, with many renovations, repairs, and improvements, stood until the 19th century, when it was destroyed in a fire. The reconstructed basilica only generally resembles the original structure.

name: S. Petrus
description: The Basilica of St. Peter in Vatican City is currently a Renaissance edifice standing on the site of Old St. Peter's Basilica, a church built by Constantine I between AD 319 and 333. The holy site is located on the Mons Vaticanus in connection with the purported site of the burial of the Christian Saint Peter.

name: S. Pietro ad centum muros
description: On the hill of Grignano, located on the outskirts of Montebuono, the remains of a Roman villa were first excavated in the nineteenth century in the area of the church of "S. Pietro ad centum muros". Excavation in the 1990s revealed further villa remains beneath the church and in the vicinity of its cemetery. Epigraphic evidence attesting to the consulship of M. Vipsanius Agrippa was discovered immured in the church (CIL 9.4677 and 9.4779).

name: S. Porphyrius
description: The oldest Orthodox church in Gaza, sacred to the fifth century Saint Porphyrius whose tomb lies within the church.

name: S. Quiricus, Mon.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 14 I3 S. Quiricus, Mon.

name: S. Scholastica
description: A convent founded within the area of the Villa Neronis.

name: S. Sebastianus
description: S. Sebastianus (San Sebastiano fuori le mura) is a Christian basilica outside the walls of Rome; the earliest phase belongs to the fourth century AD.

name: S. Sinforosa
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 43 C2 S. Sinforosa

name: S. Thekla, Mon.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 66 D4 S. Thekla, Mon.

name: S. Theodora
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 53 A2 S. Theodora

name: S. Victoriano
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 25 F3 S. Victoriano

name: S. Zion, Mon./Pharroa
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 65 C5 S. Zion, Mon./Pharroa

name: Sabiona
description: A seventeenth century Benedictine nunnery located on a site that shows occupational evidence stretching back to the Neolithic period and that includes substantial Roman and Late Antique phases.

name: Sacro Speco
description: The Monastery of Saint Benedict or Monastero di San Benedetto Sacro Speco. The Shrine of the Sacred Cave of St. Benedict (Sacro Speco di San Benedetto) at Subiaco is a significant monastic shrine connected to the Roman Catholic saint Benedict. At the site is found a fresco-adorned cave linked to the saint, dating to the eighth century.

name: Saint Gayane Church
description: A seventh century AD church located in Vagharshapat (Etchmiadzin), Armenia and built by Catholicos Ezra I. The church was listed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 2000.

name: Saint Hripsime Church
description: A fourth-century AD church was rebuilt in the seventh-century AD to house the remains of the eponymous saint. The church is located in the city of Vagharshapat (Etchmiadzin), in the Armavir Province of Armenia. Listed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 2000.

name: Saint-Pierre-d'Aurillac
description: Remains of Gallo-Roman substructures that support the choir of the present church.

name: San Crisogono
description: San Crisogono (sacred to San Crisogono) is one of Rome's early parish churches (tituli). Pope Sylvester I (314–335) likely builds the first iteration in the fourth century AD; later reworkings occur in the twelfth and seventeenth centuries, respectively.

name: San Giacomo di Rialto
description: A church in Venice, Italy. San Giacomo di Rialto is claimed to be the oldest church in the city, with AD 421 as the traditional date of its consecration. The first documented attestations of the church belong to AD 1152.

name: San Giorgio in Velabro
description: A church dedicated to St. George located in Rome's Velabrum. Legend connects the church's location with the spot where the Roman she wolf found the abandoned twins, Romulus and Remus.

name: San Giovanni de Butris
description: A church of the eleventh and twelfth century built along the line of the Roman Via Flaminia. The church incorporates Roman remains, including those of a bridge.

name: San Giovanni in Oleo
description: A sixteenth-century chapel near the Porta Latina that is believed to commemorate the location where the Christian apostle John was reportedly unharmed after being immersed in a vat of boiling oil in 92 CE.

name: San Marcello al Corso
description: The church of San Marcello al Corso is located adjacent to the Via del Corso (the ancient Via Lata) in Rome. Originally known as the Titulus Marcelli, it was established by Pope Marcellus I before 418 CE. The current edifice was completed in the seventeenth century following a destruction by fire of the earlier church in 1519.

name: San Martino ai Monti
description: San Martino ai Monti is a minor basilica in Rome. The basilica was founded on a site donated by Equitius during the reign of Pope St. Sylvester I in the fourth century.

name: San Menna
description: The church of San Menna in Rome was recorded by the anonymous pilgrim author of the Einsiedeln in the eighth century CE.  It was located along the Via Ostiensis, most likely between the Porta San Paolo and the Basilica of Saint Paul. Likely founded in 589, the church is not attested after the tenth century.

name: San Nicola di Trullas
description: A Medieval church on the island of Sardinia that is dated to 1133.

name: San Nicola in Carcere
description: San Nicola in Carcere is a titular church in Rome's historical district (rione) Sant'Angelo, close to the Forum Boarium. The twelfth-century church is built atop the remains of Roman temples of the ancient Forum Holitorium and incorporates their remains into its structure.

name: San Paolo alla Regola
description: The Catholic church of San Paolo alla Regola located in the modern Rione of Rome named "Regola", to which it gives its name. The current edifice of the church dates to the seventeenth century. Excavations carried out from 1978 to 1982 documented multiple layers of architecture dating from the imperial period when this district was used primarily as one of warehouses (horrea) located near the Tiber River.

name: San Pietro a Badia Pozzeveri
description: An abbey with tenth century origins connected to the pilgrimage route known as the Via Francigena.

name: San Pietro ad Oratorium
description: A Romanesque-style Roman Catholic church and Benedictine monastery consecrated in 1117 but with possible origins in the eighth century.

name: San Pietro di Cantoni Italic sanctuary
description: An extra-urban Italic sanctuary located in the territory of ancient Saepinum.

name: San Pietro in Vincoli
description: San Pietro in Vincoli is a Roman Catholic titular church and minor basilica in Rome, Italy. It is located on the Oppian Hill and it houses relics connected with the Catholic Saint Peter. The basilica was founded as the Basilica Eudoxiana ca. 432-440. Pope Sixtus III consecrated the site in 439. The basilica has undergone numerous stages of building and rebuilding, notably under Pope Adrian I and Pope Julius II.

name: San Saba, Rome
description: The Basilica of San Saba is located on the "Piccolo Aventino" in Rome. The basilica's origin is traced to 645 when fugitive monks from Mar Saba in Palestine traveled to Rome to attend the Lateran Council. They subsequently remained on the Aventine.

name: San Salvatore monastery
description: A monastery founded in A.D. 753 by Desiderius, future king of the Lombards. Located in modern Brescia, Italy.

name: San Teodoro al Palatino
description: A church on the Palatine Hill in Rome, sacred to Saint Theodore of Amasea, dating originally to the sixth century AD.

name: San Trifone in Posterula
description: San Trifone in Posterula is a no-longer-extant titular church of Rome. It was located at the corner of Via dei Portoghesi and Via della Scrofa in the Campus Martius. The church likely originates in the eighth century; it appears in documentary records beginning in 1006. The ancient church was destroyed in 1746 when the Basilica of Sant'Agostino was enlarged.

name: San Vitale
description: The basilica of Saints Vitalis, Valeris, Gervase, and Protase, commonly known as the Basilica di San Vitale, is a titular minor basilica church in Rome. The church was built ca. AD 400 by Pope Innocent I.

name: Sanctae Mariae in Palmis
description: A Medieval Christian sanctuary along the Via Appia, perhaps best known as the Church of Domine Quo Vadis.

name: Sanctuary of Monte Sant'Angelo
description: A Catholic sanctuary on Mount Gargano, in northern Apulia, Italy. Listed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 2011.

name: Sanctuary of Poseidon at Tainaron
description: A famous ancient sanctuary of Poseidon located at the southern end of the modern Mani peninsula of the Peloponnese. Literary and archaeological evidence indicate that the sanctuary featured a temple, a cave treated as a mouth of the underworld, and other structural and monumental components. It seems to have served as a sanctuary site for suppliants and a context for the formal manumission of enslaved persons.

name: Sannabadae, Mon.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 66 E2 Sannabadae, Mon.

name: Sant'Ilario
description: A paleochristian site located at the thirtieth mile of the ancient via Labicana, near to the ancient road station 'ad Bivium'.

name: Santa Cecilia in Trastevere
description: A church in Trastevere with origins in the fifth century CE. Pope Urban I likely founded the first church on the same site in the third century CE. Its venerated saint is a Christian martyr of the third century CE. Pope Paschal I rebuilt the church in 822 CE, and moved the relics of St Cecilia to the site from the Catacombs of San Callisto on the Via Appia.

name: Santa Croce in Gerusalemme
description: A minor Catholic basilica in Rome, Santa Croce in Gerusalemme is also a titular church in rione Esquilino and one of the Seven Pilgrim Churches. The church was consecrated ca. 325 to house relics of the Passion of Jesus Christ brought to Rome from the Holy Land by Empress St. Helena.

name: Santa Lucia in Selci
description: The church of Santa Lucia in Selci was founded by the eighth century and perhaps as early as the sixth century.

name: Santa Maria Antiqua
description: A fifth-century A.D. Christian church built in the Forum Romanum at the foot of the Palatine Hill.

name: Santa Maria dell'Orto
description: The church of Santa Maria dell'Orto is a Roman Catholic church located in Rome's Trastevere district. Its foundation is traced to 508.

name: Santa Maria della Libera
description: An eleventh century church near Aquinum built largely from spolia.

name: Santa Maria delle Vigne
description: A Paleochristian church (fifth century CE) located near ancient Lavinium.

name: Santa Maria di Devia
description: The church of Santa Maria di Devia is a Romanesque church of the eleventh century with Byzantine frescoes dating to the twelfth through fourteenth centuries.

name: Santa Maria in Cosmedin
description: A minor basilica church in Rome, Italy. Tradition holds that the church was founded in the sixth century to serve the Greek community at Rome. The beginnings of the present edifice lie in the eighth century.

name: Santa Maria in Domnica
description: The Minor Basilica of St. Mary in Domnica alla Navicella, also known as Santa Maria in Domnica, is a Roman Catholic church in Rome, Italy. The basilica has ancient origins, with likely foundations in the fifth century A.D.

name: Santa Maria in Legarano
description: Site of a large Roman villa, the remains of which were incorporated into the church of Santa Maria in Legarano. The early phases of the villa likely date to the early imperial period. At some point the villa became an Imperial property as it was donated to Pope Sylvester by Constantine.

name: Santa Maria in Monterone
description: Santa Maria in Monterone is a Catholic church located in Rome's Sant'Eustachio neighborhood. The church gives its name to the street on which it is located. The church building is situated on first-century BCE ruins in the area of the artificial basin known as the stagnum Agrippae. The present edifice dates to 1682.

name: Santa Maria in Portuno
description: A vicus and villa of the Roman period that lay along a route connecting the Via Flaminia to the coast. Archaeological evidence for the production of ceramics and tiles has been identified. A Christian monastery is established in 1090.

name: Santa Maria in Tempulo
description: A deconsecrated Christian church on the flank of Rome's Caelian Hill, Santa Maria in Tempulo is connected with the ancient tradition of the spring and grove of Camenae.

name: Santa Maria Maggiore
description: Santa Maria Maggiore is a major papal basilica as well as one of the Seven Pilgrim Churches of Rome. Located on the Mons Cispius, the origins of the church date to the reign of Pope Celestine I (422–432).

name: Santa Prisca
description: A titular church on Rome's Aventine Hill dedicated to Saint Prisca. A Mithraeum dating to the late first century A.D. was discovered on the site in 1934.

name: Santa Sabina
description: A Christian basilica on the Aventine Hill in Rome was founded in the fourth century A.D. The church takes its name from a Christian martyr who was said to have lived on the Aventine. The church is the oldest extant Roman basilica structure that preserves its original plan in the city of Rome.

name: Santa Sofia
description: A Longobard church in Benevento, Italy, listed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 2011.

name: Santa Susanna
description: The current Church of Saint Susanna at the Baths of Diocletian located on the Quirinal Hill in Rome dates from 1585. A titular church has existed on the site since ca. A.D. 280. The remains of an elite house (domus) are preserved beneath the church.

name: Santi Giovanni e Paolo
description: An ancient Christian basilica on the Caelian Hill in Rome that was built in AD 398 by will of a senator known as Pammachius. The basilica was located over the home of two Roman soldiers, John and Paul, who had been martyred under Julian in AD 362. The church was thus called the Titulus Pammachii.

name: Santi Quattro Coronati
description: Santi Quattro Coronati is an early Christian basilica on Rome's Caelian Hill begun by Pope Miltiades in the fourth century A.D.

name: Santo Stefano al Monte Celio
description: The Basilica of Saint Stephen in the Round on the Caelian Hill is an ancient titular church located on the Caelian Hill in Rome. Its earliest phase belongs to the reign of Pope Simplicius (468-483). The early martyr Saint Stephen was honored in the church's consecration. Its circular plan is highly unusual.

name: Santuario dei Tre Santi (Alfio, Filadelfio e Cirino)
description: A church of ca. 1200 AD onwards, containing ancient material from the site of Apollonia in re-use.

name: Santuario di Maria Santissima del Lattani
description: A Marian sanctuary founded in 1430.

name: Santuario di Santa Maria di Barbana
description: The Santuario di Barbana is an ancient Marian shrine that dates ca. 582. It is said that Elia, the Patriarch of Aquileia, built a church near the hut of a hermit from Treviso by the name of Barbanus.

name: Santuario di Santa Maria Regina di Anglona
description: A Marian sanctuary located on the hill of Anglona in the province of Matera, Italy, that originates in the eleventh century.

name: Satıköy
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 64 B4 Satıköy

name: Seuera, Mon.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 67 C4 Seuera, Mon.

name: Shepherds' Field
description: A chapel located in the Palestinian village of Beit Sahour that is believed to be the site of the annunciation of Jesus Christ's birth to the shepherds. A Christian monastery was founded on the site in the early fifth century.

name: Shyrvallos
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 72 A3 Shyrvallos

name: Sidi Said
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 38 C1 no. 11 (Sidi Said)

name: Sigillyah
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 76 D4 Sigillyah

name: Sikinos (settlement)
description: The principal ancient settlement on the Aegean island of the same name. The ancient site is located where the church of Agia Marina now stands on a prominent height, about 5 km southwest of the modern town of Sikinos.

name: Silla de Papa church
description: The remains of a church belonging to the early Medieval period (perhaps the seventh century) that perhaps was a project of Pymenius, Bishop of Asidonia. This edifice is located on the hill that was the site of the oppidum referred to as Silla de Papa.

name: Siniyah Island Monastery
description: A church and possible monastic complex identified in 2021/2022 on modern Siniyah Island in the emirate of Umm al-Quwain, UAE.

name: Siret el-Craat
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 38 D1 no. 26 (Siret el-Craat)

name: Siret el-Giamel/Gasrin di Beida
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 38 C1 no. 9 (Siret el-Giamel/Gasrin di Beida)

name: Siret er-Rcheim
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 38 C1 no. 12 (Siret er-Rcheim)

name: So-called Caesareum of Cyrene
description: A large architectural complex on the east side of the acropolis ridge in Cyrene that was probably built at the beginning of the first century AD and used as a gymnasium, but was later repurposed as forum in Flavian times, when the rooms north of the complex were levelled in order to build a large basilica. Various ruins can still be seen, including the podium of a temple in the central area, possibly dedicated to Dionysos. A fragmentary inscription referring to the "portico of Caesar", found over the inner side of the porch, made the place known as Caesareum.

name: So-called Central Church in Apollonia
description: A three-aisled basilical church, with an external apse, situated near the centre of the ancient settlement of Apollonia (Cyrenaica), about half way between the East and West churches. The building has a narthex, a small western atrium and a range of added rooms along the north flank. The mosaics have been dated around the sixth century AD, but the building seems to have been built in earlier times (possibly fifth century AD).

name: So-called Early Christian Basilica
description: A small basilica located outside the walls of the ancient Greek city of Akragas, at the foot of the so-called Temple of Juno, on the eastern slopes of the Hill of the Temples, in an area previously occupied by the necropolis of the late Roman period.

name: So-called East Church in Apollonia
description: A Christian church located in the ancient city of Apollonia (Cyrenaica) built approximately in the fifth century A.D., probably on earlier Hellenistic foundations. The site was excavated in 1922 and shows a three-aisled nave with transept at the eastern end, and a single apse. Traces of the mosaic floor are still visible.

name: So-called West Church in Apollonia
description: A large architectural complex that includes a three-aisled church with a projecting western apse, a small atrium, a baptistery, and other structures built up against the inner face of the western stretch of the city wall of Apollonia.

name: Son Bou Basilica
description: A three-aisled Paleochristian basilica of the fifth century A.D. located in the village of Son Bou in Menorca, Spain.

name: Sorna Roman Villa and Church
description: Near Parentium (today Porec/Parenzo in Croatia), Cape Sorna (Zorna) first hosted a large villa maritima, and then a small early Christian church.

name: Southeastern Cluster
description: The Southeastern Cluster of rock-cut churches at Lalibela.

name: Speos Artemidos
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 77 D1 Speos Artemidos

name: SS. Marcellino e Pietro
description: The church of Saints Marcellinus and Peter is a Roman Catholic church located on the Via Merulana in Rome. Pope Siricius built the original church in the fourth century A.D., close to the catacombs of Marcellinus and Peter. The relics of the saints were deposited in the church in 1256.

name: St Peters Church, Flawford
description: A ruined Medieval church located on the site of a Roman villa.

name: St-Estève-de-Ménerbes
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 15 E2 St-Estève-de-Ménerbes

name: St. Pierre Cathedral
description: Church in Geneva, Switzerland that was originally a Roman Catholic cathedral but was converted into a Protestant church during the Reformation. The original church was built in the 4th century but the present building began construction around 1150.

name: St. Raphael Church II (Banganarti)
description: Mid-11th to mid-18th century church built on top of an older church that was an important pilgrimage site in Nubia. The church is located in the modern Sudanese village of Banganarti.

name: Stancija Blek
description: The archaeological site of Stancija Blek (Tar-Vabrega, Istria-Croatia) is located on the west coast of Istria. In antiquity, the site would have been located in the territory of Parentium. Archaeological investigation has documented more than 1,500 years of occupation, beginning perhaps with a Roman-period agricultural establishment (villa) that was subsequently followed by Late Antique and Medieval phases that include a so-called tower. Nearby are the remains of the church of Santa Croce.

name: Ste-Cécile Paleochristian Church
description: Remains of this paleochristian church, located at modern Loupian in Hérault, France, were discovered and excavated in the 1980s. The structure lies immediately north of the current church of Ste-Cécile, which dates to the 14th century.

name: Suk el-Oti
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 35 G3 Suk el-Oti

name: Symeon, Mon.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 67 C4 Symeon, Mon.

name: Tabennisis
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 80 A1 Tabennisis

name: Taberna Frigida
description: Taberna Frigida was a road station on the Via Aemilia Scauri at the crossing of the Frigidus river, located between Pisae and Luna.

name: Tagai
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 66 D4 Tagai

name: Tatev
description: A ninth-century monastery located on a large basalt plateau in southeastern Armenia.

name: Tebedut
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 35 E2 Tebedut

name: Temes
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 24 D2 Temes

name: Temple de Saint-Gervais
description: Church in Geneva, Switzerland that has existed in some form since the 4th century CE. The 4th century building was followed by a 10th century Romanesque church which was destroyed by fire in the 14th century. The current church was constructed in the 15th century.

name: Temple of Apollo Lepsios
description: A temple of Apollo on the Aegean island of Lepsia, generally thought to have been located in the vicinity of the modern church of Hagios Nikolaos. It is attested in various ancient sources, including a recently published epigraphic document demarcating the temple lands.

name: Temple of Iuno Moneta
description: A major ancient sanctuary of the Capitoline Hill, the remains of which are likely to be found beneath Santa Maria in Ara Coeli.

name: Thaur
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 19 D2 Thaur

name: Thekoa
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 69 B4 Thekoa

name: Theodosios
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 70 G2 Theodosios

name: Theoktistos
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 70 G2 Theoktistos

name: Theotokos Kosmosoteira
description: A twelfth-century monastic foundation and fortified church in the modern town of Feres in Greece's Evros prefecture. The structure served as a mosque during the Ottoman empire and was returned to use as a church in the 20th century.

name: Thisbe
description: Thisbe was an ancient town of Boeotia located on the southern flank of Mt. Helicon.

name: Tholas
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 76 D4 Tholas

name: Thomas, Mon.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 67 B4 Thomas, Mon.

name: Tigani basilica
description: An Early Christian basilica dating to the fifth and sixth centuries CE.

name: Titkois
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 77 D1 Titkois

name: Torre del Fum
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 25 I4 Torre del Fum

name: Tou Batou
description: A monastery built by order of Emperor Justinian I. St. Catherine's at the foot of Mount Sinai in modern Egypt.

name: Tsenti
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 80 B2 Tsenti

name: Tura d’ Urhay
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 67 H2 Tura d’ Urhay

name: Türkmen Baba
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 62 D3 Türkmen Baba

name: Unnamed monastery
description: A monastery founded in late antiquity on the island then known as Lerina/Planasia (modern Île Saint-Honorat, France; BAtlas 16 D2 unnamed monastery)

name: Basilica at Ras el-Hilal
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 38 D1 no. 21 (Ras el-Hilal)

name: Basilica at Siret Bu Hosc
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 38 D1 no. 28 (Siret Bu Hosc)

name: Basilica (35.20, 7.09)
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 34 F2 unnamed feature ()

name: Basilica (35.94, 7.01)
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 34 F2 unnamed feature ()

name: Basilica near Macomades?
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 34 F2 unnamed feature ()

name: Basilica (35.74, 7.53)
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 34 F2 unnamed feature ()

name: Basilica (35.37, 7.77)
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 34 F2 unnamed feature ()

name: Basilica (35.26, 7.09)
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 34 F2 unnamed feature ()

name: Basilica (35.28, 7.08)
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 34 F2 unnamed feature ()

name: Basilica (35.12, 7.48)
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 34 F2 unnamed feature ()

name: Basilica (35.39, 7.83)
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 34 F2 unnamed feature ()

name: Basilica (35.25, 6.37)
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 34 E2 unnamed feature ()

name: Basilica E of Nova Sparsa?
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 34 E2 unnamed feature ()

name: Basilica W of Henchir Guessès
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 34 E2 unnamed feature ()

name: Basilica (35.62, 6.73)
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 34 E2 unnamed feature ()

name: Basilica (35.70, 6.62)
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 34 E2 unnamed feature ()

name: Basilica (35.45, 6.79)
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 34 E2 unnamed feature ()

name: Basilica W of Claudi
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 34 E2 unnamed feature ()

name: Basilica (35.53, 6.61)
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 34 E2 unnamed feature ()

name: Basilica E of Henchir Guessès
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 34 E2 unnamed feature ()

name: Basilica (34.92, 7.12)
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 34 F3 unnamed feature ()

name: Basilica (35.53, 5.82)
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 34 D2 unnamed feature ()

name: Basilica S of Zabi Iustiniana
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 34 C2 unnamed feature ()

name: Basilica at Gallinaria Ins.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 16 F1 unnamed monastery (on Gallinaria Ins.)

name: Basilica S of Ad Oleastrum?
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 33 F4 unnamed unlabeled ((1 monastery))

name: Vega del Mar
description: A paleochristian basilica.

name: Victoria Church
description: A paleochristian church located at Thugga.

name: Villa gallo-romaine de Barat de Vin
description: Rediscovered in the 19th century and partially excavated in the 1960s, this Roman-era complex east of modern Sorde-l'Abbaye (Landes, Nouvelle-Aquitaine in France) has been variously identified as a villa and a road station. Discoveries at the site included stone walls, a courtyard, a portico, baths, a mosaic floor, and a bordering Roman-era roadway. A medieval church indicated in documents and early maps seems to have used some of the ancient structures. The site appears to be largely overgrown and inaccessible today.

name: Villa Pamphili
description: The church of San Pancrazio was founded in the sixth century A.D. by Pope Symmachus. It stands beyond the Porta San Pancrazio on the Ianiculum. Below the church is a massive catacomb referred to as S. Pancrazio or Ottavilla.

name: Villa S. Silvestro
description: The remains of a Republican temple podium now support a Christian church at Villa S. Silvestro in Umbria. The temple's podium likely dates, in its first phase, to the third century B.C.

name: Visigothic basilica at Segobriga
description: The basilica of Cabeza del Griego at Segobriga dates to the fifth and sixth centuries CE.

name: Wadi Crema
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 35 E2 Wadi Crema

name: Wadi Nagat
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 78 C3 Wadi Nagat

name: Zacchaeus?, Mon.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 68 G2 Zacchaeus?, Mon.

name: Άγιος Νικόλαος
description: A paleochristian basilica dating to the fifth and sixth centuries CE, located near the harbor of Limani Khersonesou, Pediada District, northern Crete.

name: *Aquae
description: *Aquae or pagus Aquensis was a bath site located along the Strei river in the territory of Ulpia Traiana Sarmizegetusa.

name: *Evaunum
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 14 H2 *Evaunum

name: *Thermae Privatae
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 62 D2 *Thermae Privatae

name: Ad Aquas
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 32 B3 Ad Aquas

name: Ad Aquas Caesaris
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 33 A2 Ad Aquas Caesaris

name: Ad Mediam
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 21 E5 Ad Mediam

name: Agamemnoniai Thermai
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 56 E5 Agamemnoniai Thermai

name: Aidepsos (spa)
description: A famous Euboean spa city in the Hellenistic and Roman periods, located at modern Loutra Aidepsou. The polis' original location may have been 3-4 km north in the area of a modern town that carries the modern name Aidepsos (previously Lipso).

name: Ain el Hammam
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 28 C2 Ain el Hammam

name: Amélie-les-Bains
description: Remains of a Gallo-Roman bath complex are now incorporated in a modern spa at Amélie-les-Bains-Palada in the French department of Pyrénées-Orientales.

name: Aponus
description: An ancient thermal site known as Aponi fons or Aquae Patavinae.

name: Aquae
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 20 D1 Aquae

name: Aquae *Arnemetiae
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 8 F1 Aquae *Arnemetiae

name: Aquae Albulae
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 43 C2 Aquae Albulae

name: Aquae Albulae
description: A group of springs located west of Tibur (modern Tivoli). The main spring, known as Lago della Regina, preserves traces of an ancient Roman bath.

name: Aquae Apollinares
description: The Aquae Apollinares were thermal springs located between Careiae and Tarquinii, perhaps to be associated with the Bagni di Stigliano or Bagni di Vicarello.

name: Aquae Apollinares Novae
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 42 C4 Aquae Apollinares Novae

name: Aquae Aptuccensium
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 32 C4 Aquae Aptuccensium

name: Aquae Bormonis
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 18 A3 Aquae Bormonis?

name: Aquae Caeretanae
description: Therapeutic springs located near Caere.

name: Aquae Calidae
description: Aquae Calidae was an ancient spa depicted on the Peutinger Map. Modern scholarship associates it with archaeological remains found in modern Vichy (France).

name: Aquae Calidae
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 22 E6 Aquae Calidae

name: Aquae Calidae
description: An ancient Roman thermal spa in modern Caldes de Malavella

name: Aquae Calidae Neapolitanorum
description: A site of settlement connected to thermal springs, according to Ptolemy.

name: Aquae Carpitanae
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 32 G3 Aquae Carpitanae

name: Aquae Convenarum
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 25 F2 Aquae Convenarum

name: Aquae Dacicae
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 28 C5 Aquae Dacicae

name: Aquae Helveticae
description: Aquae Helveticae was a Roman vicus on the river Limmat.

name: Aquae Herculis
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 34 D2 Aquae Herculis

name: Aquae Iasae
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 20 D3 Aquae Iasae

name: Aquae Onesiorum
description: Aquae Onesiorum (modern Bagnères-de-Luchon, France)

name: Aquae Originae
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 24 C3 Aquae Originae

name: Aquae Passeris
description: Located in Etruria, Aquae Passeris was frequented in antiquity for its thermal springs, as noted by Martial. The site is included on the Tabula Peutingeriana, placing it on the road from Rome to Volsinii. Its location likely corresponds to that of Bacucco, a comune to the north of Viterbo.

name: Aquae Populoniae
description: Remains of an Etrusco-Roman thermal complex, dating from the third century B.C. to late antiquity, were documented between 2000 and 2003.

name: Aquae Rusellanae
description: A thermal site located near Roman Rusellae in regular use and reuse since at least Roman times. Its ancient name is not known. The first modern excavations and mapping efforts date to the 18th century of the modern era.

name: Aquae Segestanae
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 47 B3 Aquae Segestanae

name: Aquae Segetae
description: An ancient settlement at modern Sceaux-du-Gâtinais in France (BAtlas 11 C4 Sceaux-du-Gâtinais)

name: Aquae Siccae
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 25 G2 Aquae Siccae

name: Aquae Sulis
description: An ancient Roman thermal spa and site of the Temple of Sulis Minerva.

name: Aquae Tauri
description: A thermal site in Etruria northeast of Centumcellae.

name: Aquae Terebellicae
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 25 D2 Aquae Terebellicae

name: Aquae Traianae?
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 32 D3 Aquae Traianae?

name: Aquae Vescinae
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 44 E3 Aquae Vescinae

name: Aquae?
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 32 D4 Aquae?

name: Aquas volaternas
description: A place named on the Peutinger map, possibly to be associated with modern Monte Cerboli, Italy.

name: Aque Ange
description: An ancient road station and/or spa mentioned on the Peutinger Map. It's precise location cannot be determined today; however, some scholars have suggested that it be identified with the modern Terme di Caronte at Lamezia Terme in Italy's Cantanzaro province.

name: Arc de Diane
description: The ruins of a Roman bath complex at Divona Cadurcorum.

name: Arieldela
description: A Roman site in modern Jordan, known today as ‘Ayn Gharandal. It includes remains of a late Roman fort and bathhouse.

name: Arriana
description: A Roman-era bathing establishment was identified at modern Arriana in Greece during the 1970s. The facility dates to the second century CE and was destroyed in the third century. Subsequently, the site served as a cemetery through the fourth century.

name: Bacino Termale Euganeo
description: A key element of a well-known area of thermal spas and springs already in use in Roman antiquity, located in modern Italy's Veneto region.

name: Bagni di Nerone
description: The so-called "Baths of Nero" are located near the Porta a Lucca in Pisa.

name: Balesmes-sur-Marne
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 18 C2 Balesmes-sur-Marne

name: balneum Caesaris
description: A bath complex known from the Severan marble plan and perhaps also referred to in the Historia Augusta.

name: Balneum Regis
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 42 C3 Balneum Regis

name: Bar Hill Fort Bathhouse
description: The Bar Hill Fort Bathhouse was built around 140 CE, at the same time as the Antonine wall, and is a long and narrow stone structure with latrines and two heated rooms.

name: Barbarathermen at Trier
description: Dating to the second century, the so-called "Barbara Baths" (Barbarathermen in German) are the largest Roman bath complex known north of the Alps. The bath complex was reused first as a Medieval castle and then as a seventeenth century Jesuit college. The ruins were designated as part of the "Roman Monuments, Cathedral of St. Peter and Church of Our Lady in Trier" UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1986.

name: Batanaia
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 69 B5 Batanaia

name: Bath at northwest foot of the Areopagus
description: A bathhouse located northwest of the Areopagus Hill and southwest of the Agora in Athens, first built in the late second century BC and remaining in use until the late sixth century AD.

name: Bath C3
description: Bath building constructed by Roman soldiers in Block C3.

name: Bath E3 Phase 1
description: Bath building located in the intramuros military sector of Dura-Europos, replaced by Bath E3 Phase 2.

name: Bath E3 Phase 2
description: Bath building located in the intramuros military sector of Dura-Europos, replaces Bath E3 Phase 1.

name: Bath F3
description: Bath building originally occupying all of Block F3, located in the intramuros military sector of Dura-Europos. Excavations reveal that the bath likely had two phases of renovation (one perhaps after 165 CE, and another around c. 200 CE), before it was destroyed by fire. Dura-Europos' amphitheater was built over the remains of Bath F3, and thus the amphitheater's dedicatory inscription, dated to 216 CE, serves as a terminus ante quem for the bath building.

name: Bath M7
description: Bath building constructed by Roman soldiers in Block M7.

name: Bath-Gymnasium Complex at Sardis
description: Bath/gymnasium complex containing a palaestra and a synagogue that was developed in the 3rd century CE.

name: Baths at Cremna
description: Public baths at Cremna.

name: Baths of Antoninus at Carthage
description: The Baths of Antoninus at Carthage date to the middle of the second century and are the largest of the Roman public bath complexes in North Africa.

name: Baths of Caracalla (Albano Laziale)
description: Public baths generally dated to the reign of Caracalla.

name: Baths of Cilurnum
description: The Roman military bathhouse at Cilurnum.

name: Baths of Diocletian (Palmyra)
description: A public bath complex built at Palmyra toward the end of the second century CE and perhaps renovated during the reign of Diocletian.

name: Baths of Pozzo Pantaleo
description: A public bath (thermae) of the Roman Imperial period.

name: Baths of the Philosopher
description: The so-called "Baths of the Philosopher" or "Terme del Filosofo" in region V of the ancient city of Ostia take their modern sobriquet from a bust of Plotinus dating to the middle of the third century CE.

name: Bearsden Roman bath house
description: The remains of a stone-built Roman bath at Bearsden on the Antonine Wall.

name: Beauport Park Archaeological Site
description: The largest known Roman iron-making site in the Weald, with the remains of a substantial bath house.

name: Billingsgate Roman House and Baths
description: A Roman balneum attached either to a Roman townhouse or mansio.

name: Bothwellhaugh
description: The remains of a Roman legionary fort located in North Lanarkshire near Glasgow, Scotland, that was built in connection with the Antonine Wall (ca. A.D. 142-165).

name: Bourbon-l’Archambault
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 14 I2 Bourbon-l’Archambault

name: Bourbonne-les-Bains
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 18 C2 Bourbonne-les-Bains

name: Brestovačka Banja
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 21 E5 Brestovačka Banja

name: Buridava Roman Fort and Vicus
description: A Roman fort and associated structures at modern Stolniceni, Romania. An indigenous settlement was likely located a few kilometers to the north.

name: Buziaş
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 21 D4 Buziaş

name: Byzantine Bath
description: A thirteenth/fourteenth century Byzantine bath at Thessalonica; listed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1988.

name: Caldes de Montbui
description: An ancient Roman thermal spa in modern Caldes de Montbui.

name: Caldoval
description: A Roman settlement with public baths that was occupied from the later republican period until the fifth or sixth century CE.

name: Campo Valdés Roman Baths, Gijón
description: Roman baths dating from the first to fourth centuries.

name: Capitol Baths
description: A bath complex dating to the later second century that were subsequently repurposed as a Christian basilica.

name: Capreae (island)
description: An island of the Bay of Naples off the Sorrentine Peninsula that has, since the time of the Roman Republic, been a popular seaside resort area. The emperor Tiberius maintained twelve villas on the island.

name: Central Baths
description: A bath complex (IX, 4, 5-18) located at the intersection of the Via Stabiana and Via di Nola that was built following the earthquake of AD 62.

name: Central Baths
description: A public bath complex at Herculaneum, dating to the first century A.D. It was subdivided into "male" and "female" sectors.

name: Cortinal de los Baños
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 26 D3 Cortinal de los Baños

name: Duntocher
description: Roman fortlet and fort on the Antonine Wall in West Dunbartonshire, Scotland.

name: Exeter Roman Bathhouse
description: A stone-built bathhouse at Exeter connected with the Neronian fortress.

name: Finca del Secretario
description: An archaeological site that comprises remains of an urban area of the Roman period ranging in date from the first to the fifth centuries. Among the complexes in evidence are a salting factory, a pottery works, and thermal baths.

name: Fontaines Salées
description: Salt springs continuously exploited from the Neolithic period through the early modern era. Also the site of a Roman bath complex.

name: Forum baths
description: A bath complex adjacent to Pompeii's forum that was built after 80 BC (Reg VII, Ins 5, 2, 5, 7, 24). The baths are publicly financed by the duovir Lucius Caesius and the aediles Caius Occius and Lucius Niraemius.

name: Freilichtmuseum Römerbad
description: A Roman bath complex located at Rottweil, Germany.

name: Gallo-Roman bath complex at Mackwiller
description: A Gallo-Roman bath complex located at the modern town of Mackwiller in the French commune of Waldhambach.

name: Gallo-Roman baths
description: A suite of Roman-period public baths in Lyon, France. The site is located in the rue des Farges on the slope of the Fourvière hill. The baths may have been linked to the cult of Apollo.

name: Great City Baths at Salona
description: The so-called Great City Baths at Salona or Great Thermae were built in the second or beginning of third century A.D.

name: Great Southern baths at Conimbriga
description: The Roman baths at Conimbriga.

name: Hadrianic Baths
description: The largest of the public bath complexes at Aphrodisias dating to the early second century CE.

name: Hammam Beni Guecha
description: The so-called Hammam Beni Guecha is a ruined Roman spa located just north and east of the modern Algerian village of Chouara Torche (Beni Guecha municipality). The site may be associated with a road station recorded on the Peutinger Map: Fons Carmerata.

name: Hammath/Ammathous
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 69 C4 Hammath/Ammathous

name: Harbour Thermae, Ephesos
description: A large bath complex adjacent to the ancient harbor of Ephesus that measures ca. 160 by 170 meters and was originally built in the late first century CE.

name: Hellenistic Balaneion, Amathous
description: A circular public bath building dating to the Hellenistic period in the agora of Amathous.

name: Hierapolis/Pamukkale
description: Hierapolis (modern Pamukkale in Turkey's Denizli province) was a major ancient city in Phrygia located at the site of hot springs in southwestern Anatolia. The site was designated as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1988.

name: Hydata Lesitana
description: An ancient thermal site with both nuragic and Roman remains. Now the community of Benettuti on the island of Sardinia.

name: Jewry Wall
description: The so-called "Jewry Wall" is a substantial segment of surviving Roman masonry at Ratae.

name: Kastell Op de Hoge Woerd
description: A Roman auxiliary fort with associated vicus and port at modern Vleuten-De Meern in the Dutch province of Utrecht.

name: Kastellbad Schirenhof
description: Extensive Roman bathhouse associated with the Roman fort at modern Schirenhof in the German municipality of Schwäbisch Gmünd, Baden-Württemberg.

name: Kuršumlijska Banja
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 21 D6 Kuršumlijska Banja

name: Le Caldane
description: Ancient thermal baths in use from the Etrusco-Roman period onward.

name: Leontos Kome
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 62 E4 Leontos Kome

name: Les Rubricaire (St-Gemmes-le-Robert)
description: A Roman fort and tower in the modern French commune of St-Gemmes-le-Robert (Pays de la Loire).

name: Menthon-St-Bernard
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 17 G2 Menthon-St-Bernard

name: Military Bath House at Vindolanda
description: One of two Roman Bath Houses known at Vindolanda.

name: Minor baths of Itálica
description: The Minor baths of Itálica date to the reign of Trajan. Under Hadrian, the bath complex was expanded to an area of some 1,500 m².

name: Montoulieu-Saint-Bernard
description: A Gallo-Roman bath complex last occupied during the fourth century A.D.

name: Nopigeia
description: Remnants of an ancient settlement have been identified south and east of the modern beach village of Nopigeia on Crete that date to at least the 5th century BCE. The church of Agios Georgios Nopigion, which is located between Nopigia and Drapania, was built on the remains of a Roman-era bath complex. A single, late source (Aelian) provides the name Mithymna, which may have belonged to this settlement. Bronze Age remains have also been identified in the vicinity.

name: Prestatyn
description: The modern-day site of a Romano-British settlement dating from first and second centuries A.D.

name: Public Bath of Pella
description: The Public Bath at Pella is a bathing complex constructed and continuously renovated between the 4th and the 1st centuries BCE.

name: Public baths at Saepinum
description: The public baths at Saepinum are located along the decumanus and date to the first and second centuries.

name: Ravenglass Roman Bath House
description: The Ravenglass Roman Bath House, also known as "Walls Castle", is linked to the second-century CE fort at Glannoventa. English Heritage dates the site ca. 130 and deems it one of the tallest surviving Roman ruins in Britain.

name: Ribchester Roman Bath House
description: Roman baths at Ribchester dating ca. 100 CE.

name: Roman bath at Alange
description: A thermal spring where a Roman bath complex was first built in the third century A.D.

name: Roman bath at Fregellae
description: A two-phase complex of public baths at Fregellae dating to the third and second centuries BCE represents an important point in the transition from "Greek" bathing culture to "Roman" bathing culture.

name: Roman bath at Lugdunum Convenarum
description: The Roman bath at Lugdunum Convenarum is part of the second urban nucleus found at the foot of the Fourvière hill.

name: Roman bath at Volaterrae
description: A bath complex located adjacent to the theater of Volaterrae.

name: Roman bath house, Callendar Park, Falkirk
description: The remains of a Roman bath house were discovered in Callendar Park, Falkirk in 1980.

name: Roman Bath House, Castleford
description: The Roman Bath House, Castleford, consists of the now-buried archaeological remains of a Roman military bath house that was built in the mid to late AD 80s. The site was excavated in 1978. The ancient bath was situated within the site of a former defensible annex that was constructed to the north of Castleford Roman fort.

name: Roman Bath House, Prestatyn
description: At the Prestatyn Roman Site, a bath complex was identified via excavation (1934-7; 1981). The bathhouse measures approximately 11.7 by 4.5 meters.

name: Roman Bath-House at Little Chart, Kent
description: The remains of a Roman bath house were discovered in a field to the west of the village of Little Chart, Kent in 1942. These remains included fragments of mosaic pavement. Excavations in 1942 and later in 1947 identified the site as a Roman bathing complex.

name: Roman Baths (Bath)
description: Bathhouse complex in Aquae Sulis, modern-day Bath. This well-preserved site is now a museum and the bathing complex itself is below modern street-level.

name: Roman Baths (Parium)
description: Public baths at Parium used from the second century BCE and renovated several times in the late Roman and Byzantine periods, likely being used until at least the seventh century CE.

name: Roman baths at calle Reyes Huertas
description: The Roman baths at calle Reyes Huertas date to the third century CE. They were located outside of the ancient city center in an area that has been categorized as industrial. Their exact nature is difficult to determine.

name: Roman baths at Fiesole
description: The Roman baths at Fiesole date to the time of Sulla in the first century B.C.

name: Roman baths at Fundi
description: Imperial baths located on Fundi's decumanus maximus.

name: Roman baths at Gaujac
description: The Roman baths at Gaujac were begun ca. A.D. 20.

name: Roman baths at Hydata Hypsitana/Forum Traiani
description: The Roman baths Fordongianus dates to the first through third centuries.

name: Roman baths at Oescus
description: The Roman baths at Oescus are dated to the middle of the third century.

name: Roman baths at Sagalassos
description: The Roman baths at Sagalassos date to the early part of the first century A.D.

name: Roman baths at Sexi/Saxetanum
description: The Roman baths at Sexi/Saxetanum date to the first century.

name: Roman baths at Trier
description: The Imperial Baths at Trier (also referred to as "Kaiserthermen") are a large Roman bath complex in Trier, Germany. The site was designated as part of the "Roman Monuments, Cathedral of St. Peter and Church of Our Lady in Trier", a UNESCO World Heritage Site. The complex dates to the fourth century A.D.

name: Roman baths at Vertillum
description: The remains of baths at the Gallo-Roman site of Vertillum.

name: Roman baths of Acinipo
description: The Roman baths of Acinipo date to the first century B.C.

name: Roman baths of Aquinum
description: Roman baths at Aquinum.

name: Roman Baths of Como
description: The Roman Baths of Como were in use from the later first century until the third century CE.

name: Roman baths of Coriovallum
description: The site of Coriovallum is known for its well-preserved bath complex. These baths were first built ca. A.D. 40 and then re-built during the reign of Hadrian. They remained in use until the fourth century.

name: Roman baths of Ebora
description: The Roman baths of Ebora were in use during the second and third centuries. The archaeological site was discovered in 1987.

name: Roman baths of Taormina
description: The Roman baths of Taormina are built atop a public building of the Hellenistic period. The Roman baths originally fronted on the forum and were expanded in the second century.

name: Roman baths, Carsulae
description: Baths of the early imperial period at Carsulae.

name: Roman baths, Malvindi
description: The remains of Roman baths located in the ager of Mesagne.

name: Roman Thermae (Varna)
description: A monumental bath complex constructed in the 2nd c. CE in modern Varna, ancient Odessus.

name: Romano-British Bath House
description: A bathhouse located at Orpington, Kent was excavated between 1971 and 1975. The site flourished between ca. 270 and 400 CE. Its post-abandonment phase includes Anglo-Saxon graves of the fifth and sixth centuries.

name: Royat Roman Baths
description: Ruins of Roman baths at Royat, France, that were listed as a registered monument in 1889.

name: S. Calogero
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 47 F2 S. Calogero

name: S. Casciano dei Bagni
description: The village of San Casciano dei Bagni, located south of Siena, Italy, has been frequented since at least the first millennium BCE on account of its thermal springs with supposedly healing properties.

name: San Basilio
description: A Roman bath complex that was reused first as a monastic complex and then as the Medieval church of San Basilio.

name: Santa Maria Nova
description: A multi-period site with medieval and modern remains atop Roman levels, including a medieval farmhouse built atop a Roman cistern. Excavations revealed multi-phase evidence, including a bath building decorated with mosaics. The structures may have been related to the nearby Villa dei Quintilli.

name: Sarno Baths
description: The so-called "Sarno Baths" (Regio VIII, Insula 2, nos. 17–21) at Pompeii resulted from the merger and remodeling of two atrium houses, no. 18 and no. 21. There is considerable debate in the scholarly literature as to the architectural phasing.

name: Sasso Pisano
description: A sacred Etruscan thermal site.

name: Sauve Pre-Roman oppidum
description: A pre-Roman and Gallo-Roman oppidum in the territory of the Volcae Arecomici.

name: Segontium: Roman Bath House
description: The Roman Bath House at Segontium.

name: Shadwell Roman Bathhouse
description: A second to fourth century A.D. Romano-British bathhouse that was excavated in 2002. Findspot of RIB 3012.

name: Siminina?
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 32 G3 Siminina?

name: So-called Roman Baths in Apollonia
description: An architectural complex near the sea in Apollonia. It went through different stages and alterations between, roughly, the fourth century B.C. and the seventh century A.D. It was probably used as a private villa, then as public baths and, perhaps, as cisterns in later years.

name: Sokobanja
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 21 D6 Sokobanja

name: Souk el Arba
description: A Roman camp with baths and associated settlement were identified at modern Souk El Arbaa in Morocco in the late 19th and 20th centuries on high ground occupied by a qubba called "Sidi Aïssa". Although ancient structures and settlement extent identified previously are not visible in modern satellite imagery, the site -- bisected by a rail line predating 1941 and occupied by a cemetery and more recent structures -- can still be correlated closely with previously published photographs and plans. Recent scholarly opinion diverges from that reflected in the Barrington Atlas to lean toward identifying this site with ancient (Vo)psiciana (q.v.), attested by Ptolemy, the Antonine Itinerary, and the Ravenna Cosmographer.

name: South-Western Baths
description: A bath complex at Herculaneum that was discovered in the excavations of the so-called "Insula Occidentalis". It was not in use as a bath at the time of the 79 CE eruption as it had been repurposed as a ship-shed.

name: Stabian Baths
description: The Stabian Baths (VII, 1, 8) and (VII,2) are located at the intersection of the Via Stabiana and the Via dell'Abbondanza. They are the oldest baths in Pompeii.

name: Suburban Baths
description: A first century BC bath complex in Pompeii built against the walls near the Porta Marina

name: Suessatium
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 25 C3 Suessatium

name: Termas Menores de Mura
description: A Roman bath complex, located in the northeast part of the modern town of Liria in Spain's Valencia province.

name: Termas romanas
description: One of the 19 Roman bath complexes identified in Mérida.

name: Termas romanas de las Bóvedas
description: The Roman Baths of Las Bóvedas are near the mouth of the Guadalmina River.

name: Termas romanas de Santa María
description: Remains of Roman baths at Antequera in Spain.

name: Termas Romanas do Alto da Cividade
description: A monumental public bath (thermae) at Braga, dating from the first to third centuries CE.

name: Terme Achilliane
description: Thermal baths at Catania dating to the fourth and fifth centuries CE. The archaeological remains are located near the present-day Piazza del Duomo of Catania.

name: Terme Aquae Passeris
description: A Roman bath located at Aquae Passeris.

name: Terme del Nuotatore
description: The so-called "Baths of the Swimmer" or "Terme del Nuotatore" (V,X,3) in region 5 of the ancient city of Ostia takes its modern name from a partially preserved black-and-white mosaic pavement. These baths were built in the late first century CE during the reign of Domitian. The complex was rebuilt during the Antonine period. The complex remained in use until ca. 250. The site was first excavated during 1938-1942. Andrea Carandini directed another campaign of excavation from 1966 to 1975.

name: Terme della Regina
description: Terme della Regina, Tuscania (Italy).

name: Terme della Trinacria
description: The so-called Terme della Trinacria in Regio III of Ostia Antica were built in the Hadrianic period at the same time as the Temple of Serapis (III,XVII,4). The modern toponym is derived from a triskelion included in a mosaic decoration.

name: Terme di Caronte
description: Mineral springs located at modern Lamezia Terme in Italy's Cantanzaro province. There is some archaeological evidence for exploitation of the springs in antiquity, but the current spa complex is completely modern in date. Some scholars have suggested that the Terme di Caronte may correspond to the "Aquae angae" mentioned on the Peutinger Map.

name: Terme di Cavascura
