description: A site of ancient thermal baths.

name: Terme Erculee
description: The largest known bath complex of Roman Mediolanum, built in the late third to early fourth centuries CE.

name: Terme Romane
description: Roman baths of ancient Luceria

name: Terme romane (Regium)
description: The excavations carried out following the earthquake of 1908 revealed a number of baths dating to the early centuries of the Roman Imperial period.

name: Terme Romane con Mosaici
description: These Roman baths in modern Bevagna, Italy, date to the second century CE and were rediscovered in 1891.

name: Terme Romane, Ferento
description: The Roman baths of ancient Ferentium are located adjacent to the Roman theater.

name: Thermae Antoninianae
description: The Thermae Antoninianae, commonly known as the Baths of Caracalla, were built in Rome between AD 212 and 216.

name: Thermae Diocletiani
description: Public baths near the Viminal Hill in Rome constructed under Diocletian from 298-306 CE.

name: Thermae Helenianae
description: The Thermae Helenianae or Thermae Helenae was a thermal complex located in the Horti Spei Veteris, also referred to as the Horti Variani. The baths were situated near the arcus Neroniani adjacent to the Amphitheatrum Castrense. As Helena, mother of Constantine I, restored them following a fire in 323-326, they are named for her.

name: Thermae Novati
description: Thermae Novati, ancient baths of the Vicus Patricius that were located in the vicinity of S. Pudenziana.

name: Thermae of Hippo Regius
description: The northern bath complex of Hippo Regius.

name: Thermae Selinuntinae/Aquae Larodes
description: The site of thermal spings, founded as a sub-colony of Selinus in the seventh century B.C. Thought to be in the area of modern Sciacca.

name: Thermae Titi (Tïtianae)
description: Thermae Titi (Tïtianae), or Baths of Titus, were public baths built at Rome in 81 CE under the emperor Titus. Located at the foot of the Esquiline Hill, the baths occupy part of the Domus Aurea of Nero. The Baths of Trajan were constructed immediately adjacent to the Baths of Titus in the early second century.

name: Thermae Traiani
description: A monumental public bath of ancient Rome that was built between 104 and 109. The architect Apollodorus of Damascus built the baths for Trajan.

name: Thermai Theseos
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 62 A4 Thermai Theseos

name: Thermes de Cluny
description: A Gallo-Roman bath complex of the third century CE at Lutetia (modern Paris).

name: Thermes des Lutteurs
description: A Gallo-Roman bath complex identified in 1991 at Saint-Romain-en-Galt that originally covered some 8 ha.

name: Thermes gallo-romains, Pithiviers-le-Vieil
description: A Gallo-Roman bath complex at Pithiviers-le-Vieil.

name: Thermes romains de Cimiez
description: The ancient Roman thermal baths of Cemenelum.

name: Tomen y Mur Roman bath house
description: A bath complex associated with the first-century fort at Tomen y Mur.

name: Unnamed religious complex
description: Referred to today as "Gisacum", this vast Gallo-Roman religious complex (with major spa), is located largely in the French commune of Le Vieil Evreux.

name: Bath near Henchir-ed-Dalia
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 32 G3 unnamed bath (SW Siminina?)

name: Bath near Bararus
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 33 G2 unnamed unlabeled ((3 monuments, 1 bath))

name: Bath N of Bararus
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 33 G2 unnamed unlabeled ((3 monuments, 1 bath))

name: Bath (36.67, 10.86)
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 32 G3 unnamed unlabeled ((1 bath, 1 cemetery))

name: Bath (36.68, 10.92)
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 32 G3 unnamed unlabeled ((1 bath, 1 cemetery))

name: Bath (36.68, 10.92)
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 32 G3 unnamed unlabeled ((1 bath, 1 cemetery))

name: Usays
description: Vogüé (1868 and 1877) assumed that the large, square fort and bath facilities at the extinct Jebel Seis volcano in Syria were Roman (whence Dussaud 1927 whence BAtlas); however, excavations in the 1960s demonstrated that the fortification and other structures constitute an Umayyad fortified palace and associated settlement built in the late 7th to early 8th centuries CE, incorporating some elements of a pre-existing settlement, fort and/or monastery dating to the 6th century CE. There is no evidence for activity at the site in the early Roman empire.

name: Varignano Vecchio
description: A villa maritima in eastern Liguria, inhabited from ca. 100 BC to the fifth century AD.

name: Vieil-Évreux Roman Baths
description: The Vieil-Évreux Roman Baths of the second and third centuries A.D. have four architectural phases that have been identified.

name: Villa dei Venulei - Terme Romane
description: A first-century A.D. Roman villa and bath complex located in Etruria.

name: Vrnjačka Banja
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 21 C6 Vrnjačka Banja

name: Welwyn
description: The remains of Roman baths are preserved at Welwyn, where a Romano-British roadside settlement was located near a crossing of the river Mimram.

name: (Iulia) Myrtilis
description: An ancient settlement at modern Mértola (Portugal) on the west bank of the Guadiana river (ancient Anas). The town is mentioned in Roman sources, but most of the monumental archaeology dates to the Muslim period or later (including a number of large piers previously misidentified as a Roman bridge).

name: Afrin bridge
description: An ancient bridge on the Afrin river.

name: Bridge at Augusta Vindelicum
description: An ancient bridge over the Licca river, located at Augusta Vindelicum.

name: Euripos
description: The strait between Euboea and Boeotia, located where the modern Greek city of Chalkida now stands. A bridge connected the land masses in antiquity.

name: Healam Bridge Roman Fort
description: The site of a Roman fort and an associated vicus along Dere Street, now buried and bisected by the modern A1.

name: Kalo Pegadi (bridge)
description: On Hiera Hodos at Kopros.

name: Malabadi Bridge
description: The Malabadi Bridge spans the Batman River in southeastern Turkey. Construction began ca. 1146/47 CE and continued until 1154.

name: Petra
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 50 B4 Petra

name: Pons Auruncus
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 44 E3 Pons Auruncus

name: Pons Probi
description: The Pons Probi across the Tiber is attested in the Notitia and, according to Platner and Ashby, was likely built under Emperor Probus in the third century AD.

name: Ponte Calamone
description: A Roman bridge near Narnia that was built over the river Nera ca. 27 B.C.

name: Ponte da Senhora da Azenha
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 26 C1 Ponte da Senhora da Azenha

name: Ponte del Diavolo
description: A bridge in the territory of Blera known as the "Devil's Bridge".

name: Ponte del Toro
description: A Roman arched bridge on the Via Flaminia near modern Terni.

name: Ponte della Rocca, Blera
description: The so-called Ponte della Rocca located on the Via Clodia at Blera is situated adjacent to the Necropolis of San Giovenale and Terrone. Some sources suggest an original date in the third or second centuries BCE.

name: Ponte di Nona
description: A Roman-period bridge along the via Praenestina built of lapis Gabinus.

name: Ponte di Traiano at Tor Tre Ponti
description: A Roman bridge located on the Via Appia at modern Tor Tre Ponti.

name: Puente de la Perdiz
description: A Roman bridge located near the hamelt of Nestar, over the Rio Rubagón.

name: Puente del Chantre
description: A Medieval bridge spanning the Jucar stream.

name: Roman Bridge at Aperlae
description: Remains of a bridge located near Aperlae.

name: Savignano sul Rubicone Roman bridge
description: A Roman bridge on the Rubicon at Savignano sul Rubicone.

name: St-Sylvestre
description: An arched bridge that could be of Roman date, but also possibly Medieval.

name: Tautaendia
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 62 unlocated Tautaendia

name: feature included
description: A feature included on Barrington Atlas map 43 B2 no. 4, an unnamed bridge that crosses the river Arrone.

name: Unnamed bridge (Cessati Spiriti)
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 43 C2 unnamed bridge (Cessati Spiriti)

name: Unnamed bridge group
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 45 A2 unnamed bridge group (between Forum Novum and Aequum Tuticum)

name: Unnamed bridge over the Nar river near Terni
description: An ancient bridge near the *Via Curia.

name: Unnamed Roman bridge near Canusium
description: An ancient Roman bridge over the River Ofanto, near Canusium on the Via Traiana.

name: Bridge at Iria
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 39 E4 unnamed bridge (over Iria fl., at Iria)

name: Bridge N of Aspona
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 63 C2 unnamed bridge (Kesik Köprü)

name: Bridge (36.32, 10.49)
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 32 G4 unnamed bridge (SW Pupput)

name: Bridge near Torre di Palidoro
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 43 A2 unnamed bridge (Ad Turres)

name: Bridge near Campetti - Decumano
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 43 B2 no. 6 (bridge over Cremera fl.)

name: Bridge at Ponte Trajano
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 42 D1-C4 unnamed bridge (on Via Flaminia (20))

name: Bridge at Ponte Caldaro
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 42 D1-C4 unnamed bridge (on Via Flaminia (20))

name: Bridge S of Meydan
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 67 D3 unnamed bridge group (near Cyrrus)

name: Bridge E of Orkistos
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 62 F3 unnamed bridge (between Orkistos and Pessinous, at Çandır)

name: Bridge at Antiochia
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 62 F5 unnamed bridge (three in aqueduct NE Antiochia)

name: Bridge at Paltos
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 68 A3 unnamed bridge (Paltos)

name: Bridge at Saint-Priest-Taurion
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 14 G3 unnamed bridge (St-Priest-Taurion, NE Augustoritum)

name: Bridge W of *Catiriacum
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 14 H4 unnamed bridge (N Uxellodunum)

name: Bridge near Bisutun
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 92 C2 no. 4 (Bisutun)

name: Bridge W of KS126
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 92 D4 no. 9 (Pa-i Pol, near Ivan-i Karkheh)

name: Bridge at Alte Sauerbrücke
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 11 G3 unnamed bridge (Echternach)

name: Ussana Roman bridge
description: A Roman bridge located northwest of modern Ussana in Sardinia.

name: Circus at Antinoopolis
description: A circus was identified at the site in the nineteenth century. John Humphrey dubbed this site "the most impressive circus in Egypt outside Alexandria".

name: Circus at Calagurris
description: The Circus at Calagurris is located to the northeast of the city and could have accommodated 20,000 spectators.

name: Circus at Lepcis Magna
description: The Circus at Lepcis Magna is located to the southeast of the city and measures ca. 100 x 450 m.

name: Circus at Mirobriga
description: The Roman circus at Mirobriga has been dated to the third century.

name: Circus at Segobriga
description: The area of the Roman Circus at Segobriga had been occupied in the earlier Roman period (at least since the Augustan period) by a cremation necropolis.

name: Circus Flaminius
description: Located in the southern Campus Martius, the Circus Flaminius is a large area built by C. Flaminius Nepos in 221 B.C. Various games, including the ludi plebeii, ludi Taurii, ludi saeculares, were celebrated here. The circus was also a place of popular assembly.

name: Circus Gaii et Neronis
description: Circus Gaii et Neronis was originally built as a private racecourse for the emperor Caius. In the fourth century A.D., the north side of the circus was destroyed in order to make room for the first basilica of St. Peter.

name: Circus Maximus
description: An ancient chariot-racing venue at Rome and a major location for public entertainments (ludi), the Circus Maximus is located between the Aventine and Palatine hills.

name: Circus of Col. Tarraco
description: The Roman circus of Tarragona dates from the reign of Domitian. The circus measures ca. 325 meters in length and ca. 100 to 115 meters in width.

name: Circus of Maxentius
description: The Circus of Maxentius is part of a complex of buildings built by emperor Maxentius on the Via Appia between A.D. 306 and 312.

name: Circus Varianus
description: A circus that was part of the Sessorium palace next to the Amphitheatrum Castrense. The obelisk currently situated on the Pincian Hill was discovered on the site in the sixteenth century. It had been moved to the spina of the circus during the reign of Elagabalus.

name: Hippodrome at Ptolemais/Barkes Limen
description: The Hippodrome at Ptolemais/Barkes Limen (Cyrenaica, Libya).

name: Hippodrome of Constantinople
description: The Hippodrome was the great racetrack of the city of Constantinople and was connected to the no longer extant imperial palace. At about 450 m in length, the Hippodrome once accommodated about 100,000 spectators.

name: Jerash Hippodrome
description: The Jerash Hippodrome dates after the middle of the second century. While among the smallest of the attested examples of Roman period hippodromes, it numbers among the best preserved.

name: Roman Circus (Bosra)
description: The Roman circus of Bosra dates to the third century.

name: Roman Circus at Mérida
description: The Roman Circus at Mérida dates ca. 20 B.C.

name: Roman circus of Carthage
description: The circus of Carthage could accommodate ca. 45,000 spectators and may date to the third century A.D.

name: Roman circus of Iruña-Veleia
description: A Roman circus has been identified at the site of Iruña-Veleia.

name: Tyre Hippodrome
description: The Tyre Hippodrome dates to the second century.

name: Villa Maxentii
description: A complex on the Via Appia constructed by Maxentius in the early fourth century A.D.

name: Agora at Kibyra
description: The agora of ancient Kibyra.

name: Agora at Malia
description: The so-called "agora" is a large open courtyard to the northwest of the Middle Minoan palace at Malia.

name: Agora of Athens
description: The marketplace of the ancient city of Athens in Greece.

name: Agora of Corinth
description: The Agora of ancient Corinth.

name: Agora of Dura-Europos
description: A group of ashlar buildings at the city center, identified by excavators as civic buildings.

name: Agora of Magnesia
description: Agora of Magnesia on the Maeander

name: Agora of Palmyra
description: Agora of the Ancient City of Palmyra.

name: Agora of Priene
description: The Agora of the ancient city of Priene.

name: Agora of the Competaliasts or Hermaists
description: Originally a commercial area of ancient Delos, built in the second century BCE on artificial infill adjacent to the city's port. The function of this area seems to have changed after the expansion and consolidation of Roman rule, with residential construction taking over the original commercial activity.

name: Ancient Agora of Argos
description: The Agora of ancient Argos.

name: Ancient forum of Beroe/Augusta Traiana
description: An architectural complex deemed to be the forum of the Roman city of Augusta Traiana.

name: Archaic agora of Thasos
description: The agora of the ancient city. The site was located near the center of the city's shoreline and was surrounded by stoas and public buildings.

name: Civic forum of Praeneste
description: The so-called civil forum of ancient Praeneste served as the center of the ancient city and was used across multiple periods. Its archaeological remains today correspond to the Piazza Regina Margherita and lie beneath the Sant'Agapito martire's cathedral. A large Etrusco-Italic temple of the fourth century BCE was situated here as well as a Roman-period basilica where the famous "Nile mosaic" was located in a flanking, apsidal room.

name: Commercial Market (Tetragonos Agora)
description: The Commercial Market or Tetragonos Agora was one of the main squares of ancient Ephesus.

name: Foro Romano
description: The Roman forum of Aquileia.

name: Forum Aemilianum
description: The Roman forum of Terracina, entered by a quadrifons arch, sits along the line of the Via Appia.

name: Forum Augusti
description: The Forum of Augustus at Rome was dedicated in 2 BCE, to the northeast of Julius Caesar's Forum. It contained the Temple of Mars Ultor.

name: Forum Boarium (at Rome)
description: Rome's so-called "cattle market", the Forum Boarium is a key area of commercial and ritual activity on the east bank of the river Tiber. It is flanked by the Capitoline, Palatine, and Aventine hills.

name: Forum Iulium
description: The Forum Iulium (or Forum of Caesar, Forum Caesaris) is a forum built by Iulius Caesar and dedicated ca. 46 B.C. The main cult of the forum is that of Venus Genetrix.

name: Forum of Carsulae
description: The Forum of Carsulae is supported by terraces and it includes the basilica and a twin sanctuary, in addition to tabernae.

name: Forum of Conimbriga
description: The Roman forum of Conímbriga.

name: Forum of Cosa
description: The forum of Cosa functioned as the administrative center of the town and was the site of key administrative and ritual structures.

name: Forum of Hippo Regius
description: The Forum of Hippo Regius is one of the largest known Roman fora in north Africa.

name: Forum of Iuvanum
description: The Roman forum of Iuvanum.

name: Forum of Jublains
description: The remains of the ancient forum at modern Jublains.

name: Forum of Mactaris
description: One of two fora at Mactaris. This earlier square is adjacent to the Arch of Trajan.

name: Forum of Nerva
description: A forum complex at Rome begun under Domitian but completed under Nerva and dedicated in A.D. 97. Sometimes referred to as the Forum Transitorium, the complex was the next to last of the imperial fora at Rome.

name: Forum of Paestum
description: The forum of Roman Paestum stands north of the Hera sanctuary and measures ca. 57 x 150 meters. It occupies the site of the Greek agora and was surrounded with a peristyle of re-used Doric columns. Tabernae stood at its south side and baths funded by M. Tullius Venneianus stood to the southwest. A triple-podium structure - lararium? - stands on the western side.

name: Forum of Roman Corinth
description: The Roman forum of ancient Corinth.

name: Forum of Saepinum
description: The Forum of Saepinum assumes a trapezoidal shape and is aligned with the decumanus.

name: Forum of Scylletium/Scolacium
description: The Roman Forum of Scylletium/Scolacium.

name: Forum of Septimius Severus
description: The Forum of Septimius Severus at Lepcis Magna

name: Forum of Suasa
description: The Forum of Suasa measures 50 x 100 meters and is surrounded by a portico.

name: Forum of Theodosius
description: The Forum of Theodosius in Constantinople was founded by Constantine as the Forum Tauri. In 393, Theodosius I rebuilt and remodelled the forum to resemble the Forum of Trajan in Rome. The forum occupied the area of what is now Beyazıt Square in Istanbul.

name: Forum of Vulci
description: The forum of the ancient town, dating to the Roman period.

name: Forum Traiani
description: Imperial forum constructed at Rome under Trajan between 105-112 CE.

name: Helorus
description: An ancient settlement of Sicily that was likely a colonial foundation of Syracuse.

name: Methone (Pieria)
description: An ancient settlement in Macedonian Pieria on a hilltop named Koryphi, near modern Nea Agathoupolis in the Pydna-Kalindros municipality in Pieria, Greece. The site was occupied from the Late Neolithic period (ca. 4000 BCE) through 354 BCE, when it was destroyed by the armies of Philip II. It was succeeded by a new settlement approximately 1km to the northwest; known as Macedonian Methone, this later settlement was occupied in the Hellenistic and Roman periods.

name: North Agora
description: The Agora was the main civic square of the town. It was an enclosed space (ca. 202 x 72 m) surrounded by Ionic porticos on all sides. A fragmentary architrave inscription from the double north colonnade records its dedication by C. Julius Zoilos, the leading figure in the town in the 30s and 20s B.C.

name: Old Forum at Lepcis Magna
description: The original Roman forum at Lepcis Magna, in use prior to the construction of the Severan Forum in the early 3rd century CE.

name: Oval Forum (Jerash)
description: The Oval Forum at Jerash dates to the reign of Hadrian.

name: Roman Forum (Mérida)
description: The Roman Forum in Mérida, Spain, dates ca. 25 BCE. It was inscribed on the UNESCO World Heritage List in 1993.

name: Roman Forum at Syracuse
description: The area, roughly situated between the island of Ortygia and the district of Acradina, was used as an agora and, in later years, as the forum of the city of Syracuse. It is now a public park where ancient ruins are still visible.

name: Roman forum at Tergeste
description: The Roman forum of Tergeste.

name: Roman Forum at Thugga
description: The Roman Forum at Thugga is large, covering more than 900 square meters.

name: Roman Forum of Catania
description: The remains of a colonnaded square located near the Cortile di San Pantaleone in Catania are identified as those of the ancient Roman forum.

name: Roman forum of Col. Clunia (Sulpicia)
description: The Roman forum of Col. Clunia (Sulpicia) dates to the Imperial period. It is equipped with a rectangular basilica and a small tribunal in addition to shops. The paved decumanus enters the square through a gate.

name: Roman forum of London
description: A forum was constructed at London toward the end of the first century CE, and expanded between 90-120 CE.

name: Roman forum of Luna
description: The forum of Luna, founded as a colony in 177 B.C.

name: Roman forum of Sarsina
description: The Roman forum of Sarsina has been excavated in its northern reaches.

name: Römisches Forum Waldgirmes
description: The Roman Forum at Lahnau-Waldgirmes is the site of a fortified Roman trading outpost. It is located at the edge of the modern village of Waldgirmes.

name: South Agora
description: The so-called South Agora was the city's secondary public square. The complex dedicated by a local aristocrat Diogenes to the emperor Tiberius.

name: South Square
description: The South Square is the modern name for an enclosed area at the south end of the Athenian Agora, which was built in the mid-second century BC, incorporating some earlier structures. Parts of it were damaged in the Sullan Sack of 86 BC and demolished.

name: The Forum of Corporations
description: A key commercial center occupying the quadriporticus of the theater of Ostia, the Forum of Corporations dates initially to the Augustan period but is substantially rebuilt in the second century.

name: Tolmo de Minateda
description: Tolmo de Minateda was an early Roman hilltop oppidum located in the municipal territory of modern Helin in the Spanish autonomous community of Castilla-La Mancha. It was abandoned in the second century CE and then reoccupied in Late Antiquity (ca. 590 CE) as a Visigothic episcopal see during the Byzantine-Visigothic war. It was finally abandoned in the ninth century, and the population was resettled in Murcia, a new foundation. Tolmo de Minateda has been tentatively identified with the attested placename Ilounon.

name: ‘Stablo’
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 16 B2 ‘Stablo’

name: [Vicus Cruciniacum]
description: A Roman vicus on the Nahe river.

description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 14 G3 *Catalacum

name: Abicada
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 26 B4 Abicada

name: Acquoria
description: A basis villae near Tivoli in opus incertum on the river Aniene measuring 70 x 20 m. Two phases have been identified: second-first century B.C. and first century A.D.

name: Acropolis palace at Jebel Khalid
description: Hellenistic palace at the city of Jebel Khalid on the west bank of the Euphrates.

name: Ad Villam Serviliana
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 31 unlocated Ad Villam Serviliana

name: Adriano
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 43 D2 Adriano

name: Ag. Leontios
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 72 A3 Ag. Leontios

name: Agios Vasileios, Laconia
description: At Agios Vasileios is the archaeological site of the only Mycenaean palace thus far discovered in Lakonia. It is located near the village of Xerokambi in Lakonia, Greece.

name: Agnuli
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 45 D1 Agnuli

name: Aguglia
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 47 F4 Aguglia

description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 32 D3 Ain-el-Djemala

name: Ain-Taleb
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 32 C4 Ain-Taleb

name: Akbou des Beni Chénacha
description: Noted by Gsell (AAA, whence BAtlas), modern Akbou des Beni Chénacha was the site of a large establishment of the Roman period, probably a villa. The location was approximately 2 kilometers to the southwest of colonial Camp-du-Maréchal (now Tadmaït in Algeria's Tizi Ouzou province.

name: Albinia Roman villa
description: The remains of a villa rustica near Orbetello, found to have construction in opus latericium.

name: Alessandria della Rocca
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 47 C3 Alessandria della Rocca

name: Algorós
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 27 E3 Algorós

name: Alia
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 47 D3 Alia

name: Almoinhas Roman Villa
description: A Roman villa located near Loures, dating to the first through fifth centuries A.D.

name: Alresford
description: A Roman winged-corridor villa was discovered in a field adjacent to Alresford Lodge in 1884. Excavations were conducted under H. Laver in 1885.

name: Alto de Martim Vaz
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 24 C3 Alto de Martim Vaz

name: Alviano Scalo
description: Occupation of the Roman villa at Alviano Scalo begins in the first century BCE and continues until the fourth century CE.

name: Amendoal
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 26 C4 Amendoal

name: Ammos tou Kampouri
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 72 E3 Ammos tou Kampouri

name: Ancient Villa at Homs, Libya
description: Ancient name unknown.

description: A Gallo-Roman villa.

description: A monumental hypostyle building at Persepolis dating to the sixth century B.C.

name: Arbin
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 17 G2 Arbin

name: Arbury Road
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 8 H2 Arbury Road

name: Arcinazzo
description: An imperial villa of the Trajanic and Hadrianic periods located on the Altipiani di Arcinazzo.

name: Arnal
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 26 B2 Arnal

name: Arnesp Roman villa, Valentine, Haute-Garonne
description: A luxurious Late Roman villa near the Garonne River.

name: Arrepiado
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 26 B2 Arrepiado

name: Ashill
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 8 H2 Ashill

name: Ashtead Roman villa
description: The site of a Romano-British villa complex that included a bathhouse and tile works, occupied from ca. 67-79 CE until its abandonment ca. 200 CE.

name: Aspiran
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 15 A2 Aspiran

name: Atella
description: The site of a Roman villa in the locality of Magnone (Potenza) in South Italy. The archaeological site was first identified in 1979. Limited excavations identified a storage complex housing dolia. The presence of African Red Slip pottery suggests a date in the fourth century.

name: Athies
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 11 C3 Athies

description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 14 G3 Attanum

name: Banja
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 50 B1 Banja

name: Banon
description: A Gallo-Roman villa explored via excavation in 1956. The site was destroyed by fire. Coins of Gordian III provided a terminus post quem.

name: Baños de la Reina
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 27 F3 Baños de la Reina

name: Barcola
description: A no-longer-visible villa maritima was identified north of Tergeste in 1887. According to AdriAtlas, a reanalysis by Fontana (1993) places the beginnings of the villa's occupational sequence in the third quarter of the first century BCE.

name: Burnt Palace at Kalhu
description: The Burnt Palace, which was destroyed by fire when Kalhu fell to anti-Assyrian Babylonian and Median forces in 612 BC, has the longest building sequence yet identified at Nimrud (biblical Calah). This royal residence, which was constructed in the southeast quarter of the citadel, had at least nine phases of occupation between the Middle Assyrian and Hellenistic Periods. The so-called Burnt Palace was rebuilt/renovated three times during the ninth to seventh centuries BC and it might have temporarily served as a royal residence during the reign of Sargon II (r. 721–705 BC), while that Assyrian king was building his new capital Dūr-Šarrukīn. An immense collection of burnt ivories (the ‘Loftus ivories’) was discovered in and near the throne room in 1954–55; these are now in the British Museum (London).

name: Buxio
description: Buxio is one of several villas mentioned in a list of "donatio ecclesiae" in the "territorio Carducino" in the Life of Saint Didier, who was bishop of Cahors in the 7th century CE. BAtlas places it with a hollow symbol and a question mark on map 25, offering "Le Buz, Vabre?" in the directory for modern location. Modern Vabre is a French commune in Occitania on the Gijou river. CAG reports that the remains of a villa had been reported in the 1940s in Vabre, but without further specificity.

name: Central Building at Kalhu
description: Near the middle of the citadel of Kalhu (biblical Calah, modern Nimrud), the ninth-century-BC Assyrian king Ashurnasirpal II (r. 883–859 BC) constructed a building with a grand, south-facing entrance. This building, constructed east of the southeast corner of the Northwest Palace, might have been one of the temples built by Ashurnasirpal II when he made Kalhu the administrative capital of the Assyrian Empire. Alternatively, the Central Building might have served as a palace.

name: Central Palace at Kalhu
description: The eighth-century-BC Assyrian king Tiglath-pileser III (r. 744–727 BC) built himself a grand, ornately-decorated royal residence at Kalhu (biblical Calah, modern Nimrud). This (large) palatial complex, which included a pillared-portico (a bīt-ḫilāni), was constructed south of the Northwest Palace (built by Ashurnasirpal II [r. 883–859 BC]). The “Cedar Palace” (as it is referred to in at least one contemporary Akkadian inscription) was largely dismantled by the seventh-century-BC ruler Esarhaddon (r. 680–669 BC), who had its (sculpted and inscribed) limestone slabs reused in his own royal residence (the Southwest Palace). The building is now generally referred to as the “Central Palace,” a modern designation given to it by its excavators since the palace was constructed in the central part of the Kalhu citadel.

name: Egalgabarinutukua
description: The palace of the eighth-century-BC Assyrian Sargon II (r. 721–705 BC) at Dūr-Šarrukīn (modern Khorsabad), Egalgabarinutukua ("Palace That Has No Equal"), was constructed between 717 BC and 706 BC. This royal residence was constructed on a high, mud-brick terrace located within the city’s citadel, which was located against the northwestern stretch of the city wall. This large, ornately-decorated palace complex consisted of over two hundred rooms (including chapels to the deities Ea, Sîn, Ningal, Adad, Šamaš, and Ninurta) and courtyards, as well as a ziggurat.

name: Fort Shalmaneser
description: The ninth-century-BC Assyrian king Shalmaneser III (r. 858–824 BC) constructed an impressive palatial complex in the southeastern corner of the city Kalhu (biblical Calah). This armory, which the seventh-century-BC ruler Esarhaddon (r. 680–669 BC) referred to as an ēkal māšarti (‘review palace’), was completed ca. 846 BC. Fort Shalmaneser, the name of building dubbed by its excavators, remained in use until the very end of the Assyrian Empire (612 BC), although its importance was significantly diminished after the Assyrian court moved away from Kalhu at the end of the eighth century BC, first to Dūr-Šarrukīn and then to Nineveh.

name: Forte Tiburtina
description: At Forte Tiburtina the remains of a Roman villa located on the northern edge of the Parco della Serenissima in the eastern suburbium of the city of Rome.

name: Fosso della Crescenza
description: John Ward-Perkins identified the archaeological site at Fosso della Crescenza during the South Etruria Survey. Excavations of a Iulio-Claudian mausoleum were undertaken in 1962. Additional excavation revealed portions of the Via Veientana. Post-excavation work did not occur until the 1980s, by which time Fentress 1983 notes that the archaeological landscape had been largely eradicated by the burgeoning growth of suburban Rome.

name: Gilly-sur-Isère
description: An archaeologically attested "agglomération romaine" (evidently equipped with port and warehouse facilities) at modern Gilly on the Isere river in France (about 4km downriver from Albertville), appears to have been an extension or adjunct of the Roman road station and toll house of Ad Publicanos, thought to have been located in the area.

name: Governor's Palace at Kalhu
description: This beautifully-decorated Assyrian palace is located just north of the Nabû temple Ezida, in the southeast quarter of the citadel of Kalhu (biblical Calah, modern Nimrud). The building likely served as the office and/or residence of several important individuals in the administration of the Assyrian Empire, probably including the governor of Kalhu. Numerous cuneiform tablets were discovered in three rooms north of the central courtyard. Because many of those Akkadian texts relate to the activities of several governors, British excavators in the 1950s dubbed the building the "Governor's Palace."

A 2019 geophysical survey and its publication (Maw 2021) have made the site more sensible and better documented.

name: Khorsabad Palace F
description: The armory (ekal māšarti) of Dūr-Šarrukīn (modern Khorsabad) was located in the lower city, adjacent to the southern stretch of the southwest wall. No inscriptions of the eighth-century-BC Assyrian king Sargon II (r. 721–705 BC) record the construction of "Palace F," although several objects of that ruler were discovered there.

name: Khorsabad Residence J
description: The eighth-century-BC Assyrian king Sargon II (r. 721–705 BC) had several royal residences constructed in the citadel of his new capital, Dūr-Šarrukīn (modern Khorsabad). Residence J, which has constructed on the western side of the citadel, is presumed to have been for one of the highest officials of the Assyrian Empire. Since no inscriptions were discovered in the ruins of the building, the owner/principal occupant of the this Assyrian palace is not known.

name: Khorsabad Residence K
description: The eighth-century-BC Assyrian king Sargon II (r. 721–705 BC) had several royal residences constructed in the citadel of his new capital, Dūr-Šarrukīn (modern Khorsabad). Residence K, which has constructed on the western side of the citadel, is presumed to have been for one of the highest officials of the Assyrian Empire, possibly even Sargon’s heir designate Sennacherib.

name: Khorsabad Residence L
description: The residence of Sîn-aḫu-uṣur, brother of the Assyrian king Sargon II (r. 721–705 BC) and grand vizier of the Assyrian Empire, was constructed in the citadel of Dūr-Šarrukīn (modern Khorsabad), near the royal palace Egalgabarinutukua ("Palace That Has No Equal"). "Residence L" was largest of the four royal residences (J, K, L, and M) built in Dūr-Šarrukīn's citadel.

name: Khorsabad Residence M
description: The eighth-century-BC Assyrian king Sargon II (r. 721–705 BC) had several royal residences constructed in the citadel of his new capital, Dūr-Šarrukīn (modern Khorsabad). Residence M, which has constructed on the western side of the citadel, is presumed to have been for one of the highest officials of the Assyrian Empire. Since no inscriptions were discovered in the ruins of the building, the owner/principal occupant of the this Assyrian palace is not known.

name: Molino San Vincenzo
description: Field surveys, geophysical prospection, as well as targeted research and training excavations, have revealed the remains of a mid- to late- imperial rural settlement with evidence for agricultural activity. However, the earliest finds (e.g., bucchero) date to the sixth/fifth century B.C., indicating the likelihood of Etruscan settlement activities at this site. The entire site seems to have undergone severe recycling and reuse processes in late antiquity and the remaining structures have broken down systematically in modern times. Ongoing intensive agricultural activities affect the few archaeological structures that remain, resulting in a poor state of preservation.

name: North Palace at Babylon
description: The Neo-Babylonian king Nebuchadnezzar II (r. 604–562 BC) constructed a new royal residence for himself at Babylon. This grand palace was constructed north of the so-called “South Palace,” the traditional royal residence of the kings of Babylon. The building, which is referred to as the “new palace” (ēkallu eššu) in Achaemenid-period texts, is generally called the “North Palace,” “Nordburg,” and “New Palace.”

name: North Palace at Deir el-Ballas
description: Royal palace in the Upper Nile in use from the Second Intermediate Palace to the early New Kingdom.

name: Northwest Palace at Kalhu
description: An impressive and ornately-decorated palace constructed at Kalḫu (modern Nimrud, biblical Calah) by the ninth-century-BC Assyrian ruler Ashurnasirpal II (r. 883–859 BC), likely completed between 865 and 869 BC. This multi-winged palatial complex served as the primary residence and chief administrative center of the Assyrian Empire until Sargon II (r. 721–705 BC) moved the imperial capital to Dūr-Šarrukīn in 706 BC. The Northwest Palace, the name given to it by its excavators (since it was built in the northwestern part of Kalḫu’s citadel), remained in use until the very end of the Assyrian Empire (612 BC). The southern half of the building also functioned as the burial place of prominent royal women, including Mullissu-mukannišat-Ninua, the wife of Ashurnasirpal and the mother of Shalmaneser III.

name: Nossa Senhora da Póvoa de Mileu

name: Oplontis

This royal residence, whose walls were covered with white plaster and painted with geometric and floral motifs, was built immediately to the south of the palace of his great-grandfather Ashurnasirpal II (r. 883–859 BC).

description: At Sant'Annèa are the remains of an extensive Roman villa located near the port of Gargano, about 3 km south of Lake Lesina. The villa probably originates in the late republican period and flourishes in the third and fourth centuries CE.

name: Santa Marina (Villa)

name: So-called Villa on the Lake
description: Lugli describes the remains of a sumptuous, porticoed villa in the Doric order facing onto the Alban lake. Marzano clarifies Lugli's earlier summary of the site, which was in turn based on observations made in 1912. The remains of the villa include a 200-m long portico on two levels. About 260 m from the portico are the remains of a cistern in opus reticulatum. Chiarucci documented a structure with five barrel-vaulted rooms of unclear function. Lugli's initial report suggests that this late republican villa was eventually incorporated into the Villa of Domitian.

name: South Palace at Deir el-Ballas
description: Two-story palace near the western bank of the Nile used as an administrative center from the Second Intermediate Period to the early New Kingdom.

name: Southwest Palace at Kalhu
description: The seventh-century-BC Assyrian king Esarhaddon (r. 680–669 BC) started building a royal residence in the southwest corner of the citadel of Kalhu (biblical Calah, modern Nimrud). Sculpted and inscribed limestone slabs from the Central Palace (constructed by Tiglath-pileser III [r. 744–727 BC]) and the Northwest Palace (built by Ashurnasirpal II [r. 883–859 BC]) were used as building materials for this royal residence, which very likely remained unfinished at the time of Esarhaddon’s death in 669 BC. Its excavators called it the “Southwest Palace” since it was located in the southwest corner of the citadel.

name: Spolverino, Alberese
description: An archaeological site of the Roman period connected with the fluvial harbor of Rusellae.

name: Thermae Constantinianae
description: The last of the great imperial baths of the ancient city of Rome, the Thermae Constantinianae were constructed in Regio VI, perhaps replacing the pre-existing Balineum Claudianum. They were located at the southern end of the Quirinal Hill, between the Vicus Longus and the Alta Semita. Substantial remains existed until the sixteenth century and they were sketched by A. Palladio and E. Du Pérac. The completion of the Palazzo Pallavicini-Rospigliosi in 1621 eliminated the majority of the ancient remains.

name: Thistleton Roman Town
description: A small town of the Roman era, with likely Iron Age antecedent and associated farmsteads and field systems, located near and around modern Thistleton, Market Overton, and Kendrew Barracks (the former RAF Cottesmore) in Rutland district, England. This archaeological landscape has been known and explored since at least the 19th century through chance finds, field walking, geophysical prospecting, targeted excavation, and aerial photography.
